finding.title: Permissionless open-relay in BaseCrossChainMessenger.sendMessage enables forged beacon slashing of any operator
finding.description: SETUP: Production wiring (DeployL2Slashing OpStack path; deploy/config/base-mainnet.json l2Slashing.l1Messenger documented as 'the L1 BaseCrossChainMessenger ... NOT the connector') makes the L1 BaseCrossChainMessenger ADAPTER the sole L2 trust anchor: L2SlashingReceiver.receiveMessage in opStackMessengerMode authenticates the L1 origin via xDomainMessageSender()==opStackL1Sender, which is the adapter address. ATTACK: BaseCrossChainMessenger.sendMessage (L54-81) has NO caller restriction (no onlyOwner/allowlist) and forwards an attacker-chosen target+payload via l1Messenger.sendMessage under the adapter own L1 identity. Any address calls sendMessage(BASE_CHAIN_ID, L2SlashingReceiver, forged, gas) with forged = SLASH_MESSAGE_TYPE ++ abi.encode(victim, 10000, factor, freshNonce, anyPod). On L2 the OP messenger delivers it with xDomainMessageSender == BaseCrossChainMessenger so the receiver check passes; the calldata sender is ignored in opStack mode. _handleSlashMessage only checks slashBps!=0 and canSlash(operator), then calls TangleL2Slasher.slashOperator(victim, 10000) -> staking.slash. OUTCOME: any unprivileged attacker destroys up to 100% of any operator restaked stake (attacker fully controls operator and slashBps; uint16 even allows >10000). Confirmed by passing Foundry PoC test/redteam/BridgeForgedSlash.t.sol wired exactly as production. The adapter lends its authenticated identity to arbitrary callers, defeating the xDomainMessageSender defense the repo added for the forgeable-slash issue.

finding.title: ArbitrumCrossChainMessenger.sendMessage is likewise an unrestricted open relay (latent forged-slash)
finding.description: SETUP/ATTACK: Same anti-pattern as F-001. sendMessage (L105-151) has no caller restriction and creates a retryable ticket to an attacker-chosen target with attacker-chosen payload, originating from the messenger own L1 address (its L2 alias becomes msg.sender on L2). OUTCOME: any deployment whose L2 authentication keys on the messenger identity (alias of ArbitrumCrossChainMessenger, or an ArbitrumL2Receiver whose l1Sender is set to this messenger) inherits the F-001 forged-slash. The current shipped Arbitrum path is NOT directly exploitable: the only shipped consumer, L2SlashingReceiver in adapter (non-opStack) mode, authorizes on the calldata sender (which sendMessage sets to the real msg.sender = attacker), so authorizedSenders[chainId][attacker] is false and it reverts. This is reported as defense-in-depth / latent because the messenger is in scope and a plausible wiring (ArbitrumL2Receiver adapter, or an xDomainMessageSender-equivalent) flips it to critical exactly as Base did. Fix belongs in the messenger: restrict sendMessage to an authorized connector allowlist.

finding.title: Unbounded gasLimit silently truncated by uint32 cast in BaseCrossChainMessenger.sendMessage
finding.description: SETUP: _applyGasLimitWithBuffer enforces only a minimum (minGasLimit) and a buffer; there is NO upper bound on the requested gasLimit. sendMessage then does uint32(effectiveGasLimit) (L76). ATTACK/OUTCOME: any effectiveGasLimit >= 2^32 (caller passes gasLimit ~3.9e9; the 0.8.x checked buffer multiply only reverts far higher, ~2^252) is silently truncated mod 2^32, so the L2 minGasLimit handed to the OP messenger can become an arbitrary small value (potentially near-zero). For owner-set config.gasLimit this is a misconfiguration foot-gun causing under-gassed L2 delivery; OP-stack messages are replayable so it is recoverable rather than fund-loss, hence low. The inline comment 'effectiveGasLimit fits into uint32 because we enforce reasonable limits' is inaccurate -- no such enforcement exists. Fix: require(effectiveGasLimit <= type(uint32).max) or clamp.

finding.title: Arbitrum retryable excess-fee/call-value refunds locked at L2 alias under default config
finding.description: SETUP: l2RefundAddress defaults to address(0). In sendMessage refundTo = l2RefundAddress != 0 ? l2RefundAddress : msg.sender (the L1 connector). ATTACK/OUTCOME: with the default, both excessFeeRefundAddress and callValueRefundAddress are set to the L1 connector, so Arbitrum mints any excess submission/gas refund at the L2 ALIAS of the L1 connector -- an address with no receive/withdraw logic -- permanently locking the excess ETH for every relayed message. This is acknowledged in the contract NatSpec (L68-77) but still ships as the default, so any operator that does not call setL2RefundAddress before mainnet leaks refunds on ongoing slash-relay traffic. Severity low (only the excess over actual fee is lost). Fix: default to a recoverable sweep / require it be set before sendMessage succeeds.

finding.title: Queued withdrawal + delegation double-count restaked ETH -> phantom operator stake
finding.description: SETUP: A staker restakes beacon principal (recordBeaconChainDeposit) minting beacon-pool shares; the pod physically holds the ETH. ATTACK: (1) queueWithdrawal(allShares) is allowed because it only reverts when delegatorTotalDelegated>0, which is 0 pre-delegation. (2) delegateTo(op, amount) computes availableAssets = _convertToAssets(_pools[s],_shares[s]) — the FULL beacon valuation — and NEVER subtracts queuedShares[s] (line 514), so the same shares that are already queued for withdrawal are delegated again. (3) completeWithdrawal (line 734) burns the beacon shares and pays out real ETH via ValidatorPod.withdrawToStaker; it NEVER checks delegatorTotalDelegated. OUTCOME: the staker recovers 100% of principal while a live, fully-backed-looking delegation persists. operatorDelegatedStake(op) and getDelegation(s,op) still report the full amount with ZERO underlying assets. getOperatorStake/getOperatorStakeForAsset (used by TangleL2Slasher.getSlashableStake and by service security commitments) are inflated by unbacked phantom stake; slashing it claws back nothing. An operator can collude with a staker to advertise arbitrary slashable stake at zero real risk, defeating the restaking economic-security guarantee. The view getAvailableToWithdraw correctly subtracts BOTH queuedShares and delegated shares, but neither state-changing path (delegateTo / completeWithdrawal) enforces that mutual exclusion. PoC test/beacon/PoCDoubleCount.t.sol passes: staker withdraws 32 ETH, pod balance 0, beacon shares 0, yet operatorDelegatedStake==32 ETH.

finding.title: Service slashing of delegated stake is non-punitive in ValidatorPodManager (delegator recovers 100%)
finding.description: SETUP: src/core/Slashing.sol routes operator misbehavior to IStaking.slashForService/slashForBlueprint/slash. In ValidatorPodManager these all call _slash(operator,slashBps). ATTACK/OUTCOME: _slash decrements ONLY operatorStake[operator] (self-stake) and _operatorDelegationPools[operator].totalAssets (a pure accounting figure). It never touches the delegator beacon pool (_pools/_shares) nor the ValidatorPod ETH custody. A delegators committed collateral IS their beacon principal, which physically sits in the pod and is withdrawn via the independent beacon withdrawal queue (queueWithdrawal/completeWithdrawal -> ValidatorPod.withdrawToStaker). completeUndelegation only mutates accounting and moves NO ETH. Therefore after even a 100% service slash of the delegation pool, a delegator undelegates (accounting only) and withdraws their FULL beacon principal from the pod. Only operator self-stake is ever really clawed back. Delegated stake — the core of the restaking security promise and the value reported by getOperatorStake/getOperatorDelegatedStake used for service security commitments — provides ZERO real slashable collateral. A service relying on an operators delegated stake for security can be cheated: operator misbehaves, gets slashed on paper, delegators lose nothing. PoC test/beacon/PoCSlashNoop.t.sol: 50% slash applied, delegator still recovers 32/32 ETH.

finding.title: [ACCESS-CONTROL] Permissionless sendMessage in L1 messengers → forged BEACON_SLASH under opStack mode
finding.description:
SETUP: The slash path is L2SlashingConnector → ICrossChainMessenger.sendMessage → native L1 bridge → L2SlashingReceiver.receiveMessage. For Base/OP-Stack, L2SlashingReceiver runs in opStackMessengerMode: it DISCARDS the calldata `sender` and authenticates by `IOpStackCrossDomainMessenger(messenger).xDomainMessageSender() == opStackL1Sender[srcChain]` (L2SlashingReceiver.sol:304-314). Because the connector reaches the native L1 messenger only THROUGH the adapter, the L1 contract that calls `l1Messenger.sendMessage` is BaseCrossChainMessenger itself, so `xDomainMessageSender()` on L2 always returns the adapter address. Therefore opStackL1Sender MUST be configured to the adapter for the legit flow to authenticate.

ATTACK: BaseCrossChainMessenger.sendMessage (line 54) has NO caller authorization — only a destinationChainId check (line 64). Any EOA/contract calls `BaseCrossChainMessenger.sendMessage(8453, L2SlashingReceiver, forgedPayload, gas)`. Line 72-77 forwards to the native messenger under the ADAPTER's own identity; the attacker's address is placed only in the calldata `sender` arg (line 74), which opStack mode ignores. On L2: msg.sender == native messenger (passes onlyMessenger), xDomainMessageSender() == BaseCrossChainMessenger == opStackL1Sender (passes). forgedPayload = SLASH_MESSAGE_TYPE ‖ abi.encode(operator, slashBps, factor, nonce, pod) — every field is attacker-chosen and on-chain-derivable (see L2SlashingConnector.sol:266-268).

OUTCOME: Attacker forges an arbitrary BEACON_SLASH against ANY operator with any slashBps/nonce, bypassing beacon-chain verification. The adapter is a permissionless open relay / confused deputy: it lends its trusted xDomainMessageSender identity to attacker payloads. Fix: gate sendMessage to an authorized caller (the connector), or have the receiver authenticate the true origin rather than the relaying adapter. ArbitrumCrossChainMessenger.sendMessage (lines 105-151) is identically unauthenticated.

finding.title: [BUSINESS-LOGIC] L1/L2 adapter selector mismatch — receiver-side replay/sender protection is dead code on the slash path
finding.description:
SETUP: Each bridge ships two halves — an L1 messenger (BaseCrossChainMessenger / ArbitrumCrossChainMessenger) and an L2 receiver adapter (BaseL2Receiver / ArbitrumL2Receiver) that implements the hardened path: explicit L1-origin check (BaseL2Receiver.sol:230-231 xDomainMessageSender==l1Sender; ArbitrumL2Receiver.sol:339 msg.sender==applyL1ToL2Alias(l1Sender)) plus per-payload replay dedup (BaseL2Receiver.sol:234-242, ArbitrumL2Receiver.sol:342-350).

ATTACK/OBSERVATION: The L1 messengers encode the L2 call as `abi.encodeCall(ICrossChainReceiver.receiveMessage, ...)` (BaseCrossChainMessenger.sol:74, ArbitrumCrossChainMessenger.sol:125) — selector 0x1885c2f9. But the L2 receiver adapters expose only `relayMessage(bytes)` — selector 0xd58c736a — and have NO receiveMessage function. So an L1-adapter→L2-receiver-adapter pairing is non-functional: every delivery would call the missing receiveMessage and revert. The only selector-consistent topology is L1 adapter → L2SlashingReceiver.receiveMessage DIRECTLY (i.e. opStack/aliased-messenger mode).

OUTCOME: The L2 receiver adapters' sender-authentication and replay-dedup never sit on the live slash path — they are effectively dead code. All replay protection collapses onto L2SlashingReceiver's nonce map, and (combined with the permissionless sendMessage, see related critical) origin authentication collapses onto xDomainMessageSender, which only proves the adapter relayed the message, not that the connector originated it. The two adapter halves cannot be wired together as designed; a deployer following the apparent design (point native messenger at the L2 receiver adapter) bricks the slash channel.

finding.title: [BUSINESS-LOGIC] Per-pod beacon slashBps applied to operator's ENTIRE L2 stake → unbounded over-slash
finding.description:
SETUP: `beaconChainSlashingFactor()` is a PER-POD fraction (1e18 = 100%) of that one pod's beacon-validator balance remaining. The connector derives `slashPercentage = (lastFactor - newFactor)*1e18/lastFactor` and `slashBps` from a SINGLE pod (L2SlashingConnector.sol:242-243), then ships `slashBps` to L2. On L2, TangleL2Slasher.slashOperator → staking.slash(operator, ...) → MultiAssetDelegation._slash slashes `(operatorSelfStake + operatorDelegationPool.totalAssets) * slashBps / 10000` — the operator's ENTIRE native stake including all third-party delegators (ValidatorPodManager.sol:943-971 shows identical semantics).

ATTACK / OUTCOME (base mismatch): slashBps expresses "fraction of THIS POD's beacon balance lost", but it is applied to the operator's total L2 delegated stake, an unrelated and generally far larger base. Pod loses 1 ETH of 32 ETH beacon balance → factor 1e18→~0.969e18 → slashBps≈312 (3.1%). If the operator carries 1000 ETH of L2 delegations, 3.1% = ~31 ETH is slashed for a 1 ETH beacon loss — a ~31x over-slash borne by delegators who never backed that validator. Over-slash multiplier = (L2 total stake)/(pod beacon principal), unbounded.

ATTACK / OUTCOME (multi-pod amplification): `podOperator` (L2SlashingConnector.sol:118,396) maps MANY pods → one operator, and `lastProcessedSlashingFactorByChain` is keyed per-pod (line 107). Pod A factor 1e18→0.5e18 slashes 50% of the operator's whole stake; pod B independently 1e18→0.5e18 then slashes 50% of the REMAINING whole stake → 75% total, though each pod lost only 50% of its own balance. The shared L2 stake base is slashed once per pod.

The connector even computes `l2SlashAmount = operatorStake * slashPercentage / 1e18` (line 258-259) confirming the percentage is taken against total operator stake, not the slashed pod's principal.

finding.title: [BUSINESS-LOGIC] Sub-bps factor decrease consumed on L1 but permanently rejected on L2 (slashBps==0) → lost slash + corrupted baseline
finding.description:
SETUP: `_propagateBeaconSlashing` only gates on `newSlashingFactor < lastFactor` (L2SlashingConnector.sol:237) and never checks that the resulting `slashBps != 0`. `slashBps = uint16((slashPercentage * 10000) / 1e18)` (line 243) truncates to 0 whenever the factor decrease is below 0.01% (slashPercentage < 1e14, i.e. a beacon-balance loss delta under 1e14 relative to a 1e18 factor — realistic for inactivity-leak / correlated-penalty drips that accrue in tiny increments).

ATTACK / OUTCOME: For such a sub-bps decrease the connector (a) ADVANCES its baseline `lastProcessedSlashingFactorByChain[pod][dest] = newSlashingFactor` (line 262), (b) increments the nonce and pays the bridge fee, and (c) sends a payload with slashBps==0. On L2, `_handleSlashMessage` hard-reverts on `slashBps == 0` with InvalidPayload (L2SlashingReceiver.sol:369-371). That revert is permanent — the payload's slashBps can never change — so the message can never be redelivered successfully. Meanwhile the connector has already moved its baseline DOWN, so the consumed delta is never re-propagated (future propagations compute relative to the new lower lastFactor). Net: the slash for that delta is permanently lost (protocol under-slashes), the bridge fee is burned, and the receiver comment's own premise ("the L1 connector never wastes a bridge fee on a no-op", L2SlashingReceiver.sol:346-347) is violated. An operator can also deliberately trickle many sub-bps factor decreases to bleed off slashable deltas into the unrecoverable 0-bps gap.

FIX: revert in the connector when `slashBps == 0` BEFORE mutating `lastProcessedSlashingFactorByChain`, or round up / accumulate sub-bps remainder into the next propagation.

finding.title: [BUSINESS-LOGIC] Operator evades beacon slash permanently by zeroing L2 slashable stake (canSlash→false hard-reverts forever)
finding.description:
SETUP: The receiver's "retry-safe" CEI design (L2SlashingReceiver.sol:340-382) reverts the whole tx — leaving the nonce open for bridge redelivery — whenever `!slasher.canSlash(operator)` (line 372). TangleL2Slasher.canSlash returns false when `getSlashableStake(operator) == 0`, i.e. the operator's L2 native stake is zero (TangleL2Slasher.sol:143-152), or when slashing is paused.

ATTACK / OUTCOME: This converts a transient guard into a permanent evasion path. An operator observing a pending/likely beacon slash withdraws or undelegates their L2 native stake so `getSlashableStake == 0`. Every delivery of the slash message then reverts with SlashingNotPossible and the bridge keeps retrying forever, but the slash is NEVER applied while stake is 0. The operator can keep stake elsewhere / on other assets, or simply never re-stake the native asset on this destination, and the beacon penalty is fully escaped on L2 — there is no queue, no deferred debt, and `beaconSlashTotal`/`totalBeaconSlashed` are never charged. The design comment frames the revert as protecting against "operator just unregistered" (line 343-345) but provides no mechanism to ever collect the slash once stake returns; redelivery only succeeds if the operator voluntarily re-funds slashable stake AND a relayer happens to retry at that instant. Combined with the unbounded/retried bridge message, this is also a griefing vector (perpetual failed relays). 

Note this is independent of F-002: here the L2 slasher targets MultiAssetDelegation (TangleL2Slasher.staking), not ValidatorPodManager; the issue is the canSlash==0 → permanent-revert escape, not pool non-punitiveness.

finding.title: ValidatorPod.withdrawNonBeaconChainEth drains credited/delegated beacon principal, bypassing delay, share-burn and delegation lock
finding.description: SETUP: After a validator exits (or partial withdrawals), its principal lands in the ValidatorPod as ETH and is credited to the owner as beacon-pool shares via a checkpoint rebase; the owner may also have delegated that principal as slashable collateral. ATTACK: withdrawNonBeaconChainEth(recipient, amount) is gated only by onlyPodOwner and amount <= address(this).balance (lines 490-491). It does NOT distinguish beacon principal from stray tips, does NOT decrement the beacon pool totalAssets / owner shares, does NOT decrement withdrawableRestakedExecutionLayerGwei, and is NOT subject to withdrawalDelayBlocks or the delegatorTotalDelegated lock. OUTCOME: the owner instantly extracts the full pod balance — including exited beacon principal already counted as restaked, delegated stake — with no queue, no delay, and no share burn. The operator continues to advertise the now-unbacked delegated stake (phantom collateral, cf F-001), and the staker escapes the entire slashing/withdrawal-delay window. The function intended only for tip/accident recovery doubles as an unconditional principal escape hatch. PoC test/beacon/PoCNonBeaconDrain.t.sol passes: 32 ETH delegated, pod drained instantly via withdrawNonBeaconChainEth, beacon shares + delegation both still 32 ETH.

finding.title: REPRODUCED: [ACCESS-CONTROL] Permissionless sendMessage → forged BEACON_SLASH under opStack mode
finding.description:
REPRODUCED: Confirmed end-to-end with a Foundry PoC on the exact codebase.

SETUP: Real BaseCrossChainMessenger adapter deployed pointing at a mock native messenger. L2SlashingReceiver deployed behind ERC1967Proxy, initialized, setMessenger(mock), setOpStackMessengerMode(true), and opStackL1Sender[srcChain] timelock-activated to the ADAPTER address (the only value xDomainMessageSender can resolve to on the legit flow — confirmed by receiver.sol:308-314). Mock IL2Slasher records slashOperator.

ATTACK: BaseCrossChainMessenger.sendMessage (line 54) has NO caller auth — only the destinationChainId check (line 64). A random attacker EOA (vm.prank(attacker)) calls adapter.sendMessage(BASE_CHAIN_ID, receiver, forgedPayload, gas). The adapter forwards to the native messenger under its OWN identity (line 72-77), placing the attacker only in the ignored calldata `sender`. On L2: msg.sender==messenger (passes onlyMessenger), xDomainMessageSender()==adapter==opStackL1Sender (passes). forgedPayload = SLASH_MESSAGE_TYPE ‖ abi.encode(victimOperator, 9999, factor, nonce, pod) — all attacker-chosen.

OUTCOME: slasher.slashCount()==1, lastOperator==victimOperator, lastSlashBps==9999, beaconSlashTotal[victim]==9999. An attacker-chosen 99.99% slash applied to an arbitrary operator with ZERO beacon proof. A second test reproduces the literal forged-delivery path (mock.relayCall(adapter, receiver, receiveMessage(srcChain, attacker, payload))) with identical result.

Wiring note: matches the finding exactly. The adapter forwards `receiveMessage(block.chainid, msg.sender, payload)`, so the receiver's sourceChainId is the L1 chainid; opStackL1Sender is keyed/activated under that chainid. No deviation from the finding.

reproduction.pocStdout:

finding.title: EIP4788Oracle.latestBeaconTimestamp() returns a non-existent slot key; getBeaconBlockRoot(latest) always reverts on mainnet
finding.description: latestBeaconTimestamp() aligns to a 12s boundary via 't - (t % 12)', yielding a value congruent to 0 (mod 12). Ethereum beacon/execution slot timestamps are 'genesis + 12*slot' with mainnet genesis 1606824023 which is 11 (mod 12), so every real EIP-4788 ring-buffer key is 11 (mod 12). The returned timestamp therefore NEVER matches a stored key. SETUP: deploy EIP4788Oracle on L1 mainnet (its documented target). ATTACK/OUTCOME: any integrator following the IBeaconOracle ABI that discovers the latest root via getBeaconBlockRoot(oracle.latestBeaconTimestamp()) reverts 100% of the time (the precompile reverts on key mismatch). The alignment also ignores the genesis offset entirely (a correct form is 't - ((t-GENESIS) % 12)'), and even then block.timestamp itself is already a valid key, so the method's approach is wrong. No on-chain consumer in this repo calls it today (ValidatorPod uses block.timestamp / caller-supplied timestamps), bounding impact to low; it is a fully broken public oracle method on the primary L1 deployment and would be medium for any external integrator relying on it. Confirmed with a passing Foundry PoC: test/beacon/L1OracleAuditPoC.t.sol::test_latestBeaconTimestamp_isNeverAValidKey.

finding.title: hasBeaconBlockRoot/hasBeaconRoot check only staticcall success (not returndata length); false-positive true when EIP-4788 precompile is absent
finding.description: hasBeaconBlockRoot() (EIP4788Oracle.sol:23-26) and the analogous hasBeaconRoot() (BeaconRootRelayer.sol:115-118) return 'success' from a staticcall to BEACON_ROOTS_ADDRESS without checking returndata length, unlike getBeaconBlockRoot()/_getBeaconRoot() which require data.length==32. A staticcall to an account with NO code returns success=true with empty returndata. On the intended mainnet/testnet deployment the EIP-4788 precompile exists and reverts on missing/mismatched timestamps, so success correctly reflects existence. But on any chain where 0x000F3df6...Beac02 has no code (wrong-chain deployment, fork/devnet without EIP-4788), hasBeaconBlockRoot() returns true for EVERY timestamp while getBeaconBlockRoot() reverts for all of them -- an inconsistent has/get contract that can mislead callers into treating non-existent roots as present. OUTCOME: existence check is unsound off the intended chain. Fix: also require returndata length == 32 (mirror getBeaconBlockRoot).

finding.title: [config] DISPUTE_WINDOW_ROUNDS dead constant contradicts enforced 7-day slash window
finding.description: SETUP: ProtocolConfig.DISPUTE_WINDOW_ROUNDS = 14 is documented as 'Dispute window for slashing (14 rounds = 3.5 days)'. ATTACK/OBSERVATION: This constant is never read anywhere in src/ (0 live references). The slashing subsystem actually derives its dispute window from SlashingLib.DEFAULT_DISPUTE_WINDOW = 7 days (src/libraries/SlashingLib.sol:17), seeded into _slashState.config.disputeWindow and used by SlashingLib.proposeSlash as executeAfter = block.timestamp + disputeWindow (SlashingLib.sol:262) and resolved in Slashing._resolveDisputeWindow (src/core/Slashing.sol:130). OUTCOME: ProtocolConfig presents a single source of truth for network defaults but the slashing dispute timeline it advertises (3.5 days) is half the value actually enforced (7 days) and lives in different units (rounds vs seconds). An operator/integrator/governance actor reading ProtocolConfig to reason about how long they have to dispute, or editing this constant to retune the window, would be wrong and have no effect. Also a latent units trap: if a future change ever wires this rounds value (14) into the seconds-based config it would collapse the window to 14s — only the MIN_DISPUTE_WINDOW=1h clamp in SlashingLib.updateConfig would catch it.

finding.title: [config] REWARD_GRACE_PERIOD_ROUNDS documents an anti-JIT reward delay that is never enforced
finding.description: SETUP: ProtocolConfig.REWARD_GRACE_PERIOD_ROUNDS = 4 is documented as 'Grace period before new stake earns rewards (4 rounds = 24 hours)'. OBSERVATION: the constant has 0 live references across src/; no reward path (src/staking/RewardsManager.sol, src/core/Payments*.sol) consults a deposit/join round or applies any maturity gate before stake accrues rewards. OUTCOME: the comment implies a just-in-time deposit protection (new stake cannot immediately farm a reward distribution) that does not exist. In this codebase reward accounting is debt/share based (MasterChef-style accrual via _onDelegationChanged), so the absence is currently not exploitable for back-running an accrued pool; however the constant is a false assurance. If any future reward mode adds an epoch-snapshot or lump-sum distribution and developers assume this grace period is in force, JIT deposit-then-claim-then-withdraw reward capture becomes possible. Flagging so the phantom protection is not relied upon.

finding.title: [config] ROUNDS_PER_EPOCH dead constant (no enforced epoch boundary)
finding.description: SETUP: ProtocolConfig.ROUNDS_PER_EPOCH = 28 ('28 rounds = 7 days') is documented as a network-wide default. OBSERVATION: 0 live references in src/. The protocol's time logic is expressed purely in rounds (currentRound advanced in SlashingManager._advanceRound, delays in DelegationStorage are round counts); there is no epoch concept enforced on-chain. OUTCOME: harmless today but the constant invites the assumption that epoch-aligned behavior exists. Delay constants (DELEGATOR_DELAY_ROUNDS=28, OPERATOR_DELAY_ROUNDS=56) are described relative to 'epochs' yet only the raw round counts are used, so the epoch framing is documentation-only. Remove or wire up to avoid drift.

finding.title: [config] DEFAULT_MAX_OPERATORS_PER_SERVICE=256 sets high default for nested per-operator distribution loops
finding.description: SETUP: DEFAULT_MAX_OPERATORS_PER_SERVICE = 256 seeds _maxOperatorsPerService (Base.sol:189), the ceiling on operators per service. Its own NatSpec notes it 'Bounds the per-operator loops in the billing / distribute / terminate paths'. OBSERVATION: those paths iterate over the full operator set (PaymentsDistribution._payOperatorPoolByWeight loops n=operators.length at src/core/PaymentsDistribution.sol:325; PaymentsBilling has nested operator x m loops at 217/228 and 272/319). MITIGATIONS PRESENT: operator payouts use pull accrual (addPendingReward to a mapping) and the only per-operator external call (_forwardStakerShare -> distributeServiceFee) is wrapped in try/catch with escrow-refund fallback, so a single malicious operator cannot brick the loop, and ~256 iterations remain under the block gas limit. OUTCOME: not a clean DoS, but 256 is an aggressive default for unbounded-per-iteration accounting; under worst-case multi-asset splits (inner m loop) gas can climb materially and the keeper/biller bears it. Recommend a more conservative default and/or explicit gas-bounded batching. Defense-in-depth.

finding.title: [accounting] Commitment exposure double-applied in slash path → operators massively under-slashed
finding.description:
SETUP: A service has security requirements (`_serviceSecurityRequirements[serviceId].length > 0`) and the offending operator joined with explicit per-asset commitments (`_serviceSecurityCommitments[serviceId][operator]` non-empty) — the normal configuration for any service that enforces security.

ATTACK / ROOT CAUSE: Exposure is folded into the slash fraction TWICE (effectively three times) along the propose→execute path.
1. In `proposeSlash` (Slashing.sol:73-77) the proposal's effective rate is scaled by both the operator's service exposure and a weighted average of the per-asset commitments:
   `effectiveExposureBps = opData.exposureBps * commitmentBps / 10000`
   where `commitmentBps = _computeServiceCommitmentExposureBps(...)` is the weighted average of `_serviceSecurityCommitmentBps[serviceId][operator][assetHash]` (line 169/193) — i.e. the same per-asset `commitment.exposureBps` values.
   Then `SlashingLib.proposeSlash` computes `proposal.effectiveSlashBps = slashBps * effectiveExposureBps / 10000` (SlashingLib.sol:198,247).
2. In `executeSlash` → `_executeSlashOnStaking` (Slashing.sol:509-516) the proposal's already-exposure-scaled `effectiveSlashBps` is passed into `_staking.slashForService(..., proposal.effectiveSlashBps, ...)`.
3. `_slashForService` (SlashingManager.sol:270) re-applies the per-asset commitment exposure AGAIN: `effectiveBps = effectiveSlashBps * commitment.exposureBps / 10000`.

Net per-asset slash fraction = `slashBps * opData.exposureBps * avg(commitment.exposureBps) * commitment.exposureBps / 10000^3`. The commitment-exposure dimension is counted twice and the service exposure once more, all factors ≤ 1.0. For three exposures of 50% the operator is slashed at 0.5^3 = 12.5% of the intended amount.

OUTCOME: A misbehaving operator (and their delegators) escape the overwhelming majority of every slash that routes through the commitment path. The protocol's core economic-security guarantee — that a slash of `slashBps` removes ~that fraction of committed stake — is silently broken; `actualSlashed` is a fraction of the intended penalty. The blueprint owner / service owner who proposed a correct `slashBps` cannot achieve the punishment they specified. Fix: either store the raw `cappedSlashBps` in the proposal and let `slashForService` apply per-asset exposure once, or call `slashForBlueprint` with the pre-scaled rate — not both.

finding.title: [accounting] Dispute bond permanently stranded when treasury push reverts on executeSlash
finding.description:
SETUP: An operator disputes a slash and posts `disputeBond` (native ETH). The dispute auto-fails (resolution deadline passes) and anyone calls `executeSlash(slashId)`. Execution path: `markExecuted` (status→Executed) → counters decremented → `_settleDisputeBond(proposal, refund=false)`.

ATTACK / ROOT CAUSE: In `_settleDisputeBond` the forfeit branch pushes the bond to `_treasury` (line 570). If that call fails — treasury is a contract whose `receive`/fallback reverts or runs out of the forwarded gas — the code restores `proposal.disputeBond` and `proposal.disputer` (lines 571-574) instead of reverting. But the proposal is now in `Executed` status (markExecuted already ran at line 311). No code path ever calls `_settleDisputeBond` for an already-executed proposal again: `executeSlash`/`executeSlashBatch` require `isExecutable` (false for Executed) and `cancelSlash` reverts on Executed. The same stranding occurs when `_treasury == address(0)` at execution time (lines 563-568): the bond is restored on an Executed proposal with no re-settlement entrypoint.

OUTCOME: The disputer's bond ETH is permanently locked in the contract with no recovery function, even after a valid treasury is later configured. Impact is bounded (requires a reverting/unset treasury, which is protocol-controlled), hence low severity — but it is a real loss-of-funds defect with no recourse, unlike the cancel path which correctly uses a re-claimable pull mapping. Fix: on forfeit failure, credit a pull-claimable mapping (mirroring the refund branch) rather than re-pinning ETH onto a terminal proposal.

finding.title: [business-logic] Bondless dispute-origin bypasses anti-griefing bond → free 60-day delegator freeze
finding.description:
SETUP: The dispute bond (`SlashConfig.disputeBond`) exists specifically to make disputes costly, so that disputing-to-freeze-stake is not a free DoS (see comments at SlashingLib.sol:80-82 and Slashing.sol:243-248). A blueprint owner controls `bp.manager` and (separately) can propose slashes via `querySlashingOrigin`.

ATTACK: While a slash against `operator` is `Pending`, the operator's delegators are withdrawal-locked because `_operatorPendingSlashCount[operator] > 0` (checked in StakingDelegationsFacet.sol:147 and OperatorManager). A `Pending` slash becomes executable at `executeAfter` (≤30-day window). In `disputeSlash`, a blueprint can designate an arbitrary `disputeOrigin` via the `queryDisputeOrigin` BSM hook (lines 261-271); that origin disputes with `requiredBond = 0` (line 284). The only guard is `msg.sender != proposal.proposer` (line 280), trivially satisfied by using a second blueprint-controlled address. Disputing flips the proposal to `Disputed`, and `isExecutable` now requires `block.timestamp >= disputeDeadline + BUFFER` where `disputeDeadline = disputedAt + disputeResolutionDeadline` (up to 60 days, SlashingLib.sol:165,330,351-353). The pending-slash count is NOT decremented on dispute, so the withdrawal lock persists for the whole extended window.

OUTCOME: A malicious/colluding blueprint can propose a slash (immediate lock) and then bondless-dispute it from a controlled non-proposer address, extending the delegator withdrawal freeze from the dispute window (≤30d) to the resolution deadline (≤60d) at zero cost, repeated across up to `maxPendingSlashesPerOperator` concurrent slashes — defeating the bond's stated anti-griefing purpose. SLASH_ADMIN can cancel to release, but must actively intervene per slash. Requires the operator to have joined the blueprint (trust caveat), but the bond defense is supposed to hold even then. Fix: require the dispute bond on the dispute-origin path, or do not treat a blueprint-supplied origin as a bondless escalation.

finding.title: [business-logic] Oracle enable/disable mid-subscription corrupts TWAP bill scale → indefinite under-billing
finding.description:
SETUP: A Subscription service is activated while a price oracle is configured (`_priceOracle != 0`). `_initSubscriptionBaseline` pins `escrow.subscriptionBaselineStake` as a USD-scaled sum: for each (op,asset) it adds `_safeToUSD(oracle, token, exposedAmount)` (PaymentsDistribution.sol:139 and :154). So the denominator the bill formula divides by is in oracle-USD units (e.g. tokenAmount * price). Per-(op,asset) price snapshots are also stored.

ATTACK / ROOT CAUSE: The bill path decides USD-normalization LIVE, not from the activation-time state. `_accrueOperatorWeights` computes `useOracle = _priceOracle != address(0)` every call (PaymentsBilling.sol:264). `setPriceOracle(address(0))` (Base.sol:282, ADMIN_ROLE, `whenNotPaused`, documented as "set to address(0) to disable") is a routine operational action — the whole `_safeToUSD` design exists to tolerate a flaky oracle. After a disable, `useOracle == false`, so the per-operator contribution stays RAW token-seconds: the `if (useOracle && contribution > 0)` USD/snapshot block at PaymentsBilling.sol:296-312 and :336-344 is skipped entirely. `result.cumDeltaPeriod` is now raw token-seconds while `escrow.subscriptionBaselineStake` is still USD-scaled. No code re-baselines on oracle change (only writer of `subscriptionBaselineStake` is activation; `setPriceOracle` just rewrites the address).

`PaymentLib.twapBillAmount` then evaluates `rate * cumDelta_rawTokenSeconds / (baseline_USD * interval)` (PaymentLib.sol:119). For any asset priced above ~1 USD under a standard `toUSD` scale, `baseline_USD` is orders of magnitude larger than the raw token amount, so the quotient floors toward 0.

OUTCOME: Every subscription activated while the oracle was on is mis-billed after a single `setPriceOracle(0)` — the per-period `amount` collapses to ~0, hits the dust-skip / zero-amount branches (PaymentsBilling.sol:175-183), advances `lastPaymentAt`, and draws ~nothing from escrow. Customers receive ongoing service for free; operators/stakers/keeper get nothing; escrow is under-drawn indefinitely with no revert to signal it. The inverse transition is equally broken: a service activated with NO oracle (baseline = raw token amount, no price snapshot) then billed after the oracle is enabled takes the `snapshot == 0` → live-`_safeToUSD` fallback (PaymentsBilling.sol:307-311 / 340-343), inflating cumDelta to USD against a raw baseline and pinning the bill to the nominal cap (customer over-billed up to nominal). The protocol explicitly designs for oracle outages but does not make oracle ENABLE/DISABLE safe for already-activated subscriptions. Fix: snapshot the oracle mode per service at activation and honor it at bill time (or re-baseline / block oracle toggles while Subscription services are live).

finding.title: [business-logic] Rejoin re-pushes security commitments without clearing → duplicated array inflates billing weight, steals reward share, over-bills customer
finding.description:
SETUP: A Dynamic-membership service has security requirements (`_serviceSecurityRequirements[serviceId].length > 0`). An operator joins via `joinServiceWithCommitments`, which PUSHES each commitment into the array `_serviceSecurityCommitments[serviceId][op]` (line 204) and writes the per-asset `_serviceSecurityCommitmentBps` mapping.

ATTACK / ROOT CAUSE: The commitments ARRAY is never cleared anywhere in the repo. `_removeOperatorFromService` (lines 742-771) sets `opData.active=false` on leave but leaves the array intact (no `delete`/`pop` — confirmed by grep). `_loadJoinContext` (line 631) permits rejoin whenever the caller is `!active`. So an operator can:
1. join (commitments stored once),
2. leave (scheduleExit→executeExit, or leaveService/forceExit) — array untouched,
3. rejoin via `joinServiceWithCommitments` — the loop at lines 203-215 PUSHES the same commitments AGAIN. `_validateSecurityCommitments` only rejects duplicates WITHIN the new calldata, never against what is already stored, so identical valid commitments pass.
Each rejoin appends another full copy → after N cycles the array holds the same asset (N+1) times. The `_serviceSecurityCommitmentBps` mapping is keyed by assetHash so it just overwrites — but every consumer that iterates the ARRAY now double-counts. The inflation is permanent for the service's lifetime.

OUTCOME:
1. Reward-share theft: `_accrueOperatorWeights` (PaymentsBilling.sol:274-356) iterates the array and does `opWeight += contribution` per entry (line 345). Duplicate entries share assetHash/cursor/projectedCum/exposureBps, so an identical `contribution` is summed (N+1)× → the rejoiner's `weights[i]` is multiplied. `_distributeBill` splits each interval's payout proportionally by `weights[i]/totalWeight`, so the attacker siphons reward share from honest operators every billing period, indefinitely.
2. Customer over-billing: the same loop accumulates `result.cumDeltaPeriod += opWeight` (line 357); inflated cumDelta raises `PaymentLib.twapBillAmount` (up to the rate cap), draining more of the customer's escrow each interval than the contracted stake warrants.
3. Slashing corruption: `_executeSlashOnStaking` (Slashing.sol:501-502) loads the duplicated array via `_loadServiceCommitments` and forwards it to `_staking.slashForService`, applying the per-asset slash multiple times.

Friction (protocol defaults: minCommitment 1d, exitQueue 7d) only paces stacking; a blueprint manager's `getExitConfig` hook can set both to 0 for instant unlimited stacking, and even one cycle yields a permanent 2× weight. Fix: on leave, `delete _serviceSecurityCommitments[serviceId][op]` (and the cursor/snapshot state), or overwrite-not-append on (re)join, or merge-dedup new commitments against stored ones.

finding.title: [business-logic] Termination refunds full escrow to owner without settling the in-progress subscription period → operators forfeit rendered-service pay
finding.description:
SETUP: A Subscription service is Active. Operators have provided service during the open period [`svc.lastPaymentAt`, now], but the keeper has not yet billed it (billing is permissionless/periodic, drawn at `billSubscription` time).

ATTACK: The service owner calls `terminateService` → `_terminateService` (line 115) sets `status = Terminated` and performs NO final bill of the open period. Subscription billing is then permanently blocked because `_billSubscriptionImpl` requires `svc.status == Active` (PaymentsBilling.sol:90). The owner then calls `withdrawRemainingEscrow` (PaymentsRefund.sol:35-53), which returns the ENTIRE `escrow.balance` to the owner — including the funds corresponding to the already-rendered-but-unbilled period. Neither `onServiceTerminated` (fee distributor) nor `onServiceTermination` (manager) settles the subscription escrow to operators.

OUTCOME: Operators forfeit up to one full billing interval of pay for service they actually delivered, and the owner reclaims it. Because the owner fully controls termination timing, they can deliberately terminate immediately before the keeper's next bill to claw back nearly a whole interval of already-rendered service for free, each time leaking value from operators to the owner. Impact is bounded to <1 interval per service (hence low), but it is a real, repeatable economic asymmetry on the lifecycle path: cancellation should pro-rata-settle the open period to operators before refunding the remainder. Fix: run a final partial-period bill (or pro-rata accrual settlement) inside `_terminateService` before allowing `withdrawRemainingEscrow`.

finding.title: [business-logic] effectiveStakingPercent to BSM onApprove uses only securityCommitments[0] → operator under-reports stake
finding.description:
SETUP: A request carries multiple security requirements, e.g. requirements = [USDC (min=max=100%), TNT (min=10%)]. The inline comment at L112-115 states the per-operator exposure handed to the blueprint manager "must mirror what was actually committed." The manager receives it via onApprove (L146) and a custom BSM commonly gates approval/eligibility on this percent (e.g. "reject operators committing <50%").

ATTACK: The approving operator passes p.securityCommitments ordered so index 0 is the asset with the HIGHEST exposure. validateSecurityCommitments (ServiceValidationLib) only checks each requirement is matched within [min,max] and is order-independent, so the operator can legitimately commit USDC=100% (index 0) and TNT=10% (index 1) — or any ordering. At L116-120 the code computes effectiveStakingPercent = uint8(p.securityCommitments[0].exposureBps / 100), i.e. it reflects ONLY the first commitment (100%), not the minimum/representative commitment. The full per-asset commitment set is never passed to the manager hook (L146 only forwards the single uint8).

OUTCOME: The manager is told the operator staked 100% when its binding exposure on other required assets is the minimum (10%). A BSM that authorizes operators or sizes work/rewards based on the reported percent is deceived: the operator clears a stake-threshold gate it should fail, while its real slashable exposure (Slashing.sol L73-77 multiplies requester exposure by the per-asset-averaged commitmentBps) is far lower. This breaks the contract's own documented invariant that the reported value mirrors what was committed. Fix: derive effectiveStakingPercent from the minimum committed exposure across requirements (or pass the full commitment array to the hook).

finding.title: [dos] Unbounded permittedCallers list copied at activation → last approver bears O(N) gas, request can be bricked
finding.description:
SETUP: requestService / requestServiceWithExposure / requestServiceWithSecurity accept an arbitrary-length address[] permittedCallers. _storePermittedCallers (L316-320) pushes every entry with NO length cap (operators and security-requirements arrays are both bounded — maxOperators, MAX_SECURITY_REQUIREMENTS_PER_REQUEST — but permittedCallers is not).

ATTACK: A requester submits a request whose operator list is valid but supplies a very large permittedCallers array (thousands of entries). Operators 1..N-1 approve cheaply (approval does not touch the caller list). The FINAL approval is the one that drives req.approvalCount == req.operatorCount and synchronously calls _activateService → _grantPermittedCallers (TangleServicesFacet.sol L144-150), which loops the entire caller list adding each to an EnumerableSet (O(N) SSTOREs) — all charged to the last approver's transaction. If the list is large enough to push activation past the block gas limit, the last approve() always reverts: the service can never activate and sits until expiry.

OUTCOME: (1) Cost-shifting — the operator unlucky enough to be the activation-triggering approver pays unbounded gas for state the requester chose. (2) Griefing/DoS — a request can be constructed that is approvable but never activatable, wasting every operator's repeated approve gas; the requester risks only a refundable deposit. Fix: bound permittedCallers.length at request time (mirror the operator/security-requirement caps).

finding.title: [access-control] RFQ quote services skip _operatorActiveServiceCount → operator withdraws full stake mid-service
finding.description:
SETUP: A service is created via the RFQ quote path `createServiceFromQuotes` → `_activateQuoteService` → `_processOperatorQuotes` (QuotesCreate.sol:275-300). For each quoted operator this writes `_serviceOperators[serviceId][op]` (active=true, exposureBps) and adds them to `_serviceOperatorSet`, making them live, paid security providers for the service.

ROOT CAUSE: `_processOperatorQuotes` NEVER increments `_operatorActiveServiceCount[blueprintId][op]`. The standard (request/approval) activation path does — TangleServicesFacet.sol:140 `_operatorActiveServiceCount[blueprintId][op]++` with the comment "Track active service count per blueprint for operator unregistration checks" — and so does join (ServicesLifecycle.sol:672). The RFQ/quote creation path omits it entirely (confirmed: the only writers of this counter are TangleServicesFacet:140 and ServicesLifecycle:672/758). Service extension (QuotesExtend) likewise never sets it.

This counter is the ONLY active-service guard on two privileged exits:
1. `unregisterOperator` (Operators.sol:204): `if (_operatorActiveServiceCount[blueprintId][msg.sender] > 0) revert OperatorHasActiveServices(...)` — its docstring (L193) promises "Reverts if operator has any active services for this blueprint".
2. `startLeaving()` → `_startLeaving()` (StakingOperatorsFacet.sol:77, OperatorManager.sol:231-252): the operator-leaves-staking guard staticcalls `getOperatorTotalActiveServices(operator)` (Operators.sol:303-309), which simply SUMS `_operatorActiveServiceCount` across all blueprints, and reverts `OperatorHasActiveServices` only if >0.

ATTACK: An operator joins only RFQ/quote-created services (signs quotes consumed by `createServiceFromQuotes`). The customer pays upfront and the service relies on the operator's bonded stake as economic security for its whole TTL. Because the counter stays 0 for this operator, `getOperatorTotalActiveServices` returns 0, so — provided no slash has been proposed yet (`_operatorPendingSlashCount == 0`) — the operator calls `startLeaving()` (passes the guard), waits the leave delay, completes leaving and withdraws their ENTIRE operator bond/stake. They can also `unregisterOperator` for the blueprint, freeing their gossip key slot and calling `removeBlueprintForOperator`, all while `_serviceOperators[serviceId][op].active == true`.

OUTCOME: The core economic-security invariant — an operator backing a live, paid service cannot pull their stake — is silently void for the entire RFQ/quote service class. The operator collects the upfront RFQ payment, exits staking, and withdraws all bonded collateral while the service is still Active and customers depend on that collateral. If the operator subsequently misbehaves or goes offline, there is little or no stake left to slash. Fix: increment `_operatorActiveServiceCount[blueprintId][op]` for each operator in `_processOperatorQuotes` (and decrement on RFQ-service removal/termination), mirroring TangleServicesFacet.sol:140.

finding.title: [access-control] Operator gossip key registered with no proof-of-possession → identity squat/DoS
finding.description:
SETUP: `registerOperator` accepts an arbitrary 65-byte `ecdsaPublicKey` as the operator's gossip-network identity. The only validation is `ecdsaPublicKey.length == 65` (Operators.sol:126) and per-blueprint uniqueness via `_blueprintOperatorKeys[blueprintId][keyHash]` (L130-132). There is NO proof that the registrant controls the corresponding private key (no challenge signature, no on-curve / 0x04-prefix check), and the same first-come dedup gate also governs `updateOperatorPreferences` (L266-274, which only blocks `existing != address(0) && existing != msg.sender`).

ATTACK: Operator B's gossip public key is public (it IS their network identity). A malicious operator A — who only needs to be staking-active and registered for the blueprint — front-runs B's `registerOperator` (or `updateOperatorPreferences`) call for blueprint X using B's known public key. The write `_blueprintOperatorKeys[X][keccak(B_pubkey)] = A` succeeds. When B then tries to register/update with their real key, it reverts `DuplicateOperatorKey`.

OUTCOME: (1) DoS — B is permanently unable to register their genuine gossip identity for that blueprint and must rotate to a different key. (2) Misattribution — the on-chain pubkey→operator map that off-chain indexers/peers rely on now resolves B's real network key to attacker A's wallet, so messages B signs with that key are attributed to A (or rejected), corrupting gossip-layer identity accounting. A cannot forge B's signatures (no private key), so impact is squat/DoS/attribution rather than full impersonation — hence low severity. Fix: require a signature over a domain-separated challenge (e.g. operator wallet address) proving possession of the gossip private key before binding the key.

finding.title: [business-logic] Aggregated result accepted with empty signer set + zero-sig when no operator is staking-active → result spoofing
finding.description:
SETUP: A service is Active with a pending paid (EventDriven) aggregation job (callId not completed). All of the service's operators remain members of `_serviceOperatorSet` (svc.status stays Active — leaveService is gated by minOperators, but **staking** status is independent) yet every one of them currently returns `_staking.isOperatorActive(op) == false` (unbonding/started-leaving/slashed-to-zero — see sibling finding sub-5-deep-audit-a0505ef9 showing RFQ operators can even startLeaving while in a service).

ROOT CAUSE: `_computeSignerStats` (JobsAggregation.sol:215-228) skips every operator failing the active checks, so with zero staking-active operators it returns `operatorCount = 0`, `totalWeight = 0`. `_validateSignersAndThreshold` (184-194) then computes `required = _ceilDiv(0 * thresholdBps, 10000) = 0`. The clamp `if (required == 0 && stats.operatorCount > 0) required = 1` is guarded on `operatorCount > 0` and therefore does NOT fire when operatorCount == 0 — `required` stays 0. With an empty `signerBitmap`, `achieved = signerCount = 0`, so the gate `if (achieved < required)` is `0 < 0 == false` and the threshold check PASSES.

ATTACK: Anyone calls `submitAggregatedResult(serviceId, callId, output, signerBitmap=0, aggregatedSignature=[0,0], aggregatedPubkey=[0,0,0,0])`. With bitmap 0, `_computeExpectedAggregatedPubkeyFromList` (294-310) adds no keys and returns the point at infinity (0,0,0,0); the attacker supplies the same, so `g2Eq` passes. `verifyAggregatedBls` (BN254.sol:440-449 → verifyBls 420-432) then runs `pairingCheck(sig=O, G2, H(m), pubkey=O)` which evaluates e(O,G2)·e(-H(m),O)=1·1=1 → returns true. verifyBls never rejects the infinity point. The call reaches `_finalizeAggregatedResult`.

OUTCOME: `job.completed=true`; attacker-chosen `output` is emitted as `AggregatedResultSubmitted` and delivered to the blueprint manager via `onAggregatedResult` as a validly-attested aggregated result — pure result spoofing with NO BLS signature and NO signer. For an EventDriven paid job, `_distributeJobPayment` then pays out `job.payment` to the service operator set (the now-inactive operators, plausibly the attacker), draining the consumer's escrowed job payment for work no one performed/attested. Fix: revert when `stats.operatorCount == 0` (or `signerCount == 0`) in `_validateSignersAndThreshold`, and reject the point at infinity for signature/pubkey in `verifyBls`.

finding.title: [access-control] Any single service operator overwrites service-wide acked version/policy → forces whole service onto attacker-chosen binary
finding.description:
SETUP: A service has 2+ active operators. The "supply-chain hardening" block (TangleStorage.sol:509-516) added per-operator policy/ack mappings specifically to close a documented single-operator griefing ("any single operator could overwrite for everyone"). The per-operator resolver `effectiveBinaryVersionForOperator` (L308-328) reads only `_serviceOperatorUpgradePolicy`/`_serviceOperatorAckedVersionId`.

ROOT CAUSE: `setServiceUpgradePolicy` and `ackBinaryVersion` STILL write the legacy service-wide scalars unconditionally for any active operator:
- L161: `_serviceUpgradePolicy[serviceId] = policy;`
- L189: `_serviceAckedVersionId[serviceId] = versionId;`
There is no owner/governance gate and no "only if you are the sole operator" guard — the calling operator's choice clobbers the shared scalar for the entire service. The service-wide resolver `effectiveBinaryVersion(serviceId)` (L264-284) reads exactly these shared scalars, and TangleStorage.sol:455 documents `effectiveBinaryVersion` as THE canonical resolver ("Resolution of the effective version for a service is handled by BlueprintsBinaryVersions.effectiveBinaryVersion"). The hardening comment at L159 contradicts this, telling dispatchers to use the per-operator function. Both resolvers are exposed as facet selectors (TangleBlueprintsBinaryVersionsFacet L23 and L26), so an off-chain dispatcher following the storage-layout guidance reads the griefable path.

ATTACK (multi-operator service, attacker = one active operator):
1. Honest operators intend APPROVE pinned to genesis v0. Attacker calls `ackBinaryVersion(serviceId, N)` for any existing non-deprecated version N → `_serviceAckedVersionId[serviceId] = N` for the whole service. Or `setServiceUpgradePolicy(serviceId, AUTO)` → `_serviceUpgradePolicy[serviceId] = AUTO`, making `effectiveBinaryVersion` return `versions[_blueprintActiveVersionId]` (blueprint-owner-controlled, any version incl. deprecated).
2. Any consumer resolving via the documented-canonical `effectiveBinaryVersion` now serves version N (or the owner's active version) to EVERY operator of the service.

OUTCOME: A lone operator unilaterally moves the binary the entire service is dispatched on — forward to an unwanted/premature build, backward to a known-weak (non-deprecated) genesis, or (by flipping to AUTO) onto whatever the blueprint owner has set active. This is precisely the cross-operator griefing the per-operator mapping was introduced to eliminate; it remains fully open for any consumer on the documented resolver. Contingent on the off-chain dispatcher reading `effectiveBinaryVersion` vs `effectiveBinaryVersionForOperator` (hence medium, not high). Fix: stop writing the service-wide scalars from per-operator actions (delete L161/L189), or gate them so one operator cannot overwrite shared state; and reconcile the contradictory "canonical resolver" documentation to point exclusively at the per-operator resolver.

finding.title: [business-logic] Cold-start source repoint (setBlueprintSources) is not whenNotPaused while in-place upgrade path is → pause cannot freeze supply-chain incident
finding.description:
SETUP: The protocol has an emergency pause (the binary-version mutators `publishBinaryVersion`/`setActiveBinaryVersion`/`deprecateBinaryVersion`/`ackBinaryVersion` in BlueprintsBinaryVersions.sol are all `whenNotPaused`-gated, L67/L114/L127/L173). Per `setBlueprintSources`' own NatSpec (BlueprintsManage.sol:118-123), the off-chain manager resolves the INITIAL binary an operator boots from `_blueprintSources` (cold-start), NOT from published BinaryVersions — so the source set is the most safety-critical supply-chain surface.

ROOT CAUSE: NONE of the blueprint-lifecycle mutators in BlueprintsManage.sol carry `whenNotPaused` (confirmed: zero matches in the file): `setBlueprintSources` (L124), `updateBlueprint` (L92), `transferBlueprint`/`acceptBlueprintOwnership`/`cancelBlueprintTransfer` (L139/154/168), `ackBlueprintSources` (L190), `deactivateBlueprint` (L220), `setJobEventRates` (L238), `setBlueprintResourceRequirements` (L281). The pausable gate was applied to the in-place upgrade path but omitted from the cold-start source path and ownership transfers.

ATTACK / OUTCOME: When governance pauses in response to a discovered supply-chain compromise (e.g. a malicious binary or a leaked owner key), a blueprint owner — including a compromised one — can still call `setBlueprintSources` to repoint the cold-start artifacts to attacker-controlled binaries, and operators can still `ackBlueprintSources` them; ownership can also still be transferred (`transferBlueprint`/`acceptBlueprintOwnership`) mid-incident. The emergency stop therefore freezes the less-critical in-place upgrade path but leaves the more-critical cold-start binary distribution and ownership-handoff paths fully live. Inconsistent pause coverage; low severity because it requires a concurrent owner-key compromise and an active pause. Fix: add `whenNotPaused` to `setBlueprintSources`, `ackBlueprintSources`, and the ownership-transfer functions to match the binary-version mutators.

finding.title: REPRODUCED: RFQ quote services skip _operatorActiveServiceCount → operator unregisters mid-service
finding.description:
REPRODUCED via Foundry differential PoC (test/audit/RFQActiveServiceCountBypassPoC.t.sol, 2/2 passing).

SETUP: rfqOperator stakes MIN_OPERATOR_STAKE (staking-Active), registers for a blueprint, signs an RFQ quote. user1 calls createServiceFromQuotes{value:1 ether}. The resulting service is Active and rfqOperator is a paid member of _serviceOperatorSet.

ATTACK / OUTCOME — confirmed observations:
1. RFQ path: counter never set. Read directly from storage slot 65 (confirmed via `forge inspect`), _operatorActiveServiceCount[blueprintId][rfqOperator] == 0 WHILE the service status == Active. _processOperatorQuotes (QuotesCreate.sol:284-300) writes _serviceOperators/_serviceOperatorSet but never increments the counter.
2. Guard bypassed: rfqOperator successfully calls unregisterOperator(blueprintId) with NO revert, even though the service is still Active. unregisterOperator's only active-service guard (Operators.sol:204 `if (_operatorActiveServiceCount[...] > 0) revert OperatorHasActiveServices`) reads that same storage and passes because it is 0. getService(serviceId).status is still Active afterward.
3. CONTROL (same harness): an identical operator activated via the STANDARD request/approve path has _operatorActiveServiceCount == 1 (TangleServicesFacet.sol:140 increments it), and the identical unregisterOperator call reverts OperatorHasActiveServices(blueprintId, operator). This proves the differential: the counter is incremented on the standard path and skipped on the RFQ path.

The same 0-valued counter also feeds getOperatorTotalActiveServices, the guard for staking startLeaving() — so an RFQ-only operator can also begin leaving staking and withdraw their entire bond while backing a live, paid service. (Additional note surfaced during repro: getOperatorTotalActiveServices(address) is not registered as a router selector in the faceted deployment — calling it through the Tangle proxy reverts UnknownSelector(0x0ded8bf9) — so the startLeaving staticcall path `if (success && data.length>=32)` in OperatorManager.sol:242-251 silently skips the check entirely; this makes the startLeaving guard even weaker than the finding states. The deterministic, in-contract proof above uses the unregisterOperator guard, which reads the counter directly and is fully reachable.)

Fix unchanged: increment _operatorActiveServiceCount[blueprintId][op] for each operator in _processOperatorQuotes (and decrement on RFQ-service removal/termination), mirroring TangleServicesFacet.sol:140; and register the getOperatorTotalActiveServices selector on a facet.

reproduction.pocStdout:
