Standard request/approve path (control): counter==1, identical unregisterOperator reverts OperatorHasActiveServices. Counter value read directly from storage slot 65 (forge inspect). Also confirmed getOperatorTotalActiveServices is unrouted (UnknownSelector) in the faceted deployment, weakening the startLeaving guard further.

finding.title: [access-control] startLeaving() active-service guard is globally dead — unrouted selector → any operator exits stake while backing live services
finding.description:
SETUP: When an operator calls startLeaving() to begin exiting the staking layer (and ultimately withdraw their bond), _startLeaving() (OperatorManager.sol:231-255) is meant to block the exit if the operator still backs active services. It does this by staticcalling the Tangle core: `_tangleCore.staticcall(abi.encodeWithSignature("getOperatorTotalActiveServices(address)", msg.sender))` and reverting OperatorHasActiveServices when the returned count > 0.

ROOT CAUSE: `getOperatorTotalActiveServices(address)` is implemented in Operators.sol:303 but is NOT registered as a router selector on ANY facet. TangleOperatorsFacet.selectors() (TangleOperatorsFacet.sol:10-16) registers only preRegister, registerOperator (x2), unregisterOperator, updateOperatorPreferences. The selector 0x0ded8bf9 (= cast sig "getOperatorTotalActiveServices(address)") is absent from every facet's selectors() list. Calling it through the Tangle proxy therefore hits the router fallback and reverts UnknownSelector(0x0ded8bf9).

ATTACK: The guard swallows that failure: `if (success && data.length >= 32)`. Because the staticcall reverts, `success == false`, so the entire active-services check is skipped for EVERY operator. Empirically confirmed during reproduction (finding sub-9-repro): the proxy call reverts UnknownSelector(0x0ded8bf9). The only remaining gate in _startLeaving is `_operatorPendingSlashCount[msg.sender] > 0`.

OUTCOME: Any operator backing any number of live, paid services (standard request/approve path OR RFQ path) can call startLeaving() without reverting and proceed through the staking exit/withdrawal flow, pulling their bonded collateral out from under services that depend on it as slashable security — provided no slash is already pending. This voids the protocol-wide invariant "an operator backing an active service cannot begin exiting stake" for the entire operator population, not just the RFQ subset (cf. sub-5, which exploits the separate in-contract unregisterOperator counter). Fix: register the getOperatorTotalActiveServices selector on TangleOperatorsFacet (or a views facet), AND treat a failed/empty staticcall as fail-closed (revert) rather than skipping the check.

finding.title: Buyback swaps with zero slippage protection + permissionless trigger -> MEV sandwich drains revenue
finding.description: _executeBuyback sets amountOutMinimum=minOut where minOut=expectedOut*(10000-bps)/10000 and expectedOut comes from _getExpectedOutput which by DEFAULT returns 0 (line 269-273). So minOut=0, sqrtPriceLimitX96=0, deadline=block.timestamp. ATTACK: AUTO mode swaps on every ETH dev payment (delivered via core tryTransferPayment .call -> receive() -> _onPaymentReceived), and executeBuyback/executeBuybackAll (189-201) are permissionless (nonReentrant only). An MEV bot sandwiches the WETH->token swap: buy token before, let the contract swap at an inflated price receiving near-zero tokens, sell after. OUTCOME: protocol buyback ETH is extracted by MEV, stakers/treasury get dust. The 5% maxSlippageBps default is meaningless because it multiplies expectedOut=0.

finding.title: Instant-mode reward distribution has no stake lock -> just-in-time staking steals rewards
finding.description: In instant mode _notifyReward immediately bumps rewardPerTokenStored += amount*1e18/totalStaked. Revenue payments are public (core push to receive()) and stake()/withdraw() have NO lock or cooldown. ATTACK: observe a pending revenue payment in mempool, stake a large amount just before it lands, capturing a proportional share of the instant distribution, then withdraw+claim immediately (same/next block). OUTCOME: honest long-term stakers are diluted; the attacker extracts most of each revenue distribution with zero time-at-risk. Severity rises if the blueprint ERC20 is DEX/flash-loanable, enabling atomic stake->capture->withdraw.

finding.title: ERC20 developer revenue is permanently stranded in the BSM (no hook, no rescue)
finding.description: _onPaymentReceived is ONLY ever invoked by BlueprintServiceManagerBase.receive() with token=address(0) (confirmed: receive() -> _onPaymentReceived(address(0), msg.value) is the sole caller in src/). Core delivers ERC20 developer revenue via PaymentLib.tryTransferPayment, which does a raw IERC20.transfer to the BSM (PaymentsDistribution.sol:252) and triggers NO hook. Therefore Buyback._onPaymentReceived token!=0 branch (super._onPaymentReceived -> _notifyReward) is dead for real payments: ERC20 revenue lands in the contract balance but is never added to rewardStates, never distributed, and there is NO rescue/withdraw/sweep function in either extension (only setBuybackPaused is owner-callable). OUTCOME: any ERC20-denominated developer revenue is permanently frozen.

finding.title: AUTO-mode buyback swap revert forfeits entire developer revenue share to operators
finding.description: In AUTO mode an ETH dev payment hits receive() -> _onPaymentReceived -> _executeBuyback. If the Uniswap swap reverts, the catch block (233-240) calls weth.withdraw then `revert BuybackFailed()` -- the revert undoes the catch state changes too, so the try/catch is ineffective and the whole receive() reverts. Core treats the ETH push as best-effort: tryTransferPayment returns false and PaymentsDistribution.sol:251-255 zeroes developerAmount and folds it into operatorAmount. OUTCOME: every time the buyback swap reverts (broken/uninitialized pool, router misconfig, insufficient gas in the un-capped push), the blueprint forfeits its ENTIRE developer revenue share for that bill to the operator pool, silently. The comment promising funds are kept as pending is wrong.

finding.title: Streaming-mode rewards accrued while totalStaked==0 are permanently lost
finding.description: rewardPerToken() early-returns rewardPerTokenStored when totalStaked==0, while _updateRewardPerToken still advances lastUpdateTime=min(ts,periodFinish). If totalStaked hits 0 during a streaming period, the rewardRate keeps ticking but no rewardPerToken accrues; when a staker rejoins, lastUpdateTime has already advanced past that gap. OUTCOME: the ETH streamed during the zero-stake window is never distributed and is stuck in the contract (classic Synthetix dead-time gap). Lower severity since streamingMode defaults false.

finding.title: Instant-mode reward rounding truncates small payments to zero against 100M-supply stake
finding.description: _notifyReward instant mode: rewardPerTokenStored += (amount*1e18)/totalStaked. With genesis supply ~100M tokens (totalStaked up to ~1e26 wei), any payment with amount*1e18 < totalStaked truncates to 0 (e.g. amount < ~1e8 wei when totalStaked=1e26). Repeated small payments accrue no rewards and the ETH is stuck in the contract. Same truncation in _updateReward pendingRewards distribution (306) and rewardPerToken streaming branch (264).

finding.title: [F-SLA-1] Double exposure scaling in commitment slash path systematically under-slashes
finding.description:
SETUP: A service has security requirements (`_serviceSecurityRequirements[serviceId].length > 0`) and the operator has per-asset commitments (`_serviceSecurityCommitments`). Note `_serviceSecurityCommitmentBps[serviceId][operator][assetHash]` is set to the *same* `commitment.exposureBps` value stored in each commitment (TangleServicesFacet.sol:88, ServicesLifecycle.sol:207).

ATTACK / BUG: The operator's per-asset commitment exposure is multiplied into the slash TWICE.
1) At propose time (Slashing.sol:73-90): `effectiveExposureBps = opData.exposureBps * commitmentBps / 1e4`, where `commitmentBps` = `_computeServiceCommitmentExposureBps` = the (weighted) average of the very same `commitment.exposureBps` values. This is baked into `proposal.effectiveSlashBps = slashBps * effectiveExposureBps / 1e4` via SlashingLib.proposeSlash/calculateEffectiveSlashBps.
2) At execute time, `_executeSlashOnStaking` (Slashing.sol) routes to `_slashForService`, which for each commitment computes `effectiveBps = proposal.effectiveSlashBps * commitment.exposureBps / 1e4` (SlashingManager.sol:270). So `commitment.exposureBps` is applied a SECOND time on top of the already-exposure-scaled `effectiveSlashBps`.

Worked example: slashBps=10000 (100%), opData.exposureBps=10000, single asset commitment.exposureBps=5000 (50%). propose → effectiveSlashBps = 10000*5000/1e4 = 5000. execute → effectiveBps = 5000*5000/1e4 = 2500 = 25%. A configured 100% slash of a 50%-committed asset removes only 25% of that pool instead of the intended 50%.

OUTCOME: Every slash that goes through the commitment path is reduced by an extra factor of ~exposureBps/1e4 (quadratic in exposure). Operators and their delegators are under-slashed; the protocol's economic-security guarantee is silently halved (or worse for low exposures). The misbehaving operator escapes the bulk of the penalty. Compare with the legacy `slashForBlueprint` path (no commitments), where exposure is applied exactly once — confirming the commitment path double-counts.

finding.title: [F-SLA-1] Exposure applied twice in service-commitment slash path → under-slash
finding.description:
SETUP: A slash is proposed via proposeSlash(). At propose time the slash fraction is exposure-scaled: effectiveExposureBps = opData.exposureBps, optionally further scaled by the commitment-weighted average from _computeServiceCommitmentExposureBps (Slashing.sol:73-77). SlashingLib.proposeSlash then stores proposal.effectiveSlashBps = cappedSlashBps * effectiveExposureBps / 10000 (SlashingLib.sol:247, calculateEffectiveSlashBps). So effectiveSlashBps is ALREADY the final exposure-scaled fraction.

ATTACK / DEFECT: executeSlash -> _executeSlashOnStaking (Slashing.sol:494-517). When the operator has stored per-asset commitments, it calls _staking.slashForService(operator, blueprintId, serviceId, commitments, proposal.effectiveSlashBps, evidence) passing effectiveSlashBps as the slashBps argument. SlashingManager._slashForService (SlashingManager.sol:268-285) then re-scales by each commitment: effectiveBps = (slashBps * commitment.exposureBps) / 10000. Exposure is therefore applied a SECOND time. Net fraction slashed per asset = cappedSlashBps * effectiveExposureBps/1e4 * commitment.exposureBps/1e4. With e.g. opData.exposureBps=5000 and commitment.exposureBps=5000, a requested 100% slash applies only 25%.

This is inconsistent with the fallback blueprint path in the same function (commitments.length==0 -> _staking.slashForBlueprint(..., proposal.effectiveSlashBps, ...), SlashingManager.sol:179-231) which applies effectiveSlashBps exactly once with NO further exposure scaling. The blueprint path's single application is correct; the service path double-discounts.

OUTCOME: Operators with explicit service commitments are systematically UNDER-slashed (and their delegators correspondingly under-charged), silently weakening the protocol's core economic-security guarantee. A misbehaving operator escapes the intended penalty. Fix: pass the raw cappedSlashBps (proposal.slashBps) into slashForService so exposure is applied exactly once, OR remove the per-commitment re-scaling.

finding.title: [F-SLA-2] advanceRound() and snapshotOperator() are permissionless
finding.description:
SETUP: Every other state-mutating entrypoint on StakingSlashingFacet (slash*, increment/decrementPendingSlash) is guarded by `onlyRole(SLASHER_ROLE)`. `advanceRound()` (line 74) and `snapshotOperator()` (line 80) carry NO access modifier — any EOA can call them.

ATTACK 1 (advanceRound): `_advanceRound` (SlashingManager.sol:790) only rate-limits by `lastRoundAdvance + roundDuration` but does not restrict the caller. `currentRound` is the clock for delegator unstake maturation: `_scheduleDelegatorUnstake` stamps `requestedRound = currentRound` and `_executeDelegatorUnstake` releases once `currentRound >= requestedRound + delegationBondLessDelay` (DelegationManagerLib.sol:439,470). An attacker can front-run / drive round advancement themselves on every `roundDuration` boundary, removing the protocol's ability to gate round cadence through a trusted keeper, and deterministically advancing the unstake clock the moment it is permissible rather than when governance intends.

ATTACK 2 (snapshotOperator): `_snapshotOperator` (SlashingManager.sol:764) requires only that the operator is Active and then overwrites `_atStake[currentRound][operator]` with the operator's *current live* `stake`/`totalDelegated`. Any caller can (re)write this snapshot arbitrarily many times within a round, including late in the round after delegations/withdrawals have shifted, so the per-round "at stake" record can be manipulated away from the intended round-start value that off-chain reward/slashing accounting (`getSnapshot`) relies on.

OUTCOME: Loss of governance control over round progression and corruptible round snapshots. Bounded (advance is rate-limited; on-chain slashing math reads live pool balances rather than `_atStake`), hence low severity — but both functions should be `onlyRole(SLASHER_ROLE)` / an explicit keeper role to match the rest of the facet.

finding.title: [F-SLA-2] snapshotOperator() permissionless and re-writable within a round
finding.description:
SETUP: snapshotOperator(address) is exposed with NO access-control modifier (contrast every other mutating selector in this facet, which is onlyRole(SLASHER_ROLE)). It calls SlashingManager._snapshotOperator (SlashingManager.sol:764-777), which unconditionally overwrites _atStake[currentRound][operator] = {stake, totalDelegated} from the operator's CURRENT live state. There is no once-per-round guard.

ATTACK: Any external account can (a) take the round snapshot at an arbitrary moment instead of round start, and (b) repeatedly overwrite an already-taken snapshot later in the same round. By front-running/back-running deposits, undelegations, or slashes and then calling snapshotOperator, an attacker chooses which intra-round stake/totalDelegated value is recorded for (round, operator). This breaks the documented "snapshot of operator state at round start" invariant the struct name (_atStake) and comments rely on.

OUTCOME: On-chain impact is currently bounded because no economic logic in src/ reads _atStake — the only reader is the getSnapshot() view (SlashingManager.sol:780). The realized damage is corruption/manipulation of the historical snapshot consumed by off-chain indexers, reward dashboards, and any future upgrade that wires _atStake into slash/reward math (the module is explicitly built "for historical slashing"). If such an upgrade ships without first adding access control, this becomes a direct economic manipulation surface. Fix: gate snapshotOperator behind SLASHER_ROLE/ADMIN_ROLE and/or make _snapshotOperator a no-op when _atStake[currentRound][operator] is already set.

finding.title: Double exposure scaling in commitment slash path under-slashes operators by exposure factor
finding.description: executeSlash (reachable from in-scope TangleSlashingFacet.executeSlash) under-slashes operators that made per-asset security commitments. In Slashing.proposeSlash (Slashing.sol:73-92) effectiveExposureBps = opData.exposureBps * commitmentBps / 1e4, where commitmentBps = _computeServiceCommitmentExposureBps() = the average of the operator per-asset commitment.exposureBps values. SlashingLib.proposeSlash then stores proposal.effectiveSlashBps = slashBps * effectiveExposureBps / 1e4 (SlashingLib.sol:197,247). At execution _executeSlashOnStaking (Slashing.sol:494-517) routes commitment-bearing operators to SlashingManager._slashForService, passing proposal.effectiveSlashBps as slashBps. _slashForService (SlashingManager.sol:268-284) then re-applies per-asset exposure: effectiveBps = slashBps * commitment.exposureBps / 1e4. Since commitmentBps IS the average of those same commitment.exposureBps values, exposure is applied TWICE. Net slash = slashBps * opData.exposureBps/1e4 * commitmentBps/1e4 * commitment.exposureBps/1e4. The no-commitment fallback _slashForBlueprint (Slashing.sol:504-507) applies effectiveSlashBps once with NO further scaling, which is the correct single application — confirming the commitment branch double-counts. SETUP: operator commits exposureBps E to a service (single asset, opData.exposureBps=100%). ATTACK: governance proposes a 50% slash for misbehavior. OUTCOME: intended slash of committed pool = 50% * E; actual = 50% * E * E/1e4. For E=10% the operator loses ~10% of the intended penalty; for E=50%, half. Operators with per-asset commitments systematically escape the bulk of any slash, breaking the protocols core economic-security guarantee. The commitment branch should pass the RAW cappedSlashBps (un-exposure-scaled) to _slashForService, OR _slashForService should not re-scale by commitment.exposureBps.

finding.title: [F-SLA-2] Permissionless snapshotOperator overwrites per-round operator snapshot
finding.description:
SETUP: `StakingSlashingFacet.snapshotOperator(address)` (line 80) has NO access-control modifier — it is callable by any EOA for any operator. It forwards to `SlashingManager._snapshotOperator` (SlashingManager.sol:764-777), which writes `_atStake[currentRound][operator] = {stake, totalDelegated}` from the operator's CURRENT live metadata. There is no idempotency / "already snapshotted this round" guard, so the slot can be overwritten arbitrarily many times within a round; the only precondition is `meta.status == Active`.

ATTACK: An unprivileged attacker calls `snapshotOperator(victimOperator)` at a moment of their choosing within a round — e.g. immediately after the victim schedules a self-bond reduction, after a large delegation enters/exits, or simply repeatedly — to (re)write the round's authoritative snapshot to a value that does not represent the intended round-start state. Because the write is last-writer-wins, the attacker controls WHEN within the round the stake is sampled.

OUTCOME: The per-round snapshot (`getSnapshot(round, operator)`, the only reader) is the protocol's record of "operator stake at round R" for historical slashing / reward attribution. On-chain there is currently no consumer of `_atStake`, so direct on-chain fund impact is nil today — hence LOW. But any off-chain reward/slash computation or future on-chain logic that trusts `getSnapshot` as a faithful round-start sample can be skewed by an attacker timing/overwriting the snapshot. The function should be restricted (SLASHER_ROLE / TANGLE_ROLE / keeper) and/or made write-once-per-round, matching the role-gating already applied to the sibling slash functions in the same facet (lines 36, 53, 67, 91, 98).

finding.title: executeDelegatorUnstakeAndWithdraw halves effective unbonding period vs standard two-step exit
finding.description: The single-step redeem path gates withdrawal on req.requestedRound + leaveDelegatorsDelay and req.requestedRound + delegationBondLessDelay, both measured from the SAME original unstake-request round. With ProtocolConfig DELEGATOR_DELAY_ROUNDS=28 these two delays are equal, so both gates open simultaneously at requestedRound+28 (~7 days). The standard two-step exit is additive: scheduleDelegatorUnstake (round R) -> executeDelegatorUnstake matures at R+28 -> scheduleWithdraw sets a NEW requestedRound=currentRound>=R+28 -> executeWithdraw matures at R+56 (~14 days, DepositManager.sol:168,186). Thus executeDelegatorUnstakeAndWithdraw lets a delegator fully exit the protocol in ~7 days instead of ~14, halving the effective unbonding window during which misbehavior can still be caught and slashed (the pending-slash counter guard still applies, so this is a weakening of the time-based backstop, not a full bypass). If 14 days was the intended unbonding guarantee this is an economic-security regression; either the combined path should add both delays sequentially or the two-step path is over-delaying.

finding.title: REPRODUCED: [F-REPRO-001] Double exposure scaling in commitment slash path under-slashes 2x
finding.description:
REPRODUCED (reproduces F-COORD-001). End-to-end Foundry PoC on the real diamond harness (test/BaseTest.sol) confirms the commitment slash path applies the operator's committed exposure TWICE.

SETUP: operator1 has 1 ETH native self-stake; delegator1 delegates 10 ETH native (all-mode) => native pool exposed to slashing = 11 ETH. A service is created via requestServiceWithSecurity with one native AssetSecurityRequirement; service-level exposure is the default 100% (opData.exposureBps=10000). Operator approves committing exposureBps E=5000 (50%). Tangle-side serviceFeeDistributor disabled so _computeServiceCommitmentExposureBps takes the simple-average branch => commitmentBps = E = 5000 for the single asset.

ATTACK: service owner (user1) proposeSlash(serviceId, operator1, slashBps=10000 /*100%*/). Propose-time scaling (Slashing.sol:73-92 + SlashingLib.calculateEffectiveSlashBps) bakes exposure in ONCE: effectiveExposureBps = 10000*5000/1e4 = 5000; effectiveSlashBps = 10000*5000/1e4 = 5000. Asserted: p.effectiveSlashBps == 5000. After 7d dispute window, executeSlash routes to SlashingManager._slashForService (commitments.length==1), which re-applies commitment.exposureBps: effectiveBps = 5000*5000/1e4 = 2500.

OUTCOME (measured): pool before = 11e18; intended single-scaled (50%) = 5.5e18; actual removed = 2.75e18 (25%), exactly matching executeSlash() return value and the double-scaled formula E*E/1e4. Under-slash = 2.75e18 = exactly half the intended 5.5e18 penalty. The no-commitment fallback _slashForBlueprint would apply effectiveSlashBps (5000=50%) once with no re-scaling — confirming the commitment branch double-counts. Fix: pass raw cappedSlashBps into slashForService, or drop the per-commitment re-scale.

finding.title: [business-logic] Slash front-run: matured unstake races proposeSlash to exit at pre-slash rate
finding.description:
The only on-chain protection against delegators dodging a slash is the `_operatorPendingSlashCount` gate in `_scheduleDelegatorUnstake` (DelegationManagerLib.sol:424-427), `_executeDelegatorUnstake` (467-472) and `executeDelegatorUnstakeAndWithdraw` (StakingDelegationsFacet.sol:147-149). The code comments claim this "prevents delegators from front-running slash execution." It does not — the gate only becomes active AFTER the proposal transaction mines, because the count is incremented inside `proposeSlash` (src/core/Slashing.sol:103). Slashing is a two-phase commit (propose → dispute window → executeSlash), and `proposeSlash` is a public, mempool-visible transaction.

SETUP: A sophisticated or operator-colluding delegator schedules an unstake while `_operatorPendingSlashCount[op]==0` and lets it mature (currentRound >= requestedRound + delegationBondLessDelay). They keep this standing matured request open. If any unrelated slash is proposed and later resolved, `_decrementPendingSlash` returns the count to 0 and the standing request becomes executable again at the then-current rate, so the request survives indefinitely.

ATTACK: When the delegator observes a fresh `proposeSlash(op,...)` for their operator in the mempool (count still 0 on-chain), they submit `executeDelegatorUnstake()` / `executeDelegatorUnstakeAndWithdraw(...)` with a higher priority fee so it lands in the same block BEFORE the proposal. At execution `_operatorPendingSlashCount[op]` is still 0, so the guard passes and `amountToReturn = _sharesToAmount(req.shares)` (line 493 / facet 233) is computed at the FULL pre-slash exchange rate; tokens are returned to an arbitrary receiver.

OUTCOME: The delegator exits the operator's pool at the pre-slash rate, fully evading their share of the impending slash. Their shares and assets leave `_rewardPools[op][h]` before `_slashAllModePool` reduces `totalAssets`, so the slasher recovers less stake and the remaining (loyal) delegators are unaffected only because the exiting principal is simply gone. The documented anti-front-run guarantee is defeated for anyone holding a standing matured unstake request. Mitigations: freeze stake at the off-chain detection point / commit-reveal the proposal, or require unstake requests to snapshot a slash factor at schedule time and re-validate against any slash with requestedRound <= slashRound regardless of pending count.

finding.title: [business-logic] Fixed-mode slash escape: removeBlueprintFromDelegation moves stake out of a pending-slash target
finding.description:
The protocol blocks delegators from dodging a slash via the `_operatorPendingSlashCount` gate in `_scheduleDelegatorUnstake` (DelegationManagerLib.sol:424-427), `_executeDelegatorUnstake` (469-472) and `executeDelegatorUnstakeAndWithdraw` (StakingDelegationsFacet.sol:147-149). But `addBlueprintToDelegation`/`removeBlueprintFromDelegation` (StakingDelegationsFacet.sol:381,386 → DelegationManagerLib.sol:847,911) carry ONLY `whenNotPaused` — no pending-slash gate. Fixed-mode slashing is per-blueprint: `executeSlash` → `_executeSlashOnStaking(proposal, svc.blueprintId)` → `slashForBlueprint` → `_slashBlueprintPool(operator, blueprintId, ...)` (SlashingManager.sol:510-537) reduces ONLY that one blueprint pool's `totalAssets` at execute time. Whoever holds that pool at execute time bears the loss; rebalancing physically moves assets between pools via `_setDelegatorBlueprintPosition` (DelegationManagerLib.sol:311-346, `pool.totalAssets -= applied` on the removed bp, `+= delta` on the others, mirrored in `_operatorDelegatedAggregate`).

SETUP: Attacker holds a Fixed-mode delegation over blueprints [A,B], 1000 each (2000 total). Pools after delegate: A=[1e11 shares,1000 assets], B=[1e11,1000].
ATTACK: A service/blueprint owner calls `proposeSlash(serviceId, operator, 10000, evidence)` for blueprint A (Slashing.sol:30). This increments `_operatorPendingSlashCount` (Slashing.sol:103) and opens a dispute window before `executeSlash` is callable. The proposal is mempool-visible; even without front-running, the entire dispute window (>= MIN_DISPUTE_WINDOW) is available. The attacker calls `removeBlueprintFromDelegation(idx, A)`. `_removeBlueprintFromDelegation` computes totalAmount = s2a(A)+s2a(B)=2000, calls `_setDelegatorBlueprintPosition(A,0,0)` (A pool totalAssets 1000→0) and redistributes 2000 into B (B pool totalAssets 1000→2000). d.shares is recomputed to B's full position.
OUTCOME: When `executeSlash` runs `_slashBlueprintPool(A, 100%)`, pool A holds 0 assets — the slash collects nothing from the attacker. Attacker retains 2000 (verified by integer simulation of the exact a2s/s2a/setpos math) instead of the correct post-slash 1000. The slash is fully evaded; `_operatorPendingSlashCount` (the documented anti-front-run protection) does not cover this path. If other delegators remain in A, the fixed slash amount now falls entirely on them, amplifying their loss. The same gap lets `addBlueprintToDelegation` dilute exposure by pulling value into freshly added safe blueprints.

finding.title: [business-logic] Fixed-mode delegation accepts arbitrary/unregistered blueprintIds → permanently slash-immune stake
finding.description:
The Fixed-mode delegation path validates that `operator` is registered/active and that `blueprintIds` are non-empty and duplicate-free (DelegationManagerLib.sol:163-210), but NEVER validates that each `blueprintId` corresponds to a real, registered blueprint that the operator actually serves. The same is true of `addBlueprintToDelegation` (no existence check on the added id). Any `uint64` is accepted and lazily creates a fresh pool at `_blueprintPools[operator][blueprintId][assetHash]`.

SETUP / ATTACK: A delegator (optionally colluding with the operator) calls `delegateWithOptions(operator, token, amount, Fixed, [N])` where `N` is a blueprintId that no service references (e.g. 2^63). `_amountToSharesForBlueprint` on the empty pool mints `amount*VIRTUAL_SHARES` shares, `_increaseDelegatedStake(operator, assetHash, amount)` (via RewardsManager `_updateFixedModePools`, line 182) credits the operator's `_operatorDelegatedAggregate`, and `_getOperatorDelegatedStake` (DelegationManagerLib.sol:621) therefore counts this stake toward the operator's apparent delegated security.
OUTCOME: Slashing only ever targets `proposal.svc.blueprintId` via `_slashBlueprintPool` (SlashingManager.sol:510). Because no service maps to blueprintId `N`, that pool is never a slash target — the stake is permanently slash-immune yet still inflates the operator's reported delegated stake (and any reward/exposure logic keyed off the aggregate). Combined with the missing pending-slash guard finding, an attacker can pre-create an unregistered "safe-haven" blueprint and, on observing a `proposeSlash`, move funds into it via `addBlueprintToDelegation`, achieving a one-step permanent escape. Remediation: validate blueprintId against the registered/served-blueprint set in `_delegate` Fixed path and in `_addBlueprintToDelegation`.

finding.title: [business-logic] Lock-multiplier reward boost is permanent — never expires with the lock
finding.description:
The lock multiplier (up to 1.6x at 6 months, MULTIPLIER_SIX_MONTHS=16000) is meant to reward genuinely-locked stake, but the boost is baked into the rewards layer permanently and is NEVER re-evaluated when the lock expires.

CODE PATH: `_depositAsset` (DepositManager.sol:114-126) records a `LockInfo{amount, multiplier, expiryTimestamp}`. At delegate time, `_calculateLockMultiplierBps` (DelegationManagerLib.sol:776-807) reads `_getActiveLockTotals` (757-774), which correctly counts only unexpired locks, and `_delegate` (line 230) passes the resulting bps to `_onDelegationChanged` → `RewardsManager._notifyExternalRewardsManager` → `RewardVaults.recordDelegate(...,lockMultiplierBps)`. In recordDelegate (RewardVaults.sol:308,320,329) the boost is frozen into state: `score = amount*lockMultiplierBps/10000; debt.boostedScore += score; operatorPools[asset][operator].totalStaked += score`. Rewards then accrue CONTINUOUSLY off this frozen score forever via `_calculateDelegatorRewards` (RewardVaults.sol:735): `boostedScore * (accumulatedPerShare - lastAccumulatedPerShare) / PRECISION`. `recordUndelegate` (353) only reduces boostedScore proportionally to the AMOUNT withdrawn; NOTHING reduces it on lock expiry, and there is no keeper/re-record at expiry.

SETUP: Honest delegator H delegates 1000 unlocked → boostedScore 1000 (1.0x). Attacker A deposits 1000 with a 6-month lock and delegates → boostedScore 1600 (1.6x).
ATTACK: A keeps the position delegated. After 180 days the lock's `expiryTimestamp` passes; `_getActiveLockTotals` would now return 0, but the rewards layer never re-queries it. A's boostedScore stays 1600 indefinitely. A can keep funds delegated-but-unlocked (identical risk profile to H) yet keeps earning 1.6x.
OUTCOME: Because `accumulatedPerShare` divides each reward by total `totalStaked` (which still includes A's inflated 1600), A permanently siphons ~60% more of every distribution than an equal-risk unlocked delegator — an ongoing, unbounded drain of the shared reward pool from honest delegators, with the lock commitment served only once. Fix: store lockExpiry in the rewards layer and decay boostedScore back to base at expiry (e.g. require a re-record / lazily downgrade on claim).

finding.title: [business-logic] Slash strands deposit principal: residual delegatedAmount + currentDeposits permanently inflated
finding.description:
After a slash, a delegator's `dep.amount`, `dep.delegatedAmount` and the asset's `config.currentDeposits` are permanently left overstated, stranding the slashed principal and monotonically consuming deposit-cap headroom. (Note: I verified this does NOT enable over-withdrawal — the invariant dep.amount >= delegatedAmount and the depReduction cap preserve conservation — so it is a fund-stranding / availability bug, not theft.)

ROOT CAUSE: Slashing is purely virtual — `_slashAllModePool`/`_slashBlueprintPool` (SlashingManager.sol:482-502,510-537) only decrement `pool.totalAssets`; no tokens are transferred out, and `dep.amount`, `dep.delegatedAmount`, `config.currentDeposits` are never touched. On undelegate, `_executeDelegatorUnstake` (DelegationManagerLib.sol:514-519) computes `amountToReturn = _sharesToAmount(req.shares)` at the POST-slash (reduced) rate and reduces delegatedAmount by `depReduction = min(amountToReturn, dep.delegatedAmount)`. The same capped pattern is in the facet (`_applyBondlessUnstakeToDelegatorState`, StakingDelegationsFacet.sol:289-292).

SETUP: User deposits 100 native (dep.amount=100, currentDeposits=100, balance=100), delegates 100 (delegatedAmount=100, pool 100sh/100assets).
ATTACK/EVENT: Operator slashed 50% → pool.totalAssets 100→50. dep.amount/delegatedAmount/currentDeposits unchanged; 50 tokens remain in the contract (orphaned). User fully undelegates: shares=100, amountToReturn=_sharesToAmount(100)=50, depReduction=min(50,100)=50 → delegatedAmount=50 RESIDUAL while the delegation position (d.shares) is burned to 0 and removed. dep.amount stays 100, so available = 100-50 = 50. User withdraws 50 (dep.amount→50, currentDeposits→50).
OUTCOME: A residual dep.amount=50 with delegatedAmount=50 is left with available=0 and NO delegation to ever undelegate again → permanently unwithdrawable (the slashed 50, now orphaned tokens stuck in the contract). currentDeposits permanently counts this dead 50. Over repeated slashes this dead value accumulates against `config.depositCap` (checked in DepositManager.sol:103) and eventually bricks new deposits for the asset (DepositCapExceeded) even though live withdrawable TVL is far below the cap. Fix: decrement dep.amount/delegatedAmount/currentDeposits by the realized slash, or reconcile residual delegatedAmount to true post-slash value when the last share is burned.

finding.title: [business-logic] Fixed-mode slash escape: removeBlueprintFromDelegation moves stake out of a pending-slash target
finding.description: SETUP: Fixed-mode blueprint rebalancing (_addBlueprintToDelegation / _removeBlueprintFromDelegation) carries only whenNotPaused and lacks the _operatorPendingSlashCount pending-slash gate that guards _scheduleDelegatorUnstake (424-427), _executeDelegatorUnstake (469-472) and executeDelegatorUnstakeAndWithdraw (facet 147-149). ATTACK: After proposeSlash increments the operator's pending count and opens the dispute window, a delegator calls removeBlueprintFromDelegation(idx, A) to physically move totalAssets out of the to-be-slashed blueprint pool A into the surviving pools via _setDelegatorBlueprintPosition. OUTCOME: executeSlash → _slashBlueprintPool(A) collects nothing; the slash is evaded (or, via addBlueprintToDelegation, diluted into a safe blueprint). FIX: gate both rebalancing functions on _operatorPendingSlashCount[d.operator] > 0, reverting DelegationErrors.PendingSlashExists(d.operator, count) — identical style/placement to the unstake-path guards, inserted right after the Fixed-mode check where d.operator is in scope.

remediation.summary: Add the existing pending-slash gate to both Fixed-mode rebalancing functions. _addBlueprintToDelegation and _removeBlueprintFromDelegation now read _operatorPendingSlashCount[d.operator] immediately after the Fixed-mode check and revert DelegationErrors.PendingSlashExists(d.operator, count) when it is > 0, so a delegator can no longer shift stake between blueprint pools while a slash is pending on the operator. This mirrors the guard already present in _scheduleDelegatorUnstake / _executeDelegatorUnstake / executeDelegatorUnstakeAndWithdraw, closing the rebalancing path that was the only un-gated way to drain a to-be-slashed blueprint pool during the dispute window.
remediation.tradeoffs: Rebalancing is now blocked for the full dispute window of any pending slash on the operator (including slashes unrelated to the delegator's blueprints), so honest Fixed-mode delegators cannot add/remove blueprints until the proposal resolves — the same availability tradeoff already accepted on the unstake paths. SECONDARY RECOMMENDATION (not implemented here, tracked as finding sub-2-deep-audit-0430a9ea-f2d): validate blueprintId in _addBlueprintToDelegation (and the Fixed delegate path) against the operator's registered/served blueprint set, so an attacker cannot pre-create an unregistered, permanently slash-immune 'safe-haven' pool to escape into. The pending-slash gate alone stops the during-dispute-window move but does not prevent staging slash-immune pools ahead of time.

finding.title: [business-logic] Uncapped exposureBps in RFQ quote path lets operator inflate payment share, stealing from co-operators
finding.description: SETUP: createServiceFromQuotes activates an RFQ service. _processOperatorQuotes (QuotesCreate.sol:285-288) stores operator-signed exposure via _quoteExposure()=commitments[0].exposureBps (line 313) into _serviceOperators[serviceId][op].exposureBps. Unlike the request path which rejects exposures[i]>BPS_DENOMINATOR (ServicesRequests.sol:275), the quote path applies NO upper bound (exposureBps is uint16, up to 65535). The quote path also never persists AssetSecurityCommitment[] into _serviceSecurityCommitments, so _calculateEffectiveExposures (PaymentsEffectiveExposure.sol) returns 0 and _calculateEffectiveExposuresWithFallback (PaymentsDistribution.sol:213-224) falls back to _calculateSimpleExposures, weighting the operator pool by raw stored exposureBps. ATTACK: a malicious operator signs a quote with low totalCost (to look cheap and be bundled) but exposureBps=65535 while honest co-operators use 10000. distributePayment splits the operator pool by exposureBps, giving the attacker 65535/(65535+N*10000) regardless of the small price they quoted. OUTCOME: attacker captures a disproportionate share of pooled operator payment, draining honest co-operators; the [0,10000] exposure invariant is violated. FIX: enforce exposureBps<=BPS_DENOMINATOR in the quote path mirroring ServicesRequests.sol:275, and persist signed commitments.

finding.title: [business-logic] createServiceFromQuotes bypasses min/max operator quorum bounds
finding.description: SETUP: the standard request path validates operator count via _validateOperatorBounds (ServicesRequests.sol:295-297) reverting InsufficientOperators when operatorCount<minOperators and clamping/checking maxOperators. ATTACK: createServiceFromQuotes -> _activateQuoteService (QuotesCreate.sol:188) writes the Service struct (lines 203-216) storing minOperators (211) and maxOperators (212) but NEVER checks operators.length against either bound; no _validateOperatorBounds call exists in the quote path. A requester supplies a single operator-signed quote and activates a service whose blueprint requires e.g. minOperators=5. OUTCOME: a service mandated to run with N operators for fault tolerance/slashing collateral/quorum becomes Active with as few as 1 operator, silently degrading guarantees that slashing, aggregation, and heartbeat assume; maxOperators and the protocol ceiling are likewise unenforced, allowing oversized operator sets that inflate per-operator loops. FIX: call the request-path operator-bounds validation before quote activation.

finding.title: [signature-replay] QuoteDetails lacks flow/serviceId discriminator: create-quote steerable into extend and vice-versa
finding.description: SETUP: createServiceFromQuotes and extendServiceFromQuotes both verify QuoteDetails through the same SignatureLib.verifyQuoteBatch, same QUOTE_TYPEHASH, same domain separator, same _usedQuotes map. QUOTE_TYPEHASH (SignatureLib.sol:30-37) binds requester/blueprintId/ttlBlocks/totalCost/timestamp/expiry/confidentiality/commitments but has NO flow-intent field and NO serviceId. ATTACK: an operator signs a quote intending to join a NEW blueprint-X service (requester=Alice, blueprintId=X, ttlBlocks=T, totalCost=C). Because the digest is identical for both flows and extend only gates on requester==msg.sender and the operator already being active in the target service, Alice can consume that quote via extendServiceFromQuotes on an existing blueprint-X service the operator is in (or steer an extend-quote into creation). Single-use digest marking prevents double-spend, limiting impact to redirecting the operator's economic commitment between two undistinguished flows. OUTCOME: an operator quote can be applied to a lifecycle action it did not intend; the signature pins neither the flow nor the target serviceId. FIX: add a flow tag (and target serviceId for extend) to the signed type.

finding.title: [business-logic] Uncapped exposureBps in RFQ quote path lets operator inflate payout share
finding.description:
SETUP: createServiceFromQuotes activates an RFQ service. _processOperatorQuotes (QuotesCreate.sol:285-288) stores operator-signed exposure via _quoteExposure()=commitments[0].exposureBps (line 313) into _serviceOperators[serviceId][op].exposureBps. Unlike the request path which rejects exposures[i]>BPS_DENOMINATOR (ServicesRequests.sol:275), the quote path applies NO upper bound — exposureBps is uint16, up to 65535. The quote path also never persists AssetSecurityCommitment[] into _serviceSecurityCommitments, so at distribution _calculateEffectiveExposures (PaymentsEffectiveExposure.sol) reads an empty set, returns 0, and _calculateEffectiveExposuresWithFallback (PaymentsDistribution.sol:213-224) falls back to _calculateSimpleExposures which weights the operator pool by the raw stored exposureBps.
ATTACK: a malicious operator signs an RFQ quote with a low totalCost (to look cheap and get bundled) but exposureBps=65535, while honest co-operators sign 10000. The requester pays sum(totalCost); distributePayment splits the operator pool by exposureBps, awarding the attacker 65535/(65535+N*10000) of the pool regardless of the small price they themselves quoted.
OUTCOME: the attacker captures a disproportionate share of the pooled operator payment, draining value from honest co-operators who receive less than they quoted; the [0,10000] exposure invariant assumed everywhere else is violated. FIX: enforce exposureBps<=BPS_DENOMINATOR in the quote path (mirror ServicesRequests.sol:275) and persist the signed commitments.

finding.title: [business-logic] createServiceFromQuotes bypasses min/max operator quorum bounds
finding.description:
SETUP: the standard service-request path validates operator count via _validateOperatorBounds (ServicesRequests.sol:295-297), reverting InsufficientOperators when operatorCount<minOperators and clamping/checking maxOperators against the protocol ceiling.
ATTACK: createServiceFromQuotes -> _activateQuoteService (QuotesCreate.sol:188) writes the Service struct (lines 203-216) storing minOperators (line 211) and maxOperators (line 212) but NEVER checks operators.length against either bound — no _validateOperatorBounds (or equivalent) call exists anywhere in the quote path. A requester assembles a single operator-signed quote and activates a service whose blueprint requires e.g. minOperators=5.
OUTCOME: a service the blueprint mandates be backed by N operators (for fault tolerance, slashing collateral, quorum) becomes Active with as few as 1 operator, silently degrading the security/redundancy guarantees slashing, job aggregation, and heartbeat all assume. maxOperators and the protocol ceiling are likewise unenforced, permitting oversized operator sets that inflate every per-operator loop in bill/distribute/terminate. FIX: invoke the same operator-bounds validation the request path uses before quote activation.

finding.title: [signature-replay] QuoteDetails lacks flow/serviceId discriminator: create-quote steerable into extend
finding.description:
SETUP: createServiceFromQuotes (_verifyQuotesAndGetCost) and extendServiceFromQuotes (_verifyExtensionQuotes) both verify QuoteDetails through the same SignatureLib.verifyQuoteBatch, with the same QUOTE_TYPEHASH, same domain separator, and same _usedQuotes mapping. QUOTE_TYPEHASH (SignatureLib.sol:30-37) binds requester/blueprintId/ttlBlocks/totalCost/timestamp/expiry/confidentiality/commitments but carries NO field indicating intent (create vs extend) and NO serviceId.
ATTACK: an operator signs a quote intending to join a NEW blueprint-X service (requester=Alice, blueprintId=X, ttlBlocks=T, totalCost=C). Because the digest is identical across both flows and extend only gates on requester==msg.sender plus the operator already being active in the target service, Alice (owner of an existing blueprint-X service the operator is in) can instead consume that quote via extendServiceFromQuotes to extend her existing service — or steer an extend-intended quote into creation. Single-use digest marking prevents double-spend, so impact is limited to redirecting the operator's economic commitment between two flows the signature does not distinguish.
OUTCOME: an operator's quote can be applied to a service-lifecycle action it did not intend; the signature pins neither the consuming flow nor the target serviceId. FIX: add a flow tag (and, for extend, the target serviceId) to the signed QuoteDetails type.

finding.title: [input-validation] joinService accepts exposureBps > 10000 → reward-weight inflation & slash-scaling distortion
finding.description:
SETUP: `joinService(serviceId, exposureBps)` and `joinServiceWithCommitments(serviceId, exposureBps, commitments)` let an operator self-select the `exposureBps` stored on their `ServiceOperator` record (`_finalizeJoin`, ServicesLifecycle.sol:667-669). Unlike the request path — `ServicesRequests._validateRequestOperators` rejects `exposures[i] > BPS_DENOMINATOR` (ServicesRequests.sol:275) — NEITHER join entry point bounds `exposureBps`. A `uint16` admits values up to 65535 (6.55×).

ATTACK / IMPACT:
1. Reward-weight inflation (no-requirement Dynamic services, joined via `joinService`): the subscription billing weight uses this value directly — `_accrueOperatorWeights` fallback path computes `contribution = opDeltaRaw * fallbackBps / 10000` with `fallbackBps = ServiceOperator.exposureBps` (PaymentsBilling.sol:293-295), and non-subscription distributions use it via `_calculateSimpleExposures` (weights = exposureBps). An operator joining with `exposureBps = 65535` earns up to 6.55× the operator-pool share per unit of stake-seconds versus an honest operator capped at 10000, diluting honest operators' rewards from the same customer bill. The split is by weight, so this is direct reward-share theft among operators.
2. Slash-scaling distortion / batch-DoS (all services): `Slashing.proposeSlash` reads `opData.exposureBps` into `effectiveExposureBps` (Slashing.sol:73) and derives `effectiveSlashBps = slashBps * effectiveExposureBps / 10000`. With `exposureBps` up to 65535, `effectiveSlashBps` can exceed 10000; at execution `SlashingManager._slashForBlueprint/_slashForService` revert with `InvalidSlashBps` when the bps argument > 10000. A single such operator in an `executeSlashBatch(slashIds)` (no try/catch around the staking call) reverts the whole batch, a griefing/DoS vector against batched slash execution.

OUTCOME: An operator-controlled, unvalidated parameter corrupts reward-distribution weighting and the slash-bps domain. Pre-mainnet, both break invariants the rest of the code assumes (`exposureBps ∈ [0,10000]`).

FIX: In both join entry points, `if (exposureBps == 0 || exposureBps > BPS_DENOMINATOR) revert`, mirroring the request-side validation.

finding.title: [business-logic] Staker-share refund credits non-subscription escrow → service owner steals delegators' pool via withdrawRemainingEscrow
finding.description:
SETUP: EventDriven and native-token PayOnce services route their job payments through the SAME `_distributeBill` core as subscriptions. `TangleJobsFacet._distributeJobPayment` (TangleJobsFacet.sol:29-30) and the PayOnce path (TangleServicesFacet.sol:192-193) call `distributePayment(serviceId, blueprintId, address(0), payment, operators)` — token is always native `address(0)`. These pricing models NEVER set `_serviceEscrows[serviceId].token` (only the Subscription branch at TangleServicesFacet.sol:167-170 does), so the escrow struct stays all-zero, including `escrow.token == address(0)`.

When operators have real delegated stake, `_distributeBill` computes `hasSecurityCommitments == true` so a non-zero `stakerPool` is split out (PaymentsDistribution.sol:292-294) and each operator's slice is sent to `_forwardStakerShare`. If `_serviceFeeDistributor == address(0)` (an explicitly-handled normal mode — line 367; realistic in early deployment) OR the distributor reverts for that (op, token), control reaches `_refundStakerShareToEscrow` (lines 400-421).

ATTACK: `_refundStakerShareToEscrow` tests `if (escrow.token != token)` (line 410). For a native job payment, `escrow.token(0) == token(0)`, so it falls through and executes `escrow.balance += amount` (line 414). For an EventDriven/PayOnce service there was no deposit and no `releaseFromEscrow`, so `totalDeposited == 0` and `totalReleased == 0`; the `totalReleased -= amount` guard (415-419) just clamps to 0. Net: `escrow.balance` is credited stakerShare with NOTHING deposited — the invariant `balance == totalDeposited - totalReleased` is broken (balance = s, deposited = 0). Every job over the service's life accrues the entire staker allocation (stakerBps of each job fee, paid by arbitrary job submitters) into this phantom escrow balance. After the service is terminated, the SERVICE OWNER calls `withdrawRemainingEscrow(serviceId)` (PaymentsRefund.sol:35-53 — guarded only by `status==Terminated` + `owner==msg.sender`, NO subscription/pricing check) and pockets the full accumulated balance.

OUTCOME: funds earmarked for delegators (the staker pool) are silently diverted to the service owner — cross-account theft from stakers, plus an escrow accounting invariant break that lets an owner withdraw value never deposited. FIX: gate `_refundStakerShareToEscrow`'s escrow credit on the service actually being a Subscription with an initialized escrow (e.g. require `escrow.totalDeposited > 0` or `svc.pricing == Subscription`); otherwise route the un-routable staker share to the treasury (the `escrow.token != token` branch already does this).

finding.title: [signature-scope] RFQ job quote omits job inputs from signed digest → operator bound to arbitrary work at a fixed price
finding.description:
SETUP: `JOB_QUOTE_TYPEHASH` (SignatureLib.sol:42-44) and `hashJobQuote` (185-200) sign only `(requester, serviceId, jobIndex, price, timestamp, expiry, confidentiality)`. The job `inputs` are NOT part of the signed digest. In `JobsRFQ.submitJobFromQuote`, inputs are validated against the blueprint job schema (JobsRFQ.sol:81) and stored, but quote verification (`_verifyQuotesAndRecordOperators` → `SignatureLib.verifyAndMarkJobQuoteUsed`, JobsRFQ.sol:177-184) never binds them. Job schemas permit variable-magnitude / variable-length params (`SchemaLib.validateJobParams`), so the real cost of fulfilling a given `jobIndex` depends on the inputs.

ATTACK: A requester asks operator O off-chain for a quote on `(serviceId S, jobIndex J)` presenting a trivial input; O signs `price = P`. The requester then calls `submitJobFromQuote(S, J, HEAVY_INPUTS, [quote])` supplying a far more expensive but still schema-valid input (e.g. a large array / high iteration count). The signature still recovers (inputs not covered), the digest is fresh and unused, and the job is recorded at price P. Per `JobsSubmission._validateResultSubmissionState` (`job.isRFQ && !_jobQuotedOperators…contains(msg.sender)` → revert), only the quoted operator O may submit results for that RFQ job.

OUTCOME: Operator O is economically bound to perform arbitrary, attacker-chosen work for a price quoted against different (cheaper) inputs — the cryptographic commitment diverges from what the operator agreed to. Repeatable per job, this lets a requester systematically extract under-priced work from operators. FIX: include `keccak256(inputs)` in `JobQuoteDetails`/`JOB_QUOTE_TYPEHASH` and require it to equal the submitted inputs in `submitJobFromQuote`.

finding.title: Unauthenticated GovernanceDeployer functions allow Timelock admin takeover during deploy
finding.description: GovernanceDeployer.transferTimelockAdmin / renounceTimelockAdmin / configureProtocolRoles / transferFullControl are all external with NO access control. SETUP: deployGovernance() initializes TangleTimelock with admin=address(this) (GovernanceDeployer.sol:98), so the deployer contract (gd) holds DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE on the timelock. DeployGovernance.s.sol:80-82 calls deployGovernance() then renounceTimelockAdmin() as TWO SEPARATE transactions. ATTACK: in the window between them any address calls gd.transferTimelockAdmin(timelock, attacker) (lines 165-168) which grants DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE to an attacker-chosen address and renounces gd. Because OZ renounceRole() does not revert when the role is absent (AccessControlUpgradeable.sol:178-184), the scripts later renounceTimelockAdmin() and its post-check require(!hasRole(DEFAULT_ADMIN,gd)) still PASS, so the takeover is silent. OUTCOME: attacker holds DEFAULT_ADMIN on the Timelock, self-grants PROPOSER/EXECUTOR, and immediately executes arbitrary operations. The Timelock is intended to hold UPGRADER_ROLE/ADMIN_ROLE on Tangle core and MultiAssetDelegation, so this is full protocol takeover bypassing every governance vote and delay. The same missing-auth defect lets anyone redirect grantRole targets via configureProtocolRoles/transferFullControl (grantee is a caller-controlled param) whenever gd transiently holds admin on a protocol contract.

finding.title: [business-logic] getOrCreateLock beneficiary-gate bricks the batch lockup distribution flow
finding.description:
SETUP: `getOrCreateLock` was hardened with `if (msg.sender != beneficiary) revert NotBeneficiary(...)` (TNTLockFactory.sol:52) to close a real delegate-front-run (a third party seeding `_delegates[lock]=attacker`). That gate is correct for the "airdrop-to-predicted-address, recipient self-creates" pattern. BUT the only on-chain consumer, `DistributeTNTWithLockup.run()`, uses the opposite pattern: a single broadcasting `deployer` (PRIVATE_KEY) loops over many recipients and calls `factory.getOrCreateLock(token, t.to, unlockTimestamp, t.to)` (DistributeTNTWithLockup.s.sol:121), then transfers the locked tranche to the returned lock (line 122).

TRIGGER: For that call `msg.sender == deployer` while the `beneficiary` argument is the recipient `t.to`. In a batch distribution the deployer is never equal to the recipients, so the gate at line 52 reverts on the first recipient with `lockedAmount > 0` (i.e. any `unlockedBps < 10000`). Because the whole `run()` broadcast is atomic, the already-executed unlocked transfer at line 117 is rolled back too — the entire vesting distribution reverts.

OUTCOME: The locked/vesting distribution path is non-functional as integrated: TNT cannot be locked on behalf of beneficiaries through the factory+script. No funds are lost and no attacker is involved (availability/correctness defect, not theft), but the governance lockup mechanism is unusable until either the gate is loosened (which reopens the delegate-front-run it was added to fix) or the script is rewritten so each beneficiary self-creates their lock. The tension is intrinsic: the same restriction that blocks attacker-chosen delegatees also blocks a trusted distributor from provisioning locks for third parties.

finding.title: [access-control] TNTCliffLock clone master left initializable → stray delegate/withdraw on impl
finding.description:
SETUP: TNTLockFactory deploys one TNTCliffLock as the EIP-1167 implementation master (TNTLockFactory.sol:18, `new TNTCliffLock()`). TNTCliffLock is a plain contract guarding re-init with a bare `bool initialized` (line 28) rather than OZ Initializable; the master is never initialized and there is no constructor calling `_disableInitializers()`/setting `initialized=true`.

ATTACK: Anyone calls `implementation.initialize(realTNT, attacker, ts, attacker)` directly on the master address (line 35 has no caller restriction, only the `initialized` flag which is false on the master). This sets the master's `beneficiary=attacker` and executes `IVotes(realTNT).delegate(attacker)` *from the master address*, writing `_delegates[implementation]=attacker` in the live TNT token. The attacker is now `onlyBeneficiary` of the master and can call `implementation.withdraw(attacker)` / `implementation.delegate(...)`.

OUTCOME: Limited. EIP-1167 clones keep their own storage, so initializing the master does NOT affect any beneficiary's lock (clones are unaffected — confirmed: clone storage is independent). Impact is confined to assets/voting-units that happen to sit at the master address itself, which no flow funds, so real loss requires someone mistakenly sending TNT to the implementation. Still a defect: the clone master should be rendered non-initializable (constructor setting `initialized=true` or `_disableInitializers`) so its `initialize` cannot be hijacked. Reported as low because no production flow routes value to the master.

finding.title: [business-logic] setAuditorActive(false) leaves weight>0, breaking advertised "inactive ⇒ weight 0" invariant
finding.description:
SETUP: The contract advertises and partially enforces the invariant "removed ⇒ weight 0": `removeAuditor` zeroes weight (line 171) and `setAuditorWeight` reverts unless `active` (line 193) precisely so "the invariant 'removed ⇒ weight 0' cannot be silently violated" (NatSpec, lines 186-188). Off-chain aggregators are documented to honor `active` + `weight` together.

GAP: `setAuditorActive(auditor, false)` (lines 178-183) flips `active=false` but does NOT touch `weight`. So governance pausing an auditor via this path yields the state `active=false, weight=500` — an inactive row carrying live weight, exactly the state the weight-set guard was written to prevent. The test suite confirms it: `test_setActive_togglesAndEmits` deactivates a weight-100 auditor and asserts only `isActiveAuditor==false`, never that weight was cleared.

OUTCOME: Two concrete consequences. (1) Any aggregator that sums `auditorWeight` across `_auditorList` for normalization without also checking `isActiveAuditor` counts the paused auditor's stale weight, diluting/skewing the normalized influence of every active auditor. (2) Resurrection is asymmetric and surprising: `setAuditorActive(false)→setAuditorActive(true)` silently restores the old weight, whereas `removeAuditor→setAuditorActive(true)` forces an explicit re-weight (test `test_remove_thenSetActiveTrue_resurrectsButWeightStaysZero`). Governance-gated, and the canonical aggregator that filters by `active` is safe, so impact is low — but the contract claims an invariant it does not fully hold. Fix: zero weight on `setAuditorActive(false)`, or document that inactive rows may retain weight and require consumers to AND with `active`.

finding.title: getOrCreateLock access control breaks genesis lockup distribution (DoS)
finding.description: getOrCreateLock requires msg.sender==beneficiary, but its only intended caller, the genesis distributor DistributeTNTWithLockup.s.sol:121, invokes it as the DEPLOYER on behalf of each recipient: factory.getOrCreateLock(token, t.to, unlockTimestamp, t.to) under vm.startBroadcast(deployerKey). Since deployer != t.to, the call reverts NotBeneficiary(deployer, t.to) for EVERY recipient. A forge-script revert aborts the whole run(), so the entire locked distribution (the 90%-locked genesis tranche) cannot execute. Confirmed by Foundry PoC test_distributionFlow_alwaysReverts (PASS). The natural ops fix - removing the beneficiary guard - directly reintroduces the delegation hijack the contract comment (lines 35-42) claims to mitigate (see F-002). The factory cannot simultaneously be batch-distributable and hijack-safe under this design.

finding.title: Delegation front-running hijack via predictable lock address (latent / design-fragile)
finding.description: The lock address is deterministic in (token,beneficiary,unlockTimestamp) and exposed via public predictLockAddress, explicitly supporting a fund-before-create (lazy) airdrop model. TNTCliffLock.initialize calls IVotes(token).delegate(delegatee) from the lock address; OZ Votes._delegate sets _delegatee[lock]=delegatee with no auth. In the lazy model a third party who creates the lock first picks the delegatee, so once the airdrop funds the predictable address ERC20Votes credits the attacker with the beneficiary voting power until the beneficiary re-delegates. The only thing preventing this is the msg.sender==beneficiary guard in getOrCreateLock - the same guard that breaks the batch distributor (F-001). Currently mitigated (guard present), but the design forces a choice between a working distributor and hijack safety. Safer fix: bind delegatee into the CREATE2 salt, or ignore the init-time delegatee and require the beneficiary to delegate after funding, decoupling creation authority from delegation authority.

finding.title: Clone implementation left uninitialized; initialize has no access control
finding.description: TNTLockFactory deploys the implementation via new TNTCliffLock() and never initializes/locks it; TNTCliffLock has no _disableInitializers equivalent and initialize is external, gated only by the one-shot initialized flag. Anyone can call implementation.initialize(...) to become its beneficiary and drive delegate()/withdraw() on the implementation address. Impact is low: the implementation holds no funds and EIP-1167 clones use separate storage (clones unaffected), but any TNT mistakenly sent to the well-known implementation address is seized by the squatter, and it deviates from standard clone-hardening. Confirmed by PoC test_implementationIsInitializableByAnyone (PASS).

finding.title: [eip712] QUOTE_TYPEHASH referenced-type order violates EIP-712 → standard wallet signatures fail to verify
finding.description:
SETUP: QUOTE_TYPEHASH is computed over the encodeType string:
"QuoteDetails(...)AssetSecurityCommitment(Asset asset,uint16 exposureBps)Asset(uint8 kind,address token)ResourceCommitment(uint8 kind,uint64 count)".

EIP-712 (§"Definition of encodeType") mandates that the set of referenced struct types be **sorted by name** and appended after the primary type. The correct sort of {Asset, AssetSecurityCommitment, ResourceCommitment} is alphabetical: "Asset" < "AssetSecurityCommitment" < "ResourceCommitment". This contract instead emits AssetSecurityCommitment BEFORE Asset.

ATTACK / IMPACT: Any spec-compliant signer — ethers `signTypedData` / viem / MetaMask `eth_signTypedData_v4` — auto-sorts referenced types and therefore produces a signature over the CORRECT typehash (Asset first). The contract recovers against its WRONG typehash, so `digest.recover(sig) != operator` in verifyQuoteBatch (SignatureLib.sol:160) and every `createServiceFromQuotes` / `extendServiceFromQuotes` call reverts with InvalidQuoteSignature. The RFQ service-creation/extension flow is unusable with standard EIP-712 tooling; operators are forced onto a bespoke, non-compliant signer that replicates the bug. Conversely, a wallet visually attests to a different struct hash than the one the contract checks. Note JOB_QUOTE_TYPEHASH (line 42) has no nested types and is unaffected — so the job-RFQ path works while the quote path silently does not, masking the defect in partial testing.

OUTCOME: Availability/interop break of the quote path (signatures cannot be produced by compliant wallets); a latent integrity gap between what a wallet displays and what is verified. Fix: reorder to Asset(...)AssetSecurityCommitment(...)ResourceCommitment(...).

finding.title: [business-logic] Job quote price not bound to job inputs → caller redeems quoted price for arbitrary, costlier work
finding.description:
SETUP: JobQuoteDetails commits the operator only to {requester, serviceId, jobIndex, price, timestamp, expiry, confidentiality} (hashJobQuote, SignatureLib.sol:181-196). It does NOT include the job `inputs`. In JobsRFQ.submitJobFromQuote the caller-supplied `inputs` (JobsRFQ.sol:49,117) are merely schema-validated (SchemaLib.validateJobParams, line 81) and stored — never hashed into the verified digest (confirmed: no keccak of inputs anywhere in JobsRFQ).

ATTACK: An operator gossips a signed quote pricing a given (serviceId, jobIndex) at `price` P, implicitly for a small/cheap input it expects. A permitted caller (requester == msg.sender) submits the quote via submitJobFromQuote but supplies the most expensive inputs the schema for that jobIndex admits (e.g. large array length / high iteration count / max-size payload). The signature still recovers (inputs aren't in the digest), the quote is accepted, and the operator is obligated to perform the heavy computation while being paid only the quoted P.

OUTCOME: For any blueprint whose per-job cost is input-dependent at a fixed jobIndex, the requester extracts unbounded work at a fixed price — a systematic economic griefing / underpayment vector against operators. Severity scales with how input-sensitive the blueprint's job cost is; confidence is moderate because blueprints with flat per-jobIndex cost are unaffected. Fix: include a keccak256 of `inputs` (or a caller-chosen input commitment) in JOB_QUOTE_TYPEHASH and verify it against the submitted inputs.

finding.title: [signature-replay] QuoteDetails digest omits serviceId/intent → create-quote redeemable as extend-quote and across same-owner services
finding.description:
SETUP: hashQuote binds {requester, blueprintId, ttlBlocks, totalCost, timestamp, expiry, confidentiality, securityCommitments, resourceCommitments} but NOT a serviceId nor any create-vs-extend discriminator. Both createServiceFromQuotes (QuotesCreate.sol:_verifyQuotesAndGetCost) and extendServiceFromQuotes (QuotesExtend.sol:_verifyExtensionQuotes) call the same SignatureLib.verifyQuoteBatch over the same `_usedQuotes` map with (blueprintId, ttl=additionalTtl_or_serviceTtl, expectedRequester=msg.sender). A digest produced for one flow is therefore equally valid in the other whenever blueprintId, ttl and requester coincide.

ATTACK: (a) Cross-intent: an operator signs a quote intending to EXTEND a service it already runs (low marginal commitment, additionalTtl T). The same owner instead feeds that signature to createServiceFromQuotes(blueprintId, [quote], ttl=T): the operator is registered on the blueprint (_ensureOperatorsRegistered passes) and is enrolled into a BRAND-NEW service with the signed exposureBps/resources and capital-at-risk it did not intend to take on. (b) Cross-service: with multiple same-blueprint services under one owner, an extension quote meant for service A can be applied to service B (operator active in B passes _verifyQuoteOperatorsInService). usedQuotes still prevents double-redemption, so this is misdirection of a single signature, not a double-spend.

OUTCOME: An operator's signature is honored for a target/flow it did not authorize, committing it to exposure on an unintended service. Bounded by single-use (no fund duplication) and by the operator already being blueprint-registered, hence low severity. Fix: add `serviceId` (and a create/extend domain tag) to QuoteDetails / the typed data.

finding.title: Non-canonical EIP-712 type ordering in QUOTE_TYPEHASH → incompatible with standard signers
finding.description:
SETUP: EIP-712 (§"Definition of encodeType") requires that when a struct references other struct types, the referenced types are collected, **sorted by name**, and appended to the primary type string. `QuoteDetails` references `Asset`, `AssetSecurityCommitment`, and `ResourceCommitment`. Canonical alphabetical order is `Asset` < `AssetSecurityCommitment` < `ResourceCommitment` (`Asset` is a proper prefix of `AssetSecurityCommitment`, so it sorts first).

ATTACK/DEFECT: The QUOTE_TYPEHASH string at line 36 orders the referenced types as `...)AssetSecurityCommitment(Asset asset,uint16 exposureBps)Asset(uint8 kind,address token)ResourceCommitment(uint8 kind,uint64 count)` — i.e. `AssetSecurityCommitment` BEFORE `Asset`. This is non-canonical. Any operator signing with a spec-compliant EIP-712 library (MetaMask `eth_signTypedData_v4`, ethers `signTypedData`, viem) computes the typehash with the correct sorted order, producing a DIFFERENT `QuoteDetails` typehash → different struct hash → different digest. `hashQuote`/`computeQuoteDigest` (lines 84-103) then recover a different address than `quote.operator`, so `verifyQuoteBatch`/`verifyAndMarkQuoteUsed` revert with InvalidQuoteSignature.

OUTCOME: The RFQ service-creation (`createServiceFromQuotes`) and extension (`extendServiceFromQuotes`) flows only accept signatures from a bespoke signer that replicates this exact non-canonical string (as `test/Integration.t.sol::_signQuote` does). Standard wallet/SDK tooling cannot produce valid quotes — a functional/interop defect (feature unusable with compliant signers), not a fund-loss bug. The contract is internally self-consistent (sign-side and verify-side use the same constant), so there is no replay or forgery risk. JOB_QUOTE_TYPEHASH (line 43) has no nested types and is unaffected.

FIX: Reorder to `QuoteDetails(...)Asset(uint8 kind,address token)AssetSecurityCommitment(Asset asset,uint16 exposureBps)ResourceCommitment(uint8 kind,uint64 count)`.

finding.title: [EIP-712] QUOTE_TYPEHASH referenced-type order violates spec → standard signers fail
finding.description:
SETUP: QUOTE_TYPEHASH (line 36) hardcodes the EIP-712 encodeType string for QuoteDetails. EIP-712 mandates that referenced struct types be collected and **sorted by name**, then appended. The three referenced types are `Asset`, `AssetSecurityCommitment`, `ResourceCommitment`. Lexicographic order: `Asset` < `AssetSecurityCommitment` < `ResourceCommitment` (the shorter string `Asset` is a prefix of `AssetSecurityCommitment`, so it sorts first).

ATTACK / DEFECT: The constant orders them `...resourceCommitments)AssetSecurityCommitment(Asset asset,uint16 exposureBps)Asset(uint8 kind,address token)ResourceCommitment(...)` — i.e. `AssetSecurityCommitment` is placed BEFORE `Asset`. Spec-correct ordering is `Asset(uint8 kind,address token)AssetSecurityCommitment(Asset asset,uint16 exposureBps)ResourceCommitment(...)`. Because the type string is wrong, keccak256(QUOTE_TYPEHASH string) differs from the typeHash any spec-compliant signer computes (ethers `TypedDataEncoder`, viem, MetaMask `eth_signTypedData_v4` all sort referenced types by name). hashQuote() (line 87) feeds this wrong typehash into the struct hash, so computeQuoteDigest() yields a digest that no standard-tooling signature recovers to.

OUTCOME: verifyQuoteBatch() in createServiceFromQuotes / extendServiceFromQuotes will reject every quote signed by a spec-compliant wallet/library (recovered != operator → Errors.InvalidQuoteSignature). The RFQ create/extend flow is only usable with a bespoke signer that replicates the contract's non-standard concatenation; integrating standard wallet tooling silently produces invalid signatures. This is a correctness/interoperability defect (liveness of the RFQ feature), not an attacker-profit path. Note: JOB_QUOTE_TYPEHASH (line 42) has no nested referenced types and is unaffected; the bug is specific to QuoteDetails. Fix: swap the order so `Asset(...)` precedes `AssetSecurityCommitment(...)`.

finding.title: [business-logic] QuoteDetails digest lacks operation/serviceId binding → create-quote redeemable as extend
finding.description:
SETUP: A single QuoteDetails digest (hashQuote, lines 85-101) commits to requester, blueprintId, ttlBlocks, totalCost, timestamp, expiry, confidentiality, securityCommitments, resourceCommitments. It does NOT commit to (a) which operation the quote authorizes (service creation vs. extension) nor (b) a target serviceId. Both createServiceFromQuotes (QuotesCreate.sol:172) and extendServiceFromQuotes (QuotesExtend.sol:158) verify the SAME digest via verifyQuoteBatch with the same _usedQuotes map.

ATTACK: An operator signs a quote intending a brand-new engagement for buyer B (requester=B, blueprintId=X, ttlBlocks=T, totalCost=C). B instead calls extendServiceFromQuotes(serviceId, [quote], additionalTtl=T) on any existing service B already owns of blueprint X where that operator is currently active. All verifyQuoteBatch checks pass (requester==msg.sender==B, blueprintId match, ttlBlocks==additionalTtl). The quote is consumed as an extension. Critically, the extend path (QuotesExtend._processing) updates only the resource-commitment hash and ignores the signed securityCommitments/exposureBps — so the exposure the operator priced into C is never applied to the service operator record, whereas on the create path it would set exposureBps.

OUTCOME: The operator's price C, scoped (in their intent) to a new service with a committed exposure, is repurposed to extend an unrelated existing service without applying the committed exposure. Impact is bounded because requester==msg.sender restricts redirection to the service owner the operator already chose to deal with, and the digest is single-use; there is no direct fund theft. Still, the operator's signature does not actually constrain how the buyer applies it. Recommend adding an action/serviceId discriminator field to QuoteDetails (or distinct typehashes for create vs extend) so a quote authorizes exactly one operation against one target.

finding.title: [business-logic] QuoteDetails digest lacks create-vs-extend discriminator → cross-flow quote reuse
finding.description:
SETUP: `QuoteDetails` (Types.sol:656) and its typehash (SignatureLib.sol:35-36) commit to {requester, blueprintId, ttlBlocks, totalCost, timestamp, expiry, confidentiality, commitments}. There is NO field identifying which operation the quote authorizes. Both `createServiceFromQuotes` (QuotesCreate.sol:56→172) and `extendServiceFromQuotes` (QuotesExtend.sol:75→158) call the SAME `SignatureLib.verifyQuoteBatch` with the SAME `QUOTE_TYPEHASH`, the SAME `_usedQuotes` map, identical (blueprintId, ttl) matching, and `expectedRequester = msg.sender`. Create passes the new-service ttl as `ttl`; extend passes `additionalTtl` as `ttl`. Nothing distinguishes the two contexts in the signed payload.

ATTACK: An operator A active in a service that buyer R owns is, by construction, registered on blueprint X (joining requires registration; `_operatorRegistrations[X][A].registeredAt != 0`). Suppose A issues a *renewal/extension* quote to R — common to price renewals below new-service price — signing requester=R, blueprintId=X, ttlBlocks=T, totalCost=C_discount. Instead of calling `extendServiceFromQuotes`, R calls `createServiceFromQuotes(X, [thatQuote], …)`. `_ensureOperatorsRegistered` passes (A is registered), `verifyQuoteBatch` passes (requester==R==msg.sender, blueprint/ttl/expiry/freshness all match), and a brand-new full service is activated at the discounted extension price. The operator's signature could not scope itself to "extension of service S only" — `QuoteDetails` cannot even reference a serviceId.

OUTCOME: Buyer redeems an extension-priced (or any context-specific) quote for the more valuable create action (or vice-versa), underpaying the operator for the service actually obtained. The operator has no on-chain means to restrict a quote to one flow. Remediation: add an operation/context discriminator (e.g. a `kind` enum or distinct typehash, plus serviceId for extensions) to the signed QuoteDetails so a create quote and an extend quote produce different digests and cannot be substituted.

finding.title: [business-logic] QUOTE_TYPEHASH references nested structs in non-canonical EIP-712 order
finding.description:
SETUP: EIP-712 (`encodeType`) requires the set of referenced struct types to be "sorted by name and appended". The referenced types here are `Asset`, `AssetSecurityCommitment`, `ResourceCommitment`; alphabetical order is Asset < AssetSecurityCommitment < ResourceCommitment (the shorter "Asset" is a prefix of "AssetSecurityCommitment", so it sorts first).

BUG: `QUOTE_TYPEHASH` (line 36) appends them as `…)AssetSecurityCommitment(Asset asset,uint16 exposureBps)Asset(uint8 kind,address token)ResourceCommitment(…)` — `AssetSecurityCommitment` is placed BEFORE `Asset`, violating the canonical ordering.

OUTCOME: Any standards-compliant signer (MetaMask `eth_signTypedData_v4`, ethers/viem `signTypedData`) computes the type string with `Asset` first, yielding a different `hashStruct` and therefore a signature that fails `digest.recover(...) != operator` in `verifyQuoteBatch`. Operators cannot sign valid `createServiceFromQuotes`/`extendServiceFromQuotes` quotes with standard tooling — they must use a bespoke signer that reproduces this exact non-canonical string. This is an interoperability/availability defect (the entire service-creation RFQ path is unusable with off-the-shelf wallets) rather than a fund-loss exploit; the per-job `JOB_QUOTE_TYPEHASH` (line 42, no nested structs) is unaffected. Remediation: reorder to `Asset(...)AssetSecurityCommitment(...)ResourceCommitment(...)`.

finding.title: [business-logic] Quote-path exposureBps never clamped to BPS_DENOMINATOR → operator inflates subscription payout share
finding.description:
SETUP: In the RFQ create path, the per-operator exposure stored on the service is taken verbatim from the operator's own signed quote. `_quoteExposure` (QuotesCreate.sol:307-314) returns `commitments[0].exposureBps` with no upper bound, and `_processOperatorQuotes` (QuotesCreate.sol:285-287) writes it straight into `_serviceOperators[serviceId][op].exposureBps` (a uint16, max 65535). The request path enforces a cap: ServicesRequests.sol:275-277 reverts with InvalidState when `exposures[i] > BPS_DENOMINATOR` (10000). The quote path has NO equivalent check — and `exposureBps` is part of the signed EIP-712 digest, so the operator authorizes whatever value it embeds.

Quote-created services never populate `_serviceSecurityCommitments`, so the exposure-weighted billing code falls back to the stored `_serviceOperators[...].exposureBps`:
- PaymentsDistribution.sol:133-136 (`_initSubscriptionBaseline` fallback): `fallbackBps = _serviceOperators[serviceId][op].exposureBps; exposedAmount = stakeOp * fallbackBps / BPS_DENOMINATOR`.
- PaymentsBilling.sol:293-295 (per-bill weighting): `fallbackBps = _serviceOperators[serviceId][op].exposureBps; contribution = opDeltaRaw * fallbackBps / BPS_DENOMINATOR`, and `contribution` is the operator's payout weight.

ATTACK: A registered operator signs a quote for a Subscription-pricing blueprint with `securityCommitments[0].exposureBps = 65535` (6.55× the legitimate 10000 cap) while honest co-operators sign 10000. A buyer bundles these into `createServiceFromQuotes`; the malicious value is stored unclamped. On every subscription bill, that operator's `contribution` weight is inflated up to ~6.55×, capturing a disproportionate share of the operator payout pool — funds that would otherwise go to honest co-operators billed against the same customer escrow.

OUTCOME: Cross-operator fund misallocation: an operator steals up to ~6.5× its fair share of subscription payouts from co-operators in any quote-created multi-operator Subscription service.
