The attacker accepts proportionally higher slashing exposure (Slashing.sol:73-76 also reads the unclamped `opData.exposureBps`), but absent a slash this is pure profit. Bounded to 6.55× by the uint16 field width. Fix: clamp/reject `exposureBps > BPS_DENOMINATOR` in `_quoteExposure` / `_processOperatorQuotes`, mirroring ServicesRequests.sol:275.

finding.title: [crypto] BN254.verifyBls accepts point-at-infinity (and non-subgroup) inputs as a universally-valid BLS signature
finding.description: verifyBls/verifyAggregatedBls perform NO point validation. With signature = G1 infinity (0,0) and pubkey = G2 infinity (0,0,0,0), pairingCheck computes e(O,gen)*e(-H(m),O)=1*1=1 -> returns TRUE for ANY message. PROVEN by test/audit/BN254Infinity.t.sol (verifyBls(msgA,O,O)=1, verifyBls(msgB,O,O)=1). Root cause: the alt_bn128 pairing precompile (0x08) checks on-curve but NOT prime-order-subgroup membership, and the library never calls isValidG1/isValidG2 (and isValidG2 itself only bounds-checks coordinates, never on-curve). Beyond identity: any G2 cofactor point Q ([r]*P for P on the twist) satisfies e(H(m),Q)=1, so an operator can register a NON-zero on-curve cofactor pubkey with an all-zero proof-of-possession signature and pass ServiceValidationLib.requireBlsProofOfPossession, defeating the _isNonZeroBlsPubkey guard in ServicesApprovals.sol:201. If a threshold-meeting signer set in JobsAggregation._verifyAggregatedSignature all hold such keys, expectedPubkey is a cofactor point and an all-zero aggregatedSignature verifies, finalizing forged job results (-> _distributeJobPayment). SETUP: malicious operator(s) approve a service supplying cofactor blsPubkey + zero PoP. ATTACK: submit aggregated result with aggregatedSignature=(0,0). OUTCOME: BLS threshold authorization is bypassed for the attacker-controlled signer set. Fix: reject identity points and enforce G1/G2 on-curve + subgroup checks (scalar-mul by R_MOD == O) before pairing.

finding.title: [business-logic] RFQ job-quote price not bound to job inputs -> operator forced to execute arbitrary costlier work at quoted price
finding.description: JOB_QUOTE_TYPEHASH / JobQuoteDetails commit price to (requester,serviceId,jobIndex,...) but NOT to the job input payload. JobsRFQ.submitJobFromQuotes (src/core/JobsRFQ.sol:48-119) takes caller-supplied `inputs`, runs only SchemaLib.validateJobParams (type-shape validation, any values allowed), then binds the operator to `quote.details.price` regardless of inputs. ATTACK: requester obtains an operator quote priced for cheap inputs, then redeems the same single-use quote with arbitrarily expensive inputs (large/heavy computation). OUTCOME: operator must perform unbounded-cost work for the cheap quoted price, or eat a slash for non-delivery — an economic griefing vector against operators. Fix: bind keccak256(inputs) (or an inputs commitment) into JobQuoteDetails and verify it equals the submitted inputs in submitJobFromQuotes.

finding.title: [business-logic] Service create/extend quotes share QUOTE_TYPEHASH + _usedQuotes with no flow discriminator -> cross-flow redemption
finding.description: QuoteDetails has no field distinguishing a create-service quote from an extend-service quote. QuotesCreate.createServiceFromQuotes (src/core/QuotesCreate.sol:172) and QuotesExtend.extendServiceFromQuotes (src/core/QuotesExtend.sol:158) both call SignatureLib.verifyQuoteBatch with the SAME _usedQuotes mapping, SAME domainSeparator, and only blueprintId+ttl as cross-checks. A quote an operator signed intending one flow (e.g. onboarding to a fresh service) can be redeemed by the requester in the other flow (extending an existing service) whenever blueprintId/ttl match and requester==msg.sender. OUTCOME: operators cannot price create vs extend differently with any safety — a quote signed for one operation is silently valid for the other. Fix: add an explicit operation/intent enum (and ideally serviceId for extend) to QuoteDetails and bind it in the typehash.

finding.title: [eip712] QUOTE_TYPEHASH referenced-struct ordering is non-canonical (Asset vs AssetSecurityCommitment swapped)
finding.description: EIP-712 requires referenced struct types to be appended sorted by name. QUOTE_TYPEHASH lists ...AssetSecurityCommitment(...)Asset(...)ResourceCommitment(...). Canonical order is Asset, AssetSecurityCommitment, ResourceCommitment ("Asset" < "AssetSecurityCommitment"), so Asset and AssetSecurityCommitment are swapped. Consequence: a standards-compliant EIP-712 signer (wallets, ethers/viem signTypedData) computes a DIFFERENT QUOTE_TYPEHASH -> different digest -> signatures will not verify on-chain (verifyQuoteBatch reverts InvalidQuoteSignature). Not directly exploitable for forgery, but a correctness/interop defect that forces non-standard signing tooling and can silently break RFQ onboarding. Fix: reorder the appended struct types alphabetically.

finding.title: Inverted/truncating decimal scaling in UniswapV3Oracle prices assets at 0x or 1e6x
finding.description: _getPriceFromSqrtX96 computes the raw token ratio with (sqrtPriceX96*sqrtPriceX96)>>192 BEFORE any decimal adjustment, then applies price*(10**quoteDecimals)/(10**tokenDecimals). Both the early truncation and the decimal factor are wrong. SETUP: configurePool for the canonical WETH(18,token0)/USDC(6,token1,quoteIsUsd) pool. ATTACK/OUTCOME: raw ratio token1/token0 ~3e-9 truncates to 0 -> getPriceData returns price=0 with isValid=true (line 404,324-329). Re-running test/oracles/OraclePoC.t.sol::test_PriceTruncatesToZero_WethAsToken0 yields price=0. In the non-truncated orientation (6-dec token0 / 18-dec quote) the decimal factor is INVERTED, overstating price by 10^tokenDecimals (1e6x) -- test_DecimalScalingInverted_Overvalues returns 1.8e15 vs correct 1.8e9. The correct factor to express quote_raw per whole token is *10^tokenDecimals applied via mulDiv before truncation; the code uses 10^qd/10^td. oracle.toUSD() feeds USD-weighting in Slashing.sol:214, PaymentsEffectiveExposure.sol:94, ServiceFeeDistributor.sol:1365 -- so a $0-priced asset escapes slashing weight / exposure and a 1e6x-overpriced asset dominates reward and exposure weighting. Manifests under STANDARD pool configuration, not attacker-only config.

finding.title: UniswapV3Oracle marks price valid even when computed price is 0
finding.description: _getPriceData unconditionally sets data.isValid=true (line 329) with no price>0 guard. SETUP: any pool/decimal combination where the truncated math (F-001) or a degenerate tick yields price==0. ATTACK: getPrice() returns 0 instead of reverting PriceNotAvailable; toUSD(token,amount) returns 0. OUTCOME: in Slashing.sol:214 the zero weight makes the asset contribute nothing to weightedCommitted, so the operator dodges slashing weight for that asset; in PaymentsEffectiveExposure.sol:94 the operator exposure for that asset drops to 0, skewing payout weights. fromUSD() instead divides by price -> division-by-zero panic / DoS for consumers. Even with correct scaling the oracle should fail closed on a non-positive price exactly as ChainlinkOracle does (InvalidPrice).

finding.title: ChainlinkOracle lacks min/maxAnswer circuit-breaker bounds
finding.description: _getPriceData validates answer>0, answeredInRound>=roundId, and staleness, but never checks the aggregator minAnswer/maxAnswer bounds. SETUP: a Chainlink aggregator floors its reported value at minAnswer. ATTACK: during an extreme price move (the canonical LUNA-style crash) the feed keeps returning latestRoundData with a fresh timestamp pinned at minAnswer, well above the true market price. OUTCOME: toUSD over-values the collapsing asset; downstream USD-weighting in Slashing/PaymentsEffectiveExposure/ServiceFeeDistributor uses the floored (wrong) price. Mitigation: read the aggregator min/max and revert when answer is at the boundary.

finding.title: UniswapV3Oracle has no L2 sequencer-uptime guard and reverts (DoS) at extreme ticks
finding.description: Two issues in the same area. (1) Unlike ChainlinkOracle (_requireSequencerUp), UniswapV3Oracle performs no L2 sequencer-uptime check; the project targets L2s (Base sequencer feed is referenced in ChainlinkOracle). When the sequencer is down/restarting, the TWAP can be consumed without the protections applied to the Chainlink path. (2) _getPriceFromSqrtX96 computes sqrtPriceX96*sqrtPriceX96 in plain uint256 (no FullMath). Near the tick extremes sqrtPriceX96 approaches ~2^160, so the square (~2^320) overflows uint256 and reverts, making the oracle a hard DoS for legitimately high price-ratio pairs. Quote-token freshness for quoteIsUsd pools is also pinned to block.timestamp (line 301/327), so TWAP staleness is never surfaced to downstream maxAge checks.

finding.title: RewardVaults top-up does not settle reward debt -> steal prior epoch rewards
finding.description: recordDelegate()/recordStake() use MasterChef-style accounting (owed = boostedScore * (accumulatedPerShare - lastAccumulatedPerShare)/PRECISION) but only set lastAccumulatedPerShare when isNewDelegator (stakedAmount==0). On any TOP-UP they do debt.boostedScore += score WITHOUT first settling pending rewards and WITHOUT advancing lastAccumulatedPerShare. RewardsManager._notifyExternalRewardsManager (src/staking/RewardsManager.sol:209) calls recordDelegate with only the incremental amount on every delegation increase, so the path is live. ATTACK: (1) delegate 1 wei to operator -> lastAcc=A0~0; (2) wait for epoch rewards so pool.accumulatedPerShare grows to A1; (3) delegate a large amount L -> boostedScore becomes ~L, lastAcc still A0; (4) claimDelegatorRewards -> owed = L*(A1-A0)/PRECISION, i.e. the large stake retroactively earns ALL rewards accrued before it existed. Rewards are paid from the contract TNT balance (_transferRewards), so this drains the shared OperatorPool, stealing other delegators reward funds and rendering the vault insolvent. Same flaw in recordStake (lines 510-522).

finding.title: InflationPool epoch budget recounts unclaimed operator/customer/developer pending -> over-accrual / insolvency
finding.description: _distributeOperatorRewards/_distributeCustomerRewards/_distributeDeveloperRewards only EARMARK rewards into pendingOperatorRewards/pendingCustomerRewards/pendingDeveloperRewards (lines 610,687,727) and DO NOT transfer tokens out of the pool. Those tokens therefore remain inside poolBalance(). calculateEpochBudget() (line 1165) returns poolBalance()/epochsRemaining using the RAW token balance, never subtracting outstanding pending liabilities. Consequently the same tokens that already back unclaimed pending rewards are re-budgeted and earmarked again every epoch. Over many epochs sum(pendingOperator+pendingCustomer+pendingDeveloper) grows without bound relative to the actual balance, so the pool promises more than it holds. claimOperatorRewards/claimCustomerRewards/claimDeveloperRewards do safeTransfer(fullPending); once cumulative claims exceed the balance, later claims revert (insufficient balance) — first-come claimants are paid, others are permanently shortchanged. Root cause: budgetable balance must be poolBalance() - outstandingPendingLiabilities.

finding.title: Permissionless distributeEpoch() advances epoch with empty serviceIds, denying staker inflation
finding.description: distributeEpoch() is permissionless (no role) and calls _distributeEpoch(empty serviceIds). _distributeStakerInflation returns 0 when serviceIds.length==0, and the undistributed-redistribution math (lines 432-433) deliberately EXCLUDES the stakers category, so stakersTarget (epochBudget*stakersBps/BPS) is skipped while the epoch is marked distributed (currentEpoch++). The role-gated distributeEpochWithServices(serviceIds) is the only path that pays staker inflation, but any unprivileged account can front-run it the instant epoch.endTimestamp passes by calling distributeEpoch(), consuming the epoch and denying that epoch staker-inflation allocation. Repeating each epoch perpetually griefs staker inflation (funds stay in the pool but are denied/delayed to stakers).

finding.title: StreamingPaymentManager strands freshly-dripped chunk on operator-leave and service-termination
finding.description: _drip() (lines 189-224) is a PURE-ACCOUNTING helper: it advances p.distributed += chunk and p.lastDripTime but transfers NOTHING. Delivery of the chunk is the caller's responsibility — dripAndGetChunk (246-252) and dripOperatorStreams (290-296) call _drip and THEN _transferPayment the chunk to the distributor, which credits delegators. However onOperatorLeaving (333-336) calls bare _drip(serviceId, operator) and discards the return value: the chunk earned since lastDripTime is marked distributed but is never transferred to the distributor nor credited to any delegator accumulator — it is permanently stranded in StreamingPaymentManager (no sweep function exists). onServiceTerminated (305-330) has the same flaw: it _drip()s (inflating p.distributed by the fresh chunk), then refunds only remaining = totalAmount - distributed to the customer, so the freshly-dripped chunk is neither delivered to delegators nor refunded to the customer — it is burned. Impact: delegators of a leaving operator, and delegators at service termination, lose the final streamed chunk (up to the entire un-dripped remainder if the stream had not been dripped for a long interval); those tokens are locked forever.

finding.title: [lock-bypass] recordDelegate grants lock-multiplier boost but sets no lockExpiry → boosted rewards with no lock
finding.description: SETUP: The LIVE delegation path is recordDelegate(), called by RewardsManager._notifyExternalRewardsManager (src/staking/RewardsManager.sol:209-210), which forwards a caller-chosen lockMultiplierBps. recordDelegate computes score = amount*lockMultiplierBps/BPS_DENOMINATOR (line 308) and credits debt.boostedScore += score and operatorPools.totalStaked += score (lines 320,329) — i.e. the delegator earns a lock-multiplier-boosted share (up to 1.6x) of all accumulatedPerShare rewards. ATTACK: But lines 321-322 unconditionally set debt.lockDuration = None and debt.lockExpiry = 0 — NO lock is ever recorded. The live un-delegate path recordUndelegate() (lines 335-376) contains NO lockExpiry check at all (only the separate recordUnstake at line 542 checks it). So a delegator: (1) delegates with maximum lockMultiplierBps (1.6x), receiving 1.6x boosted reward score; (2) collects epoch rewards at 1.6x weight; (3) immediately undelegates via recordUndelegate with zero wait. OUTCOME: The lock multiplier — whose entire purpose is to reward funds committed for 30-180 days — is obtained for free, diluting honest lockers and unboosted delegators on every epoch distribution. Separately, because lines 321-322 run on every call (including top-ups), a recordDelegate top-up onto a position that recordStake() previously locked wipes its lockExpiry to 0, enabling early unlock through recordUnstake as well.

finding.title: [reward-accounting] _distributeToOperatorPool silently drops poolReward when totalStaked==0
finding.description: SETUP: _distributeToOperatorPool splits an incoming reward into commission and poolReward = amount - commission (lines 709-710). Commission is always booked (line 712), but poolReward is only converted into claimable rewards when pool.totalStaked > 0 (guard line 714: `if (pool.totalStaked > 0 && poolReward > 0)`). It is reachable for an operator with ZERO delegators via recordServiceReward (line 393), distributeRewards (line 663), and distributeEpochRewardToOperator (line 466) — none of which skip zero-stake operators (unlike distributeEpochReward, which does at line 436). ATTACK/OUTCOME: When an operator with no current delegators (totalStaked==0) receives a service/epoch reward, the operatorCommission portion is still credited to the operator, but the entire poolReward (85% by default) is silently discarded: accumulatedPerShare is not advanced and no liability is recorded, yet vaultStates.rewardsDistributed += amount (line 718) inflates as if fully distributed. The corresponding TNT held by the vault becomes unallocated/stranded — the intended delegator reward simply vanishes from accounting, and the emitted RewardsDistributed event reports a poolReward that no one can ever claim. A later delegator joining sets lastAccumulatedPerShare to the current value, so the dropped reward is never recoverable for them either.

finding.title: [reward-accounting] _distributeStakingRewards splits cross-vault by raw deposits (ignores score) and can strand TNT on swallowed notify
finding.description: SETUP: _distributeStakingRewards allocates the per-vault share using each vault's raw totalDeposits (lines 541-552), but the vault's internal distributeEpochReward (RewardVaults.sol:419-447) then splits that share across operators/delegators by score-weighted totalStaked. ISSUE 1 (allocation mismatch): lock multipliers (1.1x-1.6x), which RewardVaults applies to score, are ignored for the cross-vault split — so a vault whose delegators all chose long locks receives the same epoch allocation as an equal-deposit vault with no locks, under-rewarding lockers relative to the documented design. ISSUE 2 (stranding): line 556 transfers vaultShare TNT to the vault and line 557 counts it as actualDistributed BEFORE _notifyVaultReward (line 565) calls vaults.distributeEpochReward inside a try/catch that swallows all errors (line 566). If that call reverts — e.g. InflationPool was never granted REWARDS_MANAGER_ROLE on RewardVaults, or any future revert — the TNT sits in the vault with accumulatedPerShare never advanced (unclaimable), yet the epoch records it as fully distributed. OUTCOME: silent, recurring loss of staking inflation into an unallocated vault balance with no event signaling failure.

finding.title: [access-control] claimDelegatorRewardsFor lets anyone force-realize another account's rewards
finding.description: SETUP: claimDelegatorRewardsFor(asset, operator, delegator) has no caller restriction and lets any address trigger a claim for an arbitrary delegator. _claimDelegatorReward (line 739) transfers the owed amount to `delegator` itself, so this is NOT fund theft — proceeds reach the rightful owner. OUTCOME (griefing only): an attacker can force any delegator's pending rewards to be realized at a time of the attacker's choosing — advancing debt.lastAccumulatedPerShare and pushing TNT to the delegator. This matters for integrating contracts/accounts that rely on claiming on their own schedule (accounting, tax-event timing, or contracts that route received TNT through custom logic): they can be forced into unexpected receipt every block. There is no economic loss to the protocol, but the unrestricted third-party claim is unusual and worth gating to msg.sender or an explicit allowlist. Reported for completeness; impact is limited to forced reward realization, not theft or DoS.

finding.title: [lock-bypass] recordDelegate wipes lockExpiry on existing locked position → early unstake
finding.description:
recordDelegate() and recordStake() write the SAME struct delegatorDebts[asset][delegator][operator]. recordStake (line 516-521) sets debt.lockDuration=lock and debt.lockExpiry = block.timestamp + _lockDurationSeconds(lock) to commit a delegator to a lock in exchange for a boosted score multiplier (up to 1.6x). recordUnstake enforces it: line 542 `if (debt.lockExpiry > block.timestamp) revert StillLocked`.

SETUP: delegator locks via recordStake(asset, d, op, amt, SixMonths) -> boostedScore = 1.6x*amt, lockExpiry = now+180d.
ATTACK: any later recordDelegate(d, op, asset, 1 wei, *) for the same triple runs lines 319-322 UNCONDITIONALLY: `debt.lockDuration = LockDuration.None; debt.lockExpiry = 0;` (it never branches on an existing lock). This zeroes the lock while KEEPING the previously-minted 1.6x boostedScore.
OUTCOME: the StillLocked guard in recordUnstake (line 542) now passes, so the delegator withdraws the full position immediately — keeping the boosted reward weight earned for a six-month commitment it no longer honors. recordUndelegate (line 335) has no lock guard at all, compounding the asymmetry. The same clobber happens for every top-up, so the vault's lock-multiplier accounting can never be trusted once a delegate() touches a staked position.

Fix: in recordDelegate only set lock fields when isNewDelegator, and never downgrade an active (lockExpiry>block.timestamp) lock.

finding.title: recordDelegate grants lock-multiplier boost but sets no lockExpiry -> boosted rewards with no lock commitment
finding.description: The LIVE delegation path recordDelegate() (called by RewardsManager._notifyExternalRewardsManager, src/staking/RewardsManager.sol:209-210) forwards a caller-chosen lockMultiplierBps and computes score = amount*lockMultiplierBps/BPS_DENOMINATOR (line 308), crediting debt.boostedScore += score and operatorPools.totalStaked += score (lines 320,329). So the delegator earns up to a 1.6x boosted share of all accumulatedPerShare rewards. BUT lines 321-322 unconditionally set debt.lockDuration=None and debt.lockExpiry=0 -> no lock is recorded. The live un-delegate path recordUndelegate() (lines 335-376) has NO lockExpiry check (only the separate recordUnstake at line 542 checks it). ATTACK: delegate with max lockMultiplierBps, collect epoch rewards at 1.6x weight, then immediately undelegate with zero wait. The lock multiplier (meant to reward 30-180 day commitments) is obtained for free, diluting honest lockers every epoch. Corollary: because lines 321-322 run on every call including top-ups, a recordDelegate top-up onto a position previously locked by recordStake() wipes its lockExpiry to 0, enabling early unlock via recordUnstake.

finding.title: _distributeToOperatorPool silently drops poolReward when pool.totalStaked==0
finding.description: _distributeToOperatorPool splits a reward into commission and poolReward = amount - commission (lines 709-710). Commission is always booked (line 712) but poolReward only becomes claimable when pool.totalStaked>0 (guard line 714). It is reachable for an operator with ZERO delegators via recordServiceReward (line 393), distributeRewards (line 663) and distributeEpochRewardToOperator (line 466), none of which skip zero-stake operators (unlike distributeEpochReward at line 436). When such an operator receives a reward, commission is credited to the operator but the entire poolReward (85% by default) is silently discarded: accumulatedPerShare is not advanced and no liability recorded, yet vaultStates.rewardsDistributed += amount (line 718) inflates as if fully distributed and RewardsDistributed reports a poolReward no one can claim. The matching TNT held by the vault is stranded; a later delegator sets lastAccumulatedPerShare to the current value, so the dropped reward is never recoverable.

finding.title: _distributeStakingRewards splits cross-vault by raw deposits (ignores score) and strands TNT on swallowed notify
finding.description: _distributeStakingRewards allocates each vault's share by raw totalDeposits (lines 541-552), but the vault's distributeEpochReward (RewardVaults.sol:419-447) then splits that share by score-weighted totalStaked. Issue 1 (allocation mismatch): lock multipliers (1.1x-1.6x), applied to score inside RewardVaults, are ignored for the cross-vault split, so a vault of long-lock delegators receives the same allocation as an equal-deposit no-lock vault, under-rewarding lockers vs the documented design. Issue 2 (stranding): line 556 transfers vaultShare TNT to the vault and line 557 counts it as actualDistributed BEFORE _notifyVaultReward (line 565) calls vaults.distributeEpochReward inside a try/catch that swallows all errors (line 566). If that call reverts (e.g. InflationPool lacks REWARDS_MANAGER_ROLE on RewardVaults), the TNT sits in the vault with accumulatedPerShare never advanced (unclaimable) yet the epoch records it as fully distributed, with no event signaling failure.

finding.title: claimDelegatorRewardsFor lets anyone force-realize another account's rewards (griefing)
finding.description: claimDelegatorRewardsFor(asset, operator, delegator) has no caller restriction and lets any address trigger a claim for an arbitrary delegator. _claimDelegatorReward (line 739) transfers owed funds to `delegator` itself, so this is NOT theft. Griefing only: an attacker can force any delegator's pending rewards to be realized at the attacker's chosen time, advancing debt.lastAccumulatedPerShare and pushing TNT to the delegator. This matters for integrating contracts/accounts that rely on claiming on their own schedule (accounting, tax timing, custom TNT-receipt logic). No protocol fund loss; recommend gating to msg.sender or an explicit delegate allowlist.

finding.title: [reward-accounting] _distributeToOperatorPool burns delegator share when totalStaked==0
finding.description:
_distributeToOperatorPool splits an incoming reward into commission (line 709) and poolReward = amount - commission (line 710). The delegator portion is only credited if the pool has stake: line 714 `if (pool.totalStaked > 0 && poolReward > 0) pool.accumulatedPerShare += ...`. When pool.totalStaked == 0 the ELSE branch is missing: poolReward is neither added to accumulatedPerShare, nor refunded, nor parked as pending — yet line 718 still does `vaultStates[asset].rewardsDistributed += amount`, accounting it as fully distributed.

SETUP: an operator with no delegators (totalStaked==0 — common for an operator running only on its own bond, or before any delegation). The role-gated entrypoints recordServiceReward (line 393), distributeRewards (line 663) and distributeEpochRewardToOperator (line 466) all funnel straight into _distributeToOperatorPool WITHOUT the stake>0 pre-skip that distributeEpochReward applies (line 436).
ATTACK/EVENT: a service reward of X is routed to that operator. commission (~15% default) lands in pendingCommission (claimable), but poolReward (~85% of X) evaporates from the accounting — the TNT remains in the vault contract but no accumulatedPerShare ever reflects it.
OUTCOME: ~85% of every reward sent to a delegator-less operator is permanently locked. A delegator who stakes LATER does not recover it, because accumulatedPerShare was never bumped — MasterChef accounting only pays rewards accrued after a delegator's lastAccumulatedPerShare snapshot. Funds are stranded, not stolen.

Fix: when totalStaked==0, add poolReward to pendingCommission (or a carry-over bucket) instead of dropping it.

finding.title: REPRODUCED: RewardVaults top-up does not settle reward debt -> steal prior epoch rewards
finding.description:
REPRODUCED with a self-contained Foundry PoC. RewardVaults uses MasterChef accounting owed = boostedScore*(accumulatedPerShare - lastAccumulatedPerShare)/PRECISION. recordDelegate (L314-332) only snapshots lastAccumulatedPerShare when isNewDelegator (stakedAmount==0); on a TOP-UP it adds debt.boostedScore += score without settling pending or advancing lastAccumulatedPerShare. recordStake (L510-522) shares the flaw.

SETUP: attacker recordDelegate 1 wei to OPERATOR (lastAcc=0), honest recordDelegate LARGE=1000e18 to same operator (commission=0). ATTACK: distributeRewards(REWARD=100e18) -> accumulatedPerShare grows over totalStaked=LARGE+1. Attacker fair pending = 0 (1/(LARGE+1) share). Attacker then recordDelegate(LARGE) — boostedScore becomes LARGE+1, lastAcc STILL 0. OUTCOME: attacker pendingDelegatorRewards jumps 0 -> 99999999999999999000 (~REWARD); claimDelegatorRewardsFor pays it out. Vault was funded with exactly REWARD, so after the attacker drains it (vault remaining=1000 wei) the honest delegator is still owed ~REWARD but their claim reverts "Insufficient reward balance". The topped-up stake retroactively earned the entire reward accrued before it existed, stealing the honest delegator's funds and rendering the vault insolvent. Live path: RewardsManager._notifyExternalRewardsManager calls recordDelegate with only the incremental amount on every delegation increase.

reproduction.pocStdout:
[PASS] test_F001_TopUpStealsPriorEpochRewards() (gas: 717855)
  --- after epoch, before top-up ---
  attacker fair pending (1 wei stake): 0
  honest   fair pending (LARGE stake): 99999999999999999000
  --- after attacker top-up of LARGE ---
  attacker pending NOW: 99999999999999999000
  attacker CLAIMED: 99999999999999999000
  attacker TNT balance: 99999999999999999000
  vault TNT remaining: 1000
  honest STILL owed: 99999999999999999000
  vault can cover honest? false
  honest claim REVERTED: Insufficient reward balance (funds stolen)
Suite result: ok. 1 passed; 0 failed; 0 skipped
reproduction.notes: Deployed RewardVaults behind ERC1967Proxy; test holds ADMIN_ROLE+REWARDS_MANAGER_ROLE via initialize. tntToken mocked with a 2-selector ERC20 (balanceOf/transfer are the only methods RewardVaults invokes). operatorCommissionBps=0 for clean math. Attacker pending goes 0 -> ~100e18 purely from a post-reward top-up; honest delegator's claim then reverts on insufficient balance, proving theft + insolvency.

finding.title: [reward-accounting] InflationPool._distributeStakingRewards splits cross-vault by deposits not score
finding.description:
_distributeStakingRewards apportions the epoch staking budget ACROSS vaults using each vault's raw totalDeposits (line 541 reads vaultStates -> vaultDeposits; line 552 vaultShare = amount*deposits[i]/totalDeposits). But INSIDE each vault, distributeEpochReward and _distributeToOperatorPool distribute by SCORE (totalStaked = deposits * lock multiplier, up to 1.6x) — see RewardVaults line 715 dividing poolReward by score-weighted totalStaked.

OUTCOME: the two layers use inconsistent weights. A vault whose stakers predominantly use long locks (high totalScore relative to totalDeposits) is under-allocated at the cross-vault step relative to the reward weight it actually grants internally, while a vault of all-unlocked stake is over-allocated. Per-TNT reward rate for a locked staker is therefore diluted exactly opposite to the lock incentive the protocol advertises. Not a theft, but a systematic misallocation that breaks the lock-multiplier economics across multi-vault deployments.

Fix: use vaults.vaultStates(asset).totalScore (the score-weighted total) for the cross-vault proportion, matching the intra-vault denominator.

finding.title: [reward-accounting] Swallowed distributeEpochReward revert strands pre-transferred TNT in vault
finding.description:
_distributeStakingRewards transfers vaultShare TNT to the vault at line 556 and increments actualDistributed at line 557 BEFORE notifying the vault. The notify (line 560 -> _notifyVaultReward line 565-567) wraps vaults.distributeEpochReward in `try { } catch { }`, swallowing ALL failures.

distributeEpochReward (RewardVaults line 415-447) loops over assetOperators[asset], an array that only ever GROWS (_trackOperator line 670-674 pushes; nothing ever removes an operator). Every distinct operator that ever received a delegation/service reward for that asset is iterated forever. Once the array is large enough the loop runs out of gas and distributeEpochReward reverts.

OUTCOME: on revert the catch hides it, but the TNT was already moved into the vault (line 556) and counted as distributedThisPeriod / totalDistributed (line 557 + caller), while the vault's accumulatedPerShare was never updated. Those tokens become unclaimable dead weight inside the vault, and the pool's distributed accounting overstates what stakers can actually claim. The same silent-strand occurs for ANY revert in distributeEpochReward, not only OOG. Because operators are never untracked, the failure is monotonic and eventually permanent for that asset.

Fix: prune assetOperators (untrack on full unstake), and/or move the safeTransfer + actualDistributed increment to only occur after a successful (non-catch) notify.

finding.title: recordDelegate wipes lockExpiry on existing locked position -> early unstake bypass
finding.description: recordDelegate() and recordStake() write the SAME struct delegatorDebts[asset][delegator][operator]. recordStake (516-521) commits a delegator to a lock (lockExpiry = now + duration) in exchange for a boosted score multiplier (<=1.6x); recordUnstake enforces it at line 542 (if lockExpiry > block.timestamp revert StillLocked). SETUP: delegator locks via recordStake(...,SixMonths) -> boostedScore=1.6x, lockExpiry=now+180d. ATTACK: any later recordDelegate(same delegator/operator/asset, even 1 wei) runs lines 321-322 UNCONDITIONALLY (debt.lockDuration=None; debt.lockExpiry=0), never branching on an existing lock, while keeping the 1.6x boostedScore. OUTCOME: the StillLocked guard now passes, delegator withdraws immediately keeping reward weight earned for a 6-month commitment it no longer honors. recordUndelegate (335) has no lock guard at all. Fix: only set lock fields when isNewDelegator and never downgrade an active lock.

finding.title: _distributeToOperatorPool burns delegator share when operator totalStaked==0
finding.description: _distributeToOperatorPool splits a reward into commission (709) and poolReward=amount-commission (710). The delegator portion is only credited if pool.totalStaked>0 (line 714); there is NO else branch, so when totalStaked==0 poolReward is neither added to accumulatedPerShare, refunded, nor parked as pending — yet line 718 still does rewardsDistributed += amount. SETUP: an operator with no delegators (totalStaked==0), common for self-bond-only operators or pre-delegation. Role-gated entrypoints recordServiceReward (393), distributeRewards (663), distributeEpochRewardToOperator (466) funnel into _distributeToOperatorPool WITHOUT the stake>0 pre-skip that distributeEpochReward applies (436). EVENT: reward X routed there; commission (~15%) -> pendingCommission, but poolReward (~85% of X) evaporates from accounting (TNT stays in contract, no accumulatedPerShare bump). OUTCOME: ~85% of every reward to a delegator-less operator is permanently stranded; a later delegator cannot recover it (MasterChef accounting only pays post-snapshot accrual). Fix: when totalStaked==0, carry poolReward over instead of dropping it.

finding.title: InflationPool._distributeStakingRewards splits cross-vault by deposits not score
finding.description: _distributeStakingRewards apportions the epoch staking budget ACROSS vaults using each vault's raw totalDeposits (541 reads vaultDeposits; 552 vaultShare=amount*deposits[i]/totalDeposits). But INSIDE each vault, distributeEpochReward/_distributeToOperatorPool distribute by SCORE (totalStaked=deposits*lockMultiplier, up to 1.6x) — RewardVaults line 715 divides poolReward by score-weighted totalStaked. The two layers use inconsistent weights: a vault dominated by long-lock (high score/deposit) stake is under-allocated cross-vault relative to the weight it grants internally, while an all-unlocked vault is over-allocated. The per-TNT reward rate for locked stakers is diluted opposite to the lock incentive. Misallocation, not theft. Fix: use totalScore (vaultStates score field) for the cross-vault proportion to match the intra-vault denominator.

finding.title: Swallowed distributeEpochReward revert strands pre-transferred TNT in vault
finding.description: _distributeStakingRewards transfers vaultShare TNT to the vault (556) and increments actualDistributed (557) BEFORE notifying it. The notify (_notifyVaultReward 565-567) wraps vaults.distributeEpochReward in try{}catch{}, swallowing all failures. distributeEpochReward (RewardVaults 415-447) loops over assetOperators[asset], an array that only grows (_trackOperator pushes at 670-674; nothing ever untracks). Once large enough the loop OOGs and reverts. OUTCOME: on revert the catch hides it, but TNT already moved into the vault (556) and was counted as distributed (557 + caller) while accumulatedPerShare was never updated -> tokens become unclaimable dead weight and pool accounting overstates claimable rewards. Same strand for ANY revert in distributeEpochReward. Because operators are never untracked the failure is monotonic/eventually permanent. Fix: prune assetOperators on full unstake; move the safeTransfer + actualDistributed increment to after a successful notify.

finding.title: [oracle-math] UniswapV3Oracle sqrtPrice→USD conversion truncates to 0 / mis-scales by 10^n
finding.description:
SETUP: `_getPriceFromSqrtX96` converts the TWAP sqrtPriceX96 to a quote-denominated price, then `_getPriceData` (L324) turns it into USD consumed by `oracle.toUSD()` in Slashing.sol:214 (slash weights), PaymentsEffectiveExposure.sol:94 (exposure) and PaymentsBilling.sol (billing).

ATTACK / ROOT CAUSE: Two compounding math errors.
1) Truncation: L404 `price = (sqrtPriceX96 * sqrtPriceX96) >> 192` (and L410-412 for the inverse branch) floors the raw token1/token0 ratio to an INTEGER *before* any decimal scaling. The raw per-smallest-unit ratio is <1 for virtually every real pool (e.g. an 18-dec token priced in 6-dec USDC, or any token worth <1 WETH), so it floors to 0.
2) Inverted decimal scaling: L406 multiplies by `10^quoteDecimals / 10^tokenDecimals`. Correct priceInQuote = P_raw * 10^tokenDecimals; the code applies 10^(quoteDec - tokenDec), wrong by 10^(quoteDec - 2*tokenDec).

OUTCOME (verified numerically):
- token0=18-dec TKN, quote=USDC, 1 TKN=$2 → oracle returns $0.00 (should be $2). The token's entire staked value is counted as ZERO in slashing weight and payment exposure.
- token0=USDC(6d), quote=WETH(18d), 1 USDC=$1 → oracle returns $1,200,000 per USDC (overvalued 1.2e6×).

Because `toUSD` feeds slashing weight distribution and exposure-weighted billing across heterogeneous assets, an attacker/operator can pick an asset whose pool ratio truncates favourably: stake an asset that prices to $0 to avoid being slashed/charged proportionally, or one that over-prices to dominate reward/exposure accounting. Any deployment that wires UniswapV3Oracle as the IPriceOracle silently misprices nearly all assets. The contract's own NatSpec claims the result is 18-dec USD; it is not.

This is not a scanner-pattern issue (no scanner flagged it) — it is a semantic conversion bug.

finding.title: [precision] UniswapV3Oracle TWAP price math truncates to 0 + inverted decimals → 0/garbage price
finding.description:
SETUP: `configurePool(token, pool, ...)` for any pool where 1 whole `token` is worth < 1 whole quote-token in raw wei terms — i.e. essentially every asset quoted against WETH or any equal/higher-decimal quote (WETH/USDC, TNT/WETH, etc.).

ATTACK / ROOT CAUSE: `_getPriceFromSqrtX96` computes the raw price as `(sqrtPriceX96 * sqrtPriceX96) >> 192` (line 404) BEFORE any decimal scaling. `sqrtP^2 >> 192` equals the token1/token0 ratio in *wei* (a dimensionless ratio that is <1 for almost all real pairs), so integer truncation floors it to 0. Verified numerically: TNT/WETH(18/18, 1 TNT=0.0005 WETH) → 0; WETH/USDC(18/6, 1 WETH=3000 USDC) → 0. The subsequent decimal adjustment (line 406) is ALSO inverted: it multiplies by `10**quoteDecimals / 10**tokenDecimals`, whereas converting a wei-ratio to "quote-wei per whole token0" requires multiplying by `10**tokenDecimals` (for equal decimals the correct factor is 1e18; the code yields 1). The `isToken0==false` branch (line 410) is worse: `sqrtPriceX96 * sqrtPriceX96` is up to 320 bits and OVERFLOWS uint256 at high ticks → revert/DoS, and `(1<<192)/sqrtSquared` truncates to 0 whenever price>1.

OUTCOME: `getPrice`/`toUSD` return 0 for realistic configurations; `_getPriceData` still sets `data.isValid = true`, so callers get a silent zero rather than a revert. This oracle feeds `Slashing.sol:214` (toUSD weight per asset), `PaymentsEffectiveExposure.sol:94` (USD exposure), and `ServiceFeeDistributor.sol:1365` (fee conversion). A zero price drops the asset from slashing-weight and exposure pools (weight==0 is skipped at Slashing.sol:215-218), letting an operator commit an asset that carries no slashing weight / no exposure while still backing services. `fromUSD` divides by `data.price` and reverts on 0 → DoS of any USD→token path. Value at risk: full mispricing of every Uniswap-quoted asset across slashing and payment accounting.

finding.title: [oracle] UniswapV3Oracle has no L2 sequencer-uptime check (ChainlinkOracle does)
finding.description:
SETUP: Deployment target is Base (an OP-stack L2 — see deploy/config/base-sepolia and CLAUDE.md). `ChainlinkOracle` was hardened with `_requireSequencerUp()` (ChainlinkOracle.sol:303-311) that rejects prices while the sequencer is down or within a grace window after restart. `UniswapV3Oracle._getPriceData` has NO equivalent gate.

ATTACK: When the Base sequencer goes down and restarts, users were unable to arbitrage/transact for the outage duration. In the grace window right after restart, Uniswap pool observations reflect pre-downtime state and the market is dislocated; the quote-feed staleness check (`maxAge`, default 1h) is wide enough to still accept the quote leg. An attacker who pre-positioned (or who can land the first transactions post-restart) can push the pool and have the TWAP/observe() path read a manipulated/stale price before honest flow corrects it — then trigger slashing-weight or exposure computations (`Slashing.sol:214`, `PaymentsEffectiveExposure.sol:94`) at the favorable price.

OUTCOME: Stale/manipulated valuations are accepted on L2 immediately after a sequencer restart, skewing USD-weighted slashing and payout accounting. The inconsistency (Chainlink gated, Uniswap not) shows the protection was intended but omitted on the TWAP oracle. Remediation: add the same configurable sequencer-uptime + grace-period gate to `UniswapV3Oracle._getPriceData`.

finding.title: [oracle] UniswapV3Oracle quote-feed path has no L2 sequencer-uptime check
finding.description:
SETUP: The protocol explicitly targets Base (an L2 — see BeaconChainProofs/L2 bridges). For non-USD quote tokens, UniswapV3Oracle reads a Chainlink feed (`quoteTokenFeeds`, L303-320) to convert the TWAP into USD, using the same latestRoundData staleness pattern as ChainlinkOracle.

ATTACK: ChainlinkOracle.sol deliberately implements `_requireSequencerUp()` (L303-311) — gating every price on the L2 sequencer uptime feed plus a grace period — precisely because Chainlink L2 feeds can serve a stale answer that is still within `maxAge` immediately after a sequencer outage/restart, before fresh rounds are posted. UniswapV3Oracle omits this gate entirely: there is no sequencerUptimeFeed, no grace-period check. During the sequencer-recovery window an attacker who can transact (e.g. via the delayed-inbox / force-inclusion path) can act on a quote-token USD price that is stale-but-not-rejected, mispricing every asset whose pool is quoted in a non-USD token.

OUTCOME: USD valuations from this oracle are unguarded against the exact L2 failure mode the sibling oracle was hardened against. Impact is bounded (the TWAP term is manipulation-resistant and `maxAge` still applies), hence medium, but it is an inconsistent and exploitable gap on the protocol's stated deployment target. Fix: add the same sequencer-uptime + grace-period gate to `_getPriceData`.

finding.title: [access-control] Facet registry is a 2nd upgrade path gated below UUPS upgrader → ADMIN_ROLE priv-esc
finding.description:
SETUP: The router dispatches any registered selector by `delegatecall` into the facet address stored in `_facetForSelector` (FacetRouterBase.sol:66-75, executed for every unknown selector via the `fallback`). Whoever can write that mapping can install arbitrary code that runs in the proxy's storage/`msg.sender` context — functionally identical to a UUPS implementation swap (it can grant roles, move all staked funds, rewrite any slot). Registry writes go through `registerFacet`/`registerFacetSelectors`/`clearFacetSelectors`, each gated only by `_authorizeFacetRegistryChange()` (FacetRouterBase.sol:16-36). `_setFacetSelectors` (FacetRouterBase.sol:49-61) only requires the target have `code.length != 0`; it need not be a vetted facet — any attacker-deployed contract qualifies.

ATTACK: On `MultiAssetDelegation`, `_authorizeFacetRegistryChange()` is `onlyRole(ADMIN_ROLE)` (line 81) while the actual UUPS upgrade gate `_authorizeUpgrade()` is `onlyRole(UPGRADER_ROLE)` (line 117). These are distinct roles, and the protocol's own deployment guidance (Base.sol:164-178, H-5) tells operators to split privileged roles and route upgrades through a governance timelock. In any deployment that does so — UPGRADER_ROLE behind the timelock, ADMIN_ROLE on a faster operational key (a routine separation, since ADMIN_ROLE governs day-to-day setters like commission/delays/assets) — a holder of ADMIN_ROLE who does NOT hold UPGRADER_ROLE can call `registerFacetSelectors(maliciousContract, [withdraw/slash/etc selectors])` and immediately delegatecall arbitrary logic into proxy storage.

OUTCOME: ADMIN_ROLE escalates to full upgrade-equivalent control (drain every deposit, mint roles, zero out slash accounting), bypassing the UPGRADER_ROLE/timelock that is the protocol's intended last line of defense. The registry must be authorized by the SAME role as `_authorizeUpgrade` (UPGRADER_ROLE) and ideally the same timelock. Note Tangle.sol:16 correctly uses UPGRADER_ROLE for both gates; only the staking router is mismatched.

finding.title: [oracle] UniswapV3Oracle never enforces observation cardinality → TWAP collapses toward manipulable spot
finding.description:
SETUP: `configurePool` (lines 208-235) reads `token0/token1` from the pool but never inspects `observationCardinality`/`observationCardinalityNext` (the `slot0` fields are declared in the interface but unused), and the oracle never calls `increaseObservationCardinalityNext`. `_getArithmeticMeanTick` blindly calls `observe([twapPeriod, 0])`.

ATTACK: For a pool whose observation buffer does not span the full `twapPeriod` (default 30 min; freshly created or thin pools commonly have cardinality 1), Uniswap's `observe()` extrapolates the older endpoint from the *current* tick. The "time-weighted" average therefore degenerates toward the instantaneous spot tick, which a single-block swap can move. The SECURITY CONSIDERATIONS doc comment (lines 42-56) explicitly states the pool "should have at least (twapPeriod/12s) observations initialized" — but nothing in code enforces it, so it is purely operational and silently violated for any low-cardinality pool. An attacker swaps to dislocate the pool, reads a manipulated price through `toUSD`/`getPrice`, drives a favorable slashing-weight (`Slashing.sol:214`) or exposure (`PaymentsEffectiveExposure.sol:94`) computation in the same transaction window, then unwinds.

OUTCOME: Manipulable valuation despite the "TWAP" framing. Magnitude scales with how thin the configured pool is; combined with the price-math bug (separate finding) the oracle is unsafe for any high-value path. Remediation: require `observationCardinality >= twapPeriod/blockTime` at config time (revert otherwise) and/or call `increaseObservationCardinalityNext`.

finding.title: [access-control] StakingAdminFacet.setTangle grants TANGLE_ROLE without revoking prior holder
finding.description:
SETUP: `setTangle(address tangle)` (onlyRole ADMIN_ROLE) wires the staking module to the Tangle core by `_grantRole(TANGLE_ROLE, tangle)` and overwriting `_tangleCore = tangle`. TANGLE_ROLE gates `StakingOperatorsFacet.addBlueprintForOperator` and `removeBlueprintForOperator` (the only TANGLE_ROLE-gated entrypoints in the staking router), which mutate `_operatorBlueprints[operator]` — the set used by `slashForBlueprint` to scope which delegators are exposed and by reward attribution.

ATTACK: When governance rotates the Tangle core (deploy a new core, call `setTangle(newCore)`), the function grants TANGLE_ROLE to the new core but NEVER revokes it from the previous one — there is no `_revokeRole` path and no record of the prior holder. The stale/decommissioned core (or anyone who compromises it) retains TANGLE_ROLE indefinitely and can still call `removeBlueprintForOperator(operator, blueprintId)` to dissociate any active operator from a blueprint, or `addBlueprintForOperator` to attach arbitrary blueprints. Removing a blueprint association can drop an operator out of a blueprint's slashing/exposure set, undermining slash attribution and service bookkeeping.

OUTCOME: Permanent privilege leakage across core upgrades; a retired or compromised core address keeps write access to operator-blueprint state. Contrast `MasterBlueprintServiceManager.setTangle(address tangle, bool allowed)` (src/MasterBlueprintServiceManager.sol:47-52), which correctly toggles grant/revoke — the secure pattern exists in the same codebase but is absent here. Severity is low because triggering requires the ADMIN_ROLE-driven rotation and impact is bounded to blueprint-association integrity, but it is a concrete role-custody defect, not a malicious-admin scenario.

remediation.summary: Make setTangle revoke TANGLE_ROLE from the previous _tangleCore before granting to the new one, or follow the MBSM pattern: setTangle(address tangle, bool allowed) that grants or revokes explicitly.

finding.title: [business-logic] Validator exit misread as beacon slash in ValidatorPod → real L2 stake slash
finding.description:
SETUP: `beaconChainSlashingFactor` is meant to track ONLY proportional loss from beacon-chain slashing. `_finalizeCheckpoint` instead derives it from any drop in `totalRestakedBalanceGwei` vs the checkpoint's `priorBeaconBalanceGwei` (line 455: `if (currentBalance < priorBalance)` → newFactor = oldFactor*currentBalance/priorBalance, lines 459-463). `totalRestakedBalanceGwei` is decremented in `_verifyAndProcessCheckpointProof` (lines 419-421) for EVERY beacon-balance decrease, and a full exit sets currentBalance==0 (lines 424-428), subtracting the validator's entire ~32 ETH.

ATTACK / TRIGGER: An operator's validator exits the beacon chain *normally* (no slashing). Its 32 ETH lands in the pod and is correctly re-credited to totalAssets via `newlyWithdrawableGwei` (line 440). But `verifyCheckpointProofs` is permissionless (comment line 337). Anyone starts a checkpoint and submits the post-exit balance proof (balance=0). At finalize, currentBalance = priorBalance − 32e9 < priorBalance, so `beaconChainSlashingFactor` is cut proportionally (e.g. single-validator pod → factor ≈ 0). 

OUTCOME: `L2SlashingConnector.propagateBeaconSlashing` (it re-reads the pod's real factor at line 252, so the drop is genuine) converts the factor delta into `slashBps` and bridges a slash to `L2SlashingReceiver`, which slashes `operatorDelegatedStake * slashPercentage`. A lawful validator exit (or any balance dip) thus burns the operator's L2 delegated stake. The factor is monotonic (never restored on the principal reappearing in the EL), so the loss is permanent. This is both an honest-loss correctness bug and a griefing vector (third party finalizes the checkpoint to weaponize any exit). EigenLayer avoids this by attributing the slashing factor only to the validator `slashed` flag / loss not explained by exited principal, not to raw balance deltas.

finding.title: [business-logic] UniswapV3Oracle inverts decimal scaling and truncates price → grossly wrong USD valuation
finding.description:
SETUP: `_getPriceFromSqrtX96` converts the TWAP sqrtPriceX96 to a quote-denominated price. Both branches contain two compounding errors.

BUG 1 — premature integer truncation. isToken0 branch (line 404): `price = (sqrtPriceX96 * sqrtPriceX96) >> 192;` computes the RAW token1/token0 ratio and floors it to an integer BEFORE any decimal scaling. For the ubiquitous case where one raw unit of the priced token is worth <1 raw unit of quote (e.g. WETH 18-dec priced in USDC 6-dec: raw ratio ≈ 3000e6/1e18 = 3e-9), this floors to 0, so the returned price is 0. The token1 branch (lines 410-412, `(1<<192)/sqrtSquared`) truncates identically.

BUG 2 — inverted decimal adjustment. For isToken0, human price(quote per token) = price_raw × 10^(tokenDecimals−quoteDecimals), i.e. `* 10**tokenDecimals / 10**quoteDecimals`. The code (line 406) does the inverse: `price * (10**quoteDecimals) / (10**tokenDecimals)`. The token1 branch (line 413) is inverted the same way. So even when truncation does not zero the value, the decimal factor is off by 10^(2·|tokenDec−quoteDec|) (e.g. 10^24 for an 18/6 pair).

ATTACK / OUTCOME: An owner configures a legitimate, liquid pool via `configurePool`. `getPrice`/`toUSD` then return 0 or values mis-scaled by many orders of magnitude. This oracle's `toUSD` feeds `Slashing.sol:214` (slash weight per token) and `PaymentsEffectiveExposure.sol:94` (operator effective exposure). A token mispriced toward 0 makes an operator's exposure/slash-weight read as ~0, letting a malicious operator escape slashing or skew fee/exposure routing; an over-scaled price inflates weight to grief honest operators. The mispricing is deterministic from token decimals, not attacker-timed, so it silently corrupts every USD-normalized economic decision that routes through this oracle.

finding.title: [solvency] StandardAssetAdapter.deposit ignores actual-received → fee/deflationary token bricks pool
finding.description: StandardAssetAdapter.deposit sets shares=assets (1:1) and does safeTransferFrom(from,this,assets) WITHOUT measuring the balance delta. For any fee-on-transfer / deflationary token (USDT fee-switch, PAXG 0.02%, STA, etc.) the adapter receives <assets but credits `assets` shares and returns `assets` to DepositManager. DepositManager._handleErc20Deposit (src/staking/DepositManager.sol:74-76) trusts this return verbatim and credits config.currentDeposits + dep.amount by the full `assets` — UNLIKE both the direct-deposit path (DepositManager.sol:85-90, which explicitly reverts FeeOnTransferTokenRejected) and RebasingAssetAdapter.deposit (RebasingAssetAdapter.sol:116-122, which measures actualReceived). The adapter pool therefore becomes systematically under-collateralized: SETUP: token has 1% fee; ATTACK/normal use: Alice deposits 100 (pool gets 99, shares=100), Bob deposits 100 (pool gets 198, totalShares=200). OUTCOME: Alice withdraws 100 shares→transfer 100 (pool→98), Bob withdraws 100 shares→safeTransfer(100) reverts (only 98 held). Last withdrawer(s) permanently DoS-locked; the 1:1 shares==assets invariant the adapter documents (line 11) is violated. The StandardAdapter is the *designated* mechanism for onboarding arbitrary standard ERC-20s yet is the only deposit path with no fee-on-transfer guard.

reproduction.notes: PASS: 200 shares credited vs 198 tokens held; Alice withdraws 100, leaving 98; Bob's 100-share withdraw reverts (insolvent pool, permanent DoS).

finding.title: [access-control] Adapter owner can repoint delegationManager and drain entire shared pool
finding.description: Both StandardAssetAdapter.setDelegationManager (StandardAssetAdapter.sol:69-73) and RebasingAssetAdapter.setDelegationManager (RebasingAssetAdapter.sol:89-93) are plain onlyOwner setters with NO timelock, NO two-step, and NO guard against live balances, and are re-callable at any time. withdraw(to,shares) is gated only by onlyDelegationManager and sends assets to an arbitrary `to`. SETUP: adapters custody 100% of every delegator restaked balance for their token (the diamond holds only share bookkeeping). ATTACK: owner -- a single Ownable key (adapters use Ownable, not even the Ownable2Step the factory uses) -- calls setDelegationManager(EVIL); EVIL calls withdraw(EVIL, totalShares). OUTCOME: the full pool token balance is transferred to EVIL in one tx, orphaning all delegators diamond-side accounting. Instant single-key rug over all pooled stake with no on-chain delay or co-signer. Recommend timelock + 2-step manager rotation, or making delegationManager immutable after init.

finding.title: [loss-of-precision] RebasingAssetAdapter mints ~1e8x-scaled shares -> rebasing-asset exposure inflated 1e8x in USD payout weighting
finding.description: On a fresh pool (totalShares==0) RebasingAssetAdapter.deposit computes shares = actualReceived * (0 + VIRTUAL_SHARES) / (0 + VIRTUAL_ASSETS) = actualReceived * 1e8 (VIRTUAL_SHARES=1e8, VIRTUAL_ASSETS=1; lines 45-46,130-131). So a rebasing-asset position is recorded ~1e8x the underlying TOKEN wei, whereas StandardAssetAdapter and direct/native deposits are 1:1 (token wei). These adapter-shares become dep.amount and flow unchanged into the operator stake aggregate (_operatorDelegatedAggregate, DelegationStorage.sol:322-329) returned by getOperatorStakeForAsset. PaymentsEffectiveExposure._calculateEffectiveExposures (src/core/PaymentsEffectiveExposure.sol:82-94) then does exposedAmount = delegation*exposureBps/BPS and feeds it to oracle.toUSD(token, exposedAmount) -- but toUSD expects raw TOKEN wei, so a rebasing-adapter asset is valued at ~1e8x its true USD exposure. OUTCOME: an operator who commits a rebasing-adapter asset captures a grossly disproportionate (~1e8x) slice of service-fee distribution and skews the global payout-weight pool against honest co-operators; the same scale error mis-prices slashing exposure and admin-set depositCap/minDelegation (which are token-denominated but compared against share-denominated currentDeposits). The exact 1e8 multiple holds while the pool is near its bootstrap ratio. Root cause: the adapter emits shares in a unit (1e8x) the cross-asset USD/exposure layer assumes is token-denominated. Preconditions: rebasing adapter deployed for a committable asset and price-oracle USD normalization enabled.

reproduction.notes: PASS: first deposit of 1e18 wei mints 1e26 shares (= tokenWei * 1e8). Confirms the 1e8x scale that the token-denominated oracle USD-exposure path mis-values. Full end-to-end fee-distribution skew not wired in this unit PoC.

finding.title: [accounting] RebasingAssetAdapter.assetsToShares bootstrap returns 1e18x while deposit mints 1e8x (1e10 mismatch)
finding.description: When totalShares==0, assetsToShares returns assets * INITIAL_SHARES_PER_ASSET = assets * 1e18 (lines 184-185), and previewDeposit (line 194) forwards it. But the actual deposit path mints assets * VIRTUAL_SHARES/VIRTUAL_ASSETS = assets * 1e8 (lines 130-131). previewDeposit therefore overstates first-deposit shares by 1e10x, and assetsToShares disagrees with deposit on the bootstrap. These views are not used in value-moving paths today (only sharesToAssets is, via DepositManager._sharesToAssets), so impact is limited to integrators/keepers/UIs that size operations off previewDeposit/assetsToShares, which would be off by 1e10x. Fix: bootstrap should mirror the deposit formula (use VIRTUAL_SHARES/VIRTUAL_ASSETS), not INITIAL_SHARES_PER_ASSET.

reproduction.notes: PASS: previewDeposit(1e18) returns 1e36 while deposit would mint 1e26 -> 1e10x overstatement.

finding.title: [info] Adapter deposit/withdraw expose no slippage/min-out; declared SlippageTooHigh error is dead code
finding.description: RebasingAssetAdapter declares error SlippageTooHigh (line 54) but never uses it; neither deposit nor withdraw accepts a minSharesOut/minAssetsOut parameter. Because the adapter pool is shared and rebasing, the share price observed by a delegator at call time is not bounded by any caller-supplied tolerance. Impact is limited (shares are internal proportional claims, not an AMM swap, and withdrawals are subject to leaveDelegatorsDelay), so this is informational, but the dead error signals an intended-but-missing protection worth either implementing or removing.

finding.title: Pending-slash guard missing on blueprint rebalance lets Fixed-mode delegators evade targeted slashes
finding.description: The dispute-window protection (_operatorPendingSlashCount>0) is enforced on EVERY stake-exit path: _scheduleDelegatorUnstake (DelegationManagerLib.sol:424-427), _executeDelegatorUnstake (:469), _executeDelegatorUnstakeAndWithdrawInner (StakingDelegationsFacet.sol:147), _scheduleOperatorUnstake (OperatorManager.sol:157), _executeOperatorUnstake (:191), _startLeaving (:237). Intent: while a slash is pending against an operator, no delegator may move stake out of harm. BUT _addBlueprintToDelegation (DelegationManagerLib.sol:847) and _removeBlueprintFromDelegation (:911) -- exposed via StakingDelegationsFacet.addBlueprintToDelegation/removeBlueprintFromDelegation, gated only by whenNotPaused -- move a Fixed-mode delegator's assets BETWEEN blueprint pools with NO pending-slash check. _removeBlueprintFromDelegation pulls the delegator's full value out of blueprint X's pool via _setDelegatorBlueprintPosition(...,X,0,0) at :951 (which decrements _blueprintPools[op][X].totalAssets) and redistributes it into the remaining blueprint pools. ATTACK: (1) delegator is Fixed-mode on blueprint X; (2) SLASHER calls incrementPendingSlash(operator), opening the dispute window for a slash on X; (3) delegator front-runs execution: addBlueprintToDelegation(idx,Y) then removeBlueprintFromDelegation(idx,X), moving all value from pool X into pool Y; (4) slashForBlueprint/slashForService executes and reduces _blueprintPools[op][X].totalAssets, but the attacker is no longer in pool X and escapes entirely while honest pool-X delegators absorb a proportionally larger loss. OUTCOME: dispute-window lock defeated for Fixed-mode delegations; slash evasion with loss socialized onto honest delegators.

finding.title: [unit-mismatch] LiquidDelegationVault.deposit assumes adapter shares==assets → DoS / stranded funds for rebasing assets
finding.description:
SETUP: An asset (e.g. stETH) is configured in MultiAssetDelegation with a RebasingAssetAdapter (AssetAdapterFactory.deployRebasingAdapter), and a LiquidDelegationVault is created for it (LiquidDelegationFactory.createVault accepts ANY asset, no adapter-type check). The vault's deposit()/mint() treat the underlying staking "amount" unit as identical to the token amount.

ATTACK / BUG: In deposit() the vault does:
  staking.depositERC20(asset, assets);                       // line 211
  staking.delegateWithOptions(operator, asset, assets, ...); // line 214
But DepositManager._handleErc20Deposit (DepositManager.sol:74-76) routes through the adapter and credits dep.amount += adapterShares = RebasingAssetAdapter.deposit() return = assets*(totalShares+1e8)/(balanceBefore+1) (RebasingAssetAdapter.sol:130-131), NOT `assets`. delegateWithOptions then tries to delegate the raw `assets`, but available = dep.amount - delegatedAmount = adapterShares.
  • After ANY positive rebase, balanceBefore > totalShares ⇒ adapterShares < assets ⇒ available < assets ⇒ delegateWithOptions reverts with InsufficientDeposit → vault deposit() is permanently bricked (DoS) for that asset.
  • On the bootstrap deposit (totalShares==0,balanceBefore==0) adapterShares = assets*1e8 ≫ assets; delegate succeeds for `assets` and (assets*1e8 - assets) adapter-shares are left as undelegated deposit balance the vault has NO code path to withdraw (vault never calls scheduleWithdraw) → permanently stranded.

OUTCOME: For any rebasing (or fee-on-transfer / non-1:1-adapter) asset — a configuration the protocol explicitly supports — the vault either reverts on deposit (DoS) or strands depositor funds. getDelegation (totalAssets) is also denominated in adapter-share units, further desyncing convertToShares pricing from token value. The standard 1:1 adapter path is unaffected. Fix: have deposit()/mint() delegate the share amount actually returned by depositERC20, not the raw token `assets`.

finding.title: [VAULT] totalAssets() floor-to-zero after slash during pending redeem → share-inflation mint drains vault
finding.description:
SETUP: `totalAssets()` (152-159) returns `getDelegation(vault,operator) - _pendingRedeemAssets`, floored to 0 when reserved >= underlying. `convertToShares` (165-168) = `assets * (totalSupply()+1e3) / (totalAssets()+1e3)`. When `totalAssets()==0` while `totalSupply()>0`, the denominator collapses to the 1e3 virtual offset, so a tiny deposit mints astronomically many shares.

Reachability: `requestRedeem` adds `_pendingRedeemAssets += convertToAssets(shares)` and burns the liquid shares, but does NOT reduce the underlying delegation. Pure requests cannot push reserved above underlying (virtual offset keeps a margin). A SLASH does: SlashingManager reduces `pool.totalAssets` (SlashingManager.sol:490), shrinking `_sharesToAmount(vaultShares)=getDelegation(vault)`. With a redeem pending, `_pendingRedeemAssets` was priced pre-slash, so post-slash `getDelegation(vault) < _pendingRedeemAssets` → `totalAssets()` floors to 0.

ATTACK: (1) Holders file requestRedeem, reserving ~all underlying (totalSupply drops to S', reserved=P). (2) Operator is slashed (natural, or attacker delegates to / operates a misbehaving operator). getDelegation drops below P → totalAssets()=0. (3) Attacker calls `deposit(assets)` for a tiny `assets`; mints `assets*(S'+1e3)/1e3` shares — dominant majority of supply. (4) When pending redeems are claimed, reservation P releases and totalAssets recovers; attacker's inflated share fraction now owns nearly the entire vault.

OUTCOME: Attacker steals the non-redeeming holders' stake (and all future deposits/rewards) for the cost of a dust deposit. The floor-to-0 comment (150-151) handles underflow but ignores the share-price collapse it creates.

finding.title: [business-logic] Exiting redeemer keeps rewards on reserved position during delay; totalAssets overstated to remaining holders
finding.description:
SETUP: requestRedeem (line 304) reserves reservedAssets = convertToAssets(shares) valued at request-time rate and stores it in _pendingRedeemAssets; totalAssets() returns underlying - _pendingRedeemAssets (line 152-159). The redeem (line 400-402) executes staking.executeDelegatorUnstakeAndWithdraw(unstakeShares) at CLAIM-time rate and releases exactly the OLD reservedAssets.

ATTACK/BUG: The reserved underlying pool shares (unstakeShares) continue accruing staking rewards during the bond-less delay window (the schedule only queues; shares are not reduced until claim). reservedAssets is frozen at the request-time value. Therefore:
  • totalAssets() = underlying - reservedAssets_old overstates remaining holders' true backing by the rewards accrued on the reserved position (the reserved position is worth reservedAssets_old + rewards, but only reservedAssets_old is excluded). During the window, convertToShares/convertToAssets price shares too high.
  • At claim, amountReturned = sharesToAmount(unstakeShares) at the higher rate ≈ reservedAssets_old + rewards is sent to the redeemer, while only reservedAssets_old is released from _pendingRedeemAssets. Net Δ(totalAssets for remaining holders) = -(reservedAssets_old + rewards) + reservedAssets_old = -rewards.

OUTCOME: Rewards earned on a position that has already burned its vault shares are siphoned to the exiting redeemer; remaining holders' reported per-share rate diverges from real backing and drops by `rewards` at claim. A second redeemer who files during the window also benefits from the inflated rate. Magnitude scales with rewards over the delay window (bounded, hence low). Additionally, the ceil rounding of unstakeShares in previewDelegatorUnstakeShares (StakingViewsFacet/_previewDelegatorUnstakeShares:381,392) lets each claim withdraw up to ~1 asset-unit more than reservedAssets released, a dust leak onto remaining holders.

finding.title: [VAULT] Exiting redeemer over-claims rewards accrued during unbonding; reservedAssets vs actual-returned mismatch dilutes remaining holders
finding.description:
SETUP: At `requestRedeem` the vault prices the exit once: `assets = convertToAssets(shares)` (287), burns the liquid shares (295), stores `reservedAssets = assets` and `unstakeShares = previewDelegatorUnstakeShares(assets)` (308-314), and reserves `_pendingRedeemAssets += assets` (304). Crucially `scheduleDelegatorUnstake` only QUEUES — it does NOT reduce the vault's delegation shares `d.shares`. So during the bond-less delay those `unstakeShares` keep earning pool rewards and keep counting in `getDelegation(vault)`.

ATTACK: A redeemer files a request, then waits for the operator's pool to accrue rewards over the unbonding window (rewards raise `pool.totalAssets`, hence the pool exchange rate). At `redeem` (400-402) the vault calls `executeDelegatorUnstakeAndWithdraw(unstakeShares, ...)`, which returns `_sharesToAmount(unstakeShares)` at the CURRENT (higher) rate = more than `reservedAssets`, sent straight to the redeemer's receiver. But the vault only releases `reservedAssets` (the stale, request-time value) from `_pendingRedeemAssets` (393-398).

OUTCOME: The underlying delegation shrinks by `unstakeShares*rate_now` while the reservation released is only the smaller `reservedAssets`, so remaining holders' `totalAssets() = getDelegation - reserved` drops by the reward delta. The redeemer pockets rewards on already-burned liquid shares — value siphoned from the holders who stayed. Magnitude = reward accrual over the unbonding delay; repeatable each request. No reconciliation between reserved and actually-withdrawn assets exists.

finding.title: [business-logic] LiquidDelegationFactory.createVault never validates asset is enabled/adapter-compatible; order-sensitive vault key
finding.description:
SETUP: createVault is permissionless (no onlyOwner) and only checks staking.isOperatorActive(operator). The AssetNotEnabled error is declared (line 57) but NEVER used — the factory does not verify the asset is enabled in staking, nor that its adapter (if any) is 1:1-compatible with the vault's amount==assets assumption.

BUG/OUTCOME:
  • A vault can be created for a disabled or non-existent asset; deposits then revert at staking.depositERC20 (AssetNotEnabled), but the vault is registered and indexed, and the (operator,asset,blueprintIds) key is now permanently occupied (VaultAlreadyExists) — a griefer can pre-squat the canonical vault address for any (operator,asset,blueprint) tuple before the legitimate deployment.
  • For assets configured with a rebasing/non-1:1 adapter, createVault happily produces a vault that is broken per the unit-mismatch finding (sub-4-deep-audit-e6c24032-a8e). No guard prevents it.
  • computeVaultKey = keccak256(abi.encode(operator, asset, blueprintIds)) (line 132) is order-sensitive and not deduped, so [1,2] and [2,1] yield two distinct "duplicate" vaults for the same logical Fixed-mode selection, fragmenting liquidity and confusing the canonical-vault lookup.

These are correctness/operational hardening gaps (low severity, no direct fund theft) but enable address-squatting griefing and silent creation of unusable/broken vaults. Fix: gate creation on asset-enabled + adapter-compatibility, and sort/dedupe blueprintIds before hashing.

finding.title: [VAULT] Vault approves staking (not the adapter) → deposits revert / mis-account for adapter-backed assets; permissionless factory does no asset validation
finding.description:
SETUP: `deposit()` does `asset.forceApprove(address(staking), assets)` then `staking.depositERC20(asset, assets)` (200-211). Inside the staking layer, `_handleErc20Deposit` routes through a registered adapter when one exists: `IAssetAdapter(adapter).deposit(msg.sender=vault, amount)` (DepositManager.sol:76), and the adapter does `safeTransferFrom(vault, adapter, assets)` (e.g. StandardAssetAdapter.sol:88, RebasingAssetAdapter.sol:119) — pulling from the ADAPTER's allowance, which the vault never granted (it approved `staking`). 

ATTACK / OUTCOME (two regimes):
(1) If an adapter is registered for the vault's asset (now or later by governance), every `deposit()`/`mint()` reverts on the adapter transferFrom — all live vaults for that asset are silently bricked (DoS), with no migration path since the approval target is hard-coded.
(2) Adapters also return shares in NON-1:1 units for rebasing tokens (RebasingAssetAdapter mints `assets*1e8`-scale shares, line 130-131). `depositERC20` credits `dep.amount += adapterShares` while the vault then calls `delegateWithOptions(operator, asset, assets)` with the RAW token amount (214) and `scheduleDelegatorUnstake(asset, assets)` at redeem — a units mismatch between the vault's liquid-share accounting (raw amount) and the staking deposit credit (adapter shares).

Aggravating: `LiquidDelegationFactory.createVault` (76-110) is permissionless and performs NO asset-enabled / adapter check — the declared `AssetNotEnabled` error (57) is never used — so vaults are freely creatable for adapter-backed or non-enabled assets, and the deterministic `vaultKey` lets anyone front-run/squat the canonical vault for any (operator, asset, blueprints) tuple.

finding.title: [business-logic] RebasingAssetAdapter previewDeposit/assetsToShares bootstrap factor (1e18) disagrees with deposit (1e8) by 1e10x
finding.description:
SETUP: When totalShares==0, assetsToShares() (and thus previewDeposit(), line 193-195) returns `assets * INITIAL_SHARES_PER_ASSET` = assets * 1e18 (line 184-185). But the actual deposit() path for the same bootstrap state computes shares = actualReceived*(totalShares+VIRTUAL_SHARES)/(balanceBefore+VIRTUAL_ASSETS) = assets*(0+1e8)/(balanceBefore+1) ≈ assets*1e8 (line 130-131).

BUG: previewDeposit overstates the minted shares by a factor of ~1e10 for the first deposit (and assetsToShares uses a different formula entirely from the live pool ratio). ERC4626/adapter consumers expect preview* to match the executed result. DepositManager._sharesToAssets uses adapter.sharesToAssets on the withdraw side (DepositManager.sol:306-308), and any integrator/quote that sizes deposits via previewDeposit on a fresh pool will mis-size by 10 orders of magnitude.

OUTCOME: Incorrect off-chain/on-chain share quoting on adapter bootstrap; an integrator relying on previewDeposit to pre-compute shares could under/over-provision drastically. No direct theft (the real deposit recomputes), so low severity, but it is a latent accounting-API inconsistency that should be reconciled (preview must mirror the virtual-offset formula used in deposit()).

finding.title: enableAsset overwrites AssetConfig and resets currentDeposits to 0, bricking all withdrawals of a live asset
finding.description: enableAsset (StakingAssetsFacet.sol:40) and enableAssetWithAdapter (:142) assign a brand-new Types.AssetConfig struct to _assetConfigs[assetHash] with currentDeposits hard-coded to 0. There is NO updateAssetConfig / setMinDelegation / setDepositCap / setRewardMultiplier function (see selectors() at :24-37) -- enableAsset is the ONLY way to change an existing asset's parameters (minDelegation, depositCap, rewardMultiplierBps) after launch. Re-calling it on an asset that already has live deposits silently resets currentDeposits from its true value to 0, even though sibling functions registerAdapter (:101-102) and removeAdapter (:117-118) explicitly REVERT when currentDeposits != 0, proving the team treats currentDeposits as a load-bearing accounting invariant. IMPACT: DepositManager._executeWithdraw (DepositManager.sol:225) and StakingDelegationsFacet._executeDelegatorUnstakeAndWithdrawInner (:206) both do `_assetConfigs[assetHash].currentDeposits -= amount` with Solidity 0.8 checked arithmetic. After the reset currentDeposits==0, so 0 - amount underflows and REVERTS for every holder of that asset -- all withdrawals and combined unstake-withdraws for that ERC20 are permanently bricked until a contract upgrade. ATTACK PATH: a routine, well-intentioned ASSET_MANAGER_ROLE parameter update (e.g. bumping depositCap or reward multiplier on an already-funded asset) is sufficient to irreversibly lock every depositor's funds for that token. Recommend a dedicated update path that preserves currentDeposits, or guard enableAsset against re-configuring an asset whose currentDeposits != 0.

finding.title: [VAULT] RebasingAssetAdapter preview/actual share inconsistency (assetsToShares=assets*1e18 vs deposit mints assets*1e8 on empty pool)
finding.description:
SETUP: On an empty pool (`totalShares==0`), `deposit()` mints `actualReceived * (0 + VIRTUAL_SHARES) / (0 + VIRTUAL_ASSETS)` = `actualReceived * 1e8` shares (130-131, VIRTUAL_SHARES=1e8, VIRTUAL_ASSETS=1). But the preview path `assetsToShares` (183-190) returns `assets * INITIAL_SHARES_PER_ASSET` = `assets * 1e18` when `totalShares==0`, and `previewDeposit` (193-195) forwards that. 

OUTCOME: `previewDeposit` over-reports the first-deposit share count by 1e10× versus what `deposit` actually mints. Any integrator (including a vault) that sizes minted-share expectations or slippage bounds off `previewDeposit`/`assetsToShares` for the bootstrap deposit will be wildly wrong. Similarly `sharesToAssets`/`previewWithdraw` (175-200) use the raw `currentBalance/totalShares` ratio with NO virtual offset, diverging from the offset-based math used in `withdraw` (151). View functions are not self-consistent with the state-changing functions. Not directly profitable, but breaks any on-chain consumer relying on the previews for accounting or invariant checks.

finding.title: LiquidDelegationVault.deposit assumes adapter shares==assets -> DoS / stranded funds for rebasing assets
finding.description: deposit()/mint() call staking.depositERC20(asset,assets) then staking.delegateWithOptions(operator,asset,assets,...). With a RebasingAssetAdapter registered, DepositManager._handleErc20Deposit credits dep.amount += adapterShares (= assets*(totalShares+1e8)/(balanceBefore+1)), not assets. delegateWithOptions then delegates raw `assets` > available (after any positive rebase) -> InsufficientDeposit revert (deposit DoS); on bootstrap adapterShares=assets*1e8, leaving assets*1e8-assets stranded with no vault withdraw path. Standard 1:1 adapter unaffected. Fix: delegate the share amount returned by depositERC20.

finding.title: Exiting redeemer keeps rewards on reserved position during delay; totalAssets overstated to remaining holders
finding.description: requestRedeem freezes reservedAssets at request-time rate in _pendingRedeemAssets; totalAssets()=underlying-_pendingRedeemAssets. Reserved pool shares keep accruing rewards during bond-less delay. At claim, executeDelegatorUnstakeAndWithdraw pays sharesToAmount(unstakeShares) at higher rate (reservedAssets_old+rewards) but releases only reservedAssets_old -> remaining holders lose `rewards`; totalAssets overstated during window. Plus ceil-rounded unstakeShares leaks ~1 unit/claim. Bounded -> low.

finding.title: LiquidDelegationFactory.createVault never validates asset enabled/adapter-compatible; order-sensitive vault key enables squatting
finding.description: Permissionless createVault only checks isOperatorActive; AssetNotEnabled error declared but unused. Allows registering vaults for disabled assets or rebasing-adapter assets (broken per F-VAULT-1), and permanently squatting the canonical (operator,asset,blueprintIds) key (VaultAlreadyExists). computeVaultKey hashes unsorted/undeduped blueprintIds so [1,2] vs [2,1] create duplicate vaults. Fix: validate asset enabled + adapter compatibility; sort/dedupe blueprintIds.

finding.title: RebasingAssetAdapter previewDeposit/assetsToShares bootstrap factor (1e18) disagrees with deposit (1e8) by 1e10x
finding.description: When totalShares==0, assetsToShares/previewDeposit returns assets*INITIAL_SHARES_PER_ASSET (1e18) but deposit() mints assets*(0+1e8)/(balanceBefore+1)~assets*1e8. Preview overstates first-deposit shares by ~1e10x and uses a different formula than the live virtual-offset pool ratio, violating preview==execute. No direct theft (deposit recomputes) -> low, but a latent accounting-API inconsistency.

finding.title: Vault totalAssets() floor-to-zero after slash during pending redeem enables share-inflation mint that drains remaining holders
